title: Penn State baseball suffers crushing loss to Bucknell
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
After its win against St. Bonaventure last night in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park, Penn State returned in search of another chance to improve its record against the Bucknell Bisons.

The Nittany Lions fell to the Bisons, with Bucknell dominating all game to finish 9-0 against the blue and white.

The Bisons came out swinging, as senior Michael Trommer started off the game with a two-run home run, putting the team at an early lead. Penn State remained off the board, getting three outs before Cohl Mercado and Michael Anderson could run home.

Penn State responded with a strong outfield performance to keep Bucknell at two, with all hitters grounding out before reaching first base. The Nittany Lions fell short on their offensive performance, leaving the second inning off the board.

Penn State continued to struggle through the third, getting slammed on both defense and offense. The Bisons quickly loaded up the bases with only one out in the top of the third, before pitcher Ethan Bauerschmidt was relieved by Mason Butash. Despite his fast throws, it wasn’t enough as Bucknell scored three more runs.

On the offense, Nittany Lion Spencer Barnett had a power hit that was sure to be a homerun, but fell just short, hitting the wall into right fielder Chris Morabito’s glove, signaling another inning with no runs.

“We’ve had some tough stretches here, where guys have been hurt, guys have been sick, and guys not feeling well,” coach Mike Gambino said. “The guys competed tonight for the standard that we want here; it was not good enough.”

Butash redeemed himself in the top of the fourth, striking out all Bucknell hitters, giving Penn State a chance at a much-needed comeback. It looked promising with a single to third by Jayden Davis and a walk from Maddox McDonald, leaving both on base with only one out on the board. Only to be met by a strikeout and a disappointing double out off Preston Yaucher’s groundball.

Looking for a pitching change in the fifth, sophomore Harrison Lollin went in to keep Bucknell at five runs. While the Bisons put another on the board, Lollin got the job done under pressure, pitching a strikeout with the bases loaded and only one out, and first baseman Barnett finishing it off, catching a pop-up ball to get Penn State out of a nightmare setup.

However, once again the blue and white failed to make a statement in the bottom of the fifth. Even with the bases loaded and only one out, Penn State couldn’t get its batting together with both Bryce Molinaro and Davis getting struck out by Bucknell pitcher Dante Pavone, leaving morale low in the bullpen.

“I thought our effort, our attention, and our compete and our fight were not who we want to be,” Gambino said. “Whether or not we won the game or lost the game is irrelevant. We can’t be okay with that.”

After a quiet sixth inning on both sides, Bucknell kept their dominance over Penn State as sophomore John Calabrese singled into center field, allowing Zach Selfon to score, moments before Billy Fluharty hit a single into left field that slipped past the blue and white, letting in two more runs, leaving the score 9-0 at the top of the seventh.

Going into the eighth, Bucknell was just short of a mercy-rule victory, with Penn State having the same problem they had all game. Powerful hits by players like Yaucher and McDonald that kept landing wrong and getting caught.

“I thought from all nine, I didn't think we had a ton of quality bats, it was soft, weak contact in the air,” Gambino said. “Just not who we want to be,”

Another pitching switch at the top of the ninth saw senior Dimond Loosli on the mound, marking the seventh pitcher to throw for Penn State over the course of this game.

Bucknell took home the win, putting up nine runs and 12 hits, compared to Penn State’s zero runs and five hits. The Nittany Lions finish this game with a 7-16 record.

“We are talking very much about staying present and staying positive. Whatever we do, win or lose, when we show up to the park the next day, we just move forward,” Gambino said. “Good or bad, it doesn’t matter one day at a time.”

Up Next

Penn State returns home to kick off its series against Illinois this Friday at 5:30 p.m.

title: Penn State men’s volleyball defeats rival No. 15 Ohio State in four sets
author: Chase Mitchell | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State and Ohio State are familiar foes in just about every sport – and the two squads don’t like each other very much.

The Nittany Lions and the Buckeyes met at the Fitzgerald Field House in Pittsburgh for a neutral-site showdown for Penn State’s first match in 11 days. In the end, the blue and white came out on top, winning 3-1.

The Buckeyes’ Shane Wetzel opened up the scoring in the first set, quickly tallying two service aces to give his squad the early lead. Wetzel entered Wednesday’s match with 220 kills – the most of any Buckeye this season.

Both sides traded back-and-forth for the next several points until the Buckeyes went on a 4-0 scoring run that forced Mark Pavlik and the Nittany Lions to call a timeout.

Penn State got back on the board coming out of the timeout, picking up a couple of points off errors and a kill by Owen Rose that brought the score closer at 12-9.

Wetzel notched three more kills, all from Daniel Henwood Rodriguez, who is fifth in the NCAA in assists per set, to extend the Buckeyes’ lead to 17-13

The Nittany Lions started to build momentum, going on a 3-1 scoring run to tighten the gap at 19-17, but the Buckeyes wouldn’t back down and notched another kill to stay ahead of the curve.

Back-to-back blue and white scores brought the first set to within just one point and Ohio State was forced to take a timeout as it watched its lead shrink.

Penn State took its first lead of the night thanks to a Rose service ace that put the Nittany Lions up 21-20.

The two squads made it a battle at the end of the first set after reaching a 24-24 standstill, but the blue and white got the job done with the help of a Sean Harvey kill that captured the first set 27-25 for the Nittany Lions, who fought from behind in the early running.

In the exact same fashion, the Buckeyes took an early 2-0 lead to kick off the second set due to a Michael Schwob service error and another Wetzel kill.

Six-straight kills followed and made it a 5-3 Ohio State advantage, but a 3-1 blue and white scoring run tied the set at 6-6.

Penn State moved ahead thanks to three-straight Harvey kills, but the Buckeyes recaptured their lead, going back out in front 13-10, backed by two more Wetzel kills.

Henwood Rodriguez was all over the place as Ohio State made its advantage four points, but the blue and white were keeping its foot in the door.

Harvey and Penn State outside hitter Matthew Luoma kept the blue and white alive in the latter stages of the second set, but it was all for nothing as a Henwood Rodriguez service ace shut the door on the Nittany Lions as the Buckeyes stole the second set 25-20.

A Wetzel service error put the blue and white on the board first to start the third set, as it quickly went ahead two points.

Penn State capitalized on its early scoring, staying one step ahead and matching the Buckeyes’ scoring efforts with a point of its own until it went on a 3-0 scoring run to put some separation between the two as the blue and white went up 9-6.

The Buckeyes tied it up at 11-11 after scoring three-straight points, but another 3-0 scoring run put the Nittany Lions back in front at 14-11.

A 5-1 scoring run backed by three service errors put Ohio State back on top, seemingly out of nowhere, but Penn State quickly wrote back and recaptured its lead, going up 20-17 thanks to three quick Harvey kills, in turn forcing the Buckeyes to take another timeout.

Luoma sealed the deal for the blue and white with a service ace after a Bennett Wilson kill, leaving Penn State with a 25-20 third-set victory.

Pavlik’s unit went on an early 5-0 scoring run in the fourth set, with the points coming by way of a Harvey kill and four straight Buckeye errors, as the blue and white jumped out to a 6-2 lead in the early running.

Gaige Gabriel was having a quiet night until a service ace put his squad up four points.

The sophomore entered Wednesday’s match with the second-most service aces for Penn State, with 16, just one spot behind Rose.

A Luoma service error was followed by a Gabriel attack error, giving the Buckeyes two free points. Ohio State capitalized, and Stanislaw Chacinski recorded a kill that shrunk Penn State’s lead to 15-13, causing Pavlik to take a timeout.

Harvey moved up to 20 kills on the night as Wetzel picked up his 14th not long before another Chacinski kill caused the blue and white, holding a slight 22-20 advantage, to opt for another timeout.

The winningest active coach in NCAA men’s volleyball must have given his squad enough fire in their huddle, as it was enough for Penn State to take the fourth set 25-20 after three consecutive Buckeye errors to cap off the night with the 3-1 victory.

Up Next

Penn​​ State returns to Rec Hall for the first time in a month when it hosts NJIT for a two-match series this weekend. The first match is scheduled for 6 p.m. Friday, with the second match at 3 p.m. Saturday.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: 2 Penn State women's lacrosse representatives drafted to PLL
author: Zach Schroder | For the Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Amy Moreau and former player Kristin O'Neill were drafted by the Boston Guard. The Penn State women's lacrosse team members will join one of the four teams in the Women's Lacrosse League.

Congrats to Amy and Kristin to being drafted to the Boston Guard‼️‼️‼️🎉🎉🎉🎉 pic.twitter.com/AvEnC9KMpk— Penn State Women’s Lacrosse (@PennStateWLAX) March 25, 2026

Moreau is currently an assistant coach for the Nittany Lions. Previously, she played at the University of Massachusetts for five years before making the switch to coaching. She has also played professional lacrosse before with Athletes Unlimited.

O'Neill is an assistant coach with Towson, a position she's held since 2024. Before that, she played four years in Happy Valley, where she earned a pair of Big Ten First Team accolades.

The WLL recently finished its championship series, where the New York Charging was crowned after beating the California Palms 18-11. The next action for the WLL will be on May 16, when New York faces Boston.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘I Love’ Malcolm Todd | SPA hosts Malcom Todd in the BJC
author: Serina Chan | For The Collegian
description:
Penn State’s Student Programming Association (SPA) welcomed Malcolm Todd at the Bryce Jordan Center Tuesday night, featuring an opening act by Laundry Day.

Todd, a singer-songwriter and producer, is known for his R&B and indie pop fusion music. His first album entitled “Malcom Todd” was a hit on social media platforms, like TikTok, leading to his single, "Chest Pain (I Love),” making the Billboard Top 100.

Doors opened at 7 p.m., and fans flooded the BJC, stocking up on snacks and beverages.

On the floor, Catherine Waropay, said she appreciated that SPA organized the venue in a simple and easy way, allowing students to enter and find their designated areas without issues.

“It’s a great chance for everyone to get together and hang out while listening to music we all like,” Waropay, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “I’m personally most excited for the song ‘Sweet Boy.’”

Unlike Waropay, some people, like Adrianna Doyle, came to the concert without knowing much about Malcolm Todd, hoping to just have a good time and listen to new music. Since Todd recently rose in popularity on social media, he’s reached over 16.5 million monthly listeners on Spotify.

“I don’t know all of his songs but I was listening to him while I was getting ready today and I just became so excited for his concert,” Doyle, a second-year studying nursing, said.

With concert prices on the rise and out of budget for a lot of fans, Doyle said the discounted price of the student tickets pushed them to attend.

“I see so many events throughout the year and I love that the tickets were discounted and marketed towards what students want,” Doyle said. “SPA does a really good job of taking feedback and putting artists of what the students want to see.”

SPA also gave out free merchandise for the event, including a clear wrap-around bag, hand sanitizers and several accessories such as stickers, pens and a stuffed animal.

Once the concert began, Todd began with one of his most popular hits, “Earrings,” followed by other fan favorites such as “Chest Pain,” “4Me 4Me,” “Mr. Incorrect,” “Bleed” and “Cheer Me On.”

Todd performed his latest single, “Breathe,” for the first time live for audience members in the BJC.

Clara Cox said Todd was a great performer, and thought he engaged well with the audience during the concert.

“He really brought the energy to the crowd,” Cox, a second-year studying biobehavioral health, said. “But I loved how he also made us slow down and really think about living in the moment.”

Although she was impressed by Todd’s performance, Kate Tzoumas was pleasantly surprised by the organization of the event by SPA. She felt safe and well cared for when the SPA members were handing out refreshments to audience members on the floor.

“The whole concert ran so smoothly,” Tzoumas, a second-year student studying biobehavioral health, said. “I loved the trinkets and I loved the free refreshments. If they do this every time, I’m definitely going to more concerts in the future.”

Tzoumas was also excited to see SPA have such a variation in the artists they invite to Penn State.

“I love having a different type of artist coming here besides country music,” Tzoumas said. “I really appreciate it because we don’t usually get a lot of alternative music here.”

MORE LIFESTYLE COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's basketball guard Kiyomi McMiller to enter transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a record-breaking first year at Penn State, star guard Kiyomi McMiller will find her third team in as many seasons.

McMiller announced her entry to the transfer portal on Wednesday, according to Talia Goodman of On3, capping off a 627-point season in Happy Valley.

Penn State’s Kiyomi McMiller plans to enter the transfer portal, she told @On3. One of the nation’s leading scorers is on the move, following a coaching change for the Nittany Lions. https://t.co/8oEZDEwJpz— Talia Goodman (@TaliaGoodmanWBB) March 25, 2026

McMiller is the second Lady Lion to enter the portal, after Maggie Mendelson did on Tuesday. The two leave after the hiring of new head coach Tanisha Wright last Thursday.

The Silver Springs, Maryland, native spent her freshman year at Rutgers, starting in 19 games while averaging 18.7 points per game.

On March 27, 2025, she entered the transfer portal and ultimately committed to Penn State a month later. Her commitment to the Lady Lions sparked a comparison to the commitment of the Ann Meyers Drysdale Award winner in June 2023.

McMiller’s Penn State career started with three straight 20-point games, followed by her first of three double-doubles this season.

While starting in 26 of Penn State’s 29 games, McMiller tied Caitlin Clark’s Big Ten record for the most consecutive 30-point games, ending the year with 210 points in February. Against USC on Feb. 25, she recorded a career-high 40 points as she crossed the 1,000-point marker.

McMiller was named to the Big Ten Weekly Honor Roll in back-to-back weeks to end the season, ultimately being named to the All-Big Ten Media Second Team and All-Big Ten Coaches Third Team.

The 5-foot-8 guard finished her Penn State career with the 10th-most points in a single season in Penn State history, the fourth-most points in a sophomore year and the third-most 30-point games in a single season.

title: Takeaways from 2026 NCAA Wrestling Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
The 2026 NCAA Wrestling Championships featured some major upsets, while some of the top wrestlers at weight classes rolled through the bracket en route to a championship.

Penn State finished in first place, clinching the team title in Session 5 on Saturday morning. The Nittany Lions ended the weekend with 181.5 points, breaking their record from 2025. Oklahoma State finished in second with 131 points, Nebraska in third with 100.5 points and Iowa in fourth with 92.5 points to round out the trophy winners.

Here’s some takeaways from the weekend’s showing of wrestling on the biggest stage.

Penn State continues dominance

Penn State was the heavy favorite entering the championships, but it was unknown how commanding the victory would be for the Nittany Lions. As the first two days got underway, it was clear Penn State was going to win, and it was going to win big.

The Nittany Lions sent eight wrestlers to the quarterfinals, going a perfect 8-for-8, in sending the lineup to the semifinals. As each round continued, the number of points for the team continued to grow.

In addition to this, Penn State knew it had to score bonus points, which set it apart from the other teams. This year, the Nittany Lions scored 40.5 bonus points across the six sessions. A national champion who doesn’t score any bonus points earns 20 points for the team, meaning Penn State essentially had two additional national champions.

PJ Duke, Penn State’s 157-pound freshman who lost to eventual national champion Landon Robideau of Oklahoma State, scored more points for the team than Robideau. Duke had two falls and a tech fall, earning bonus points while Robideau won each match by decision, earning him the aforementioned 20 points.

The Nittany Lions dominated the field, with four champions — Luke Lilledahl, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines and Josh Barr. They had two wrestlers finish as the runner up with Shayne Van Ness and Rocco Welsh. Duke finished in third, Marcus Blaze finished in fourth and Braeden Davis and Cole Mirasola each went 2-2 in the tournament.

Oklahoma State’s surge

The Cowboys made their presence known in the NCAA Championships. They not only finished second in the team scoring, but also finished second in individual champions.

The big thing is that all three title winners were freshman with Jax Forrest at 133 pounds, Sergio Vega at 141 pounds and Landon Robideau at 157 pounds.

While Oklahoma State clearly has cemented itself as a top team in the country under former Penn State wrestler and assistant coach David Taylor, it is still a bit away from competing with the Nittany Lions.

With the young stars Taylor has, and the impressive incoming recruiting class which features the No. 4-6 2027 recruits per FloWrestling in Dreshaun Ross and Jordyn and Jayden Raney, it has the national outlook in anticipation for a potential overtaking of the Nittany Lions.

Ross has garnered high expectations, but last summer lost to Mirasola in the U20 World Team Trials. While that’s freestyle, and collegiate wrestling is folkstyle, it’s still likely that Ross has more ground to make up in the heavyweight class than what’s perceived.

The biggest key for the Cowboys is more bonus points. They notched 20.5 bonus points, an entire national championship run’s worth behind Penn State in that category.

While it’s trending toward solidifying itself as the No. 2 program, it’s going to be a bit until Oklahoma State can catch the Nittany Lions.

Ohio State underperforms

The team that entered as the favorites to finish second, Ohio State, severely underperformed. Multiple highly seeded wrestlers failed to place, causing a severe drop in points. The Buckeyes finished in fifth place with 84.5 points after getting some hype to potentially give Penn State a run for its money.

At 125 pounds, Nic Bouzakis was the No. 3 seed. Not only did he not wrestle to his seeding, he didn’t finish inside the top eight. The big questions for him were whether he’d be at top form with having to cut to 125 pounds each day to make weight. On the second day, he lost by major decision to No. 14 seed Jacob Moran of Indiana.

Ethan Stiles at 149 pounds also didn’t place despite being the No. 7 seed. In fact, he went 1-2 in the championships, failing to earn many points at all.

Nick Feldman at heavyweight was the No. 5 seed and didn’t place inside the top eight. He went 2-2, including a loss to No. 21-seed Juan Mora of Oklahoma.

There’s likely to be some significant changes in weight classes and starters for the next season as Ohio State attempts to have its wrestlers more prepared to thrive in March.

Stanford shines, places 6th

A couple years ago, it was unknown whether Stanford would even have a wrestling program. Now, the Cardinal are coming off a sixth-place finish at the 2026 NCAA Championships with 67.5 points.

With eight wrestlers qualified, half of them earned All-American status, including national champion Aden Valencia at 149 pounds.

Nico Provo and Tyler Knox at 125 pounds and 133 pounds, respectively, each placed, as the lightweights shined for Stanford. The other wrestler who placed was Angelo Posada at 197 pounds, but guys like Daniel Cardenas at 157 pounds earned additional points for a bonus-point victory.

The intriguing aspect of Stanford is the fact it doesn’t have a single senior on roster. It’s an extremely young team that performed exceptionally well at the national championships, giving it the opportunity to build on that for years to come.

title: Column | Penn State softball utilizing full pitching rotation will be key to success
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State kicked off the 2026 season on a fast start, going 19-6 in early nonconference play. However, the Nittany Lions have struggled as of late, with a 1-5 record in their first two Big Ten series against Oregon and Wisconsin.

Last season, the blue and white had a formidable offense, but couldn’t find that same consistency from their arms. At the start of this year, Penn State put the pieces together that it didn’t have in 2025, with complete production in both pitching and hitting.

However, since the start of conference play, the Nittany Lions’ numbers haven’t looked the same, especially from the circle.

In 25 games played and 163 innings pitched against nonconference opponents this season, Penn State posted a 2.40 ERA, ranking second among all Big Ten teams.

Through the Nittany Lions' six games and 37.2 innings pitched against two conference foes, they’ve totaled a 7.06 ERA, which is 13th in the Big Ten. This is a far higher number in even further less innings pitched.

For a bigger perspective, before its series against Oregon started on March 13, the blue and white owned a 2.40 ERA, which ranked third in the Big Ten and 19th nationally.

Penn State’s ERA now stands at 3.28, which is still highly impressive and enough for fifth-best in the conference. Still, if the Nittany Lions want to go on a strong run through Big Ten play and finish the season how they started, then this number will likely have to improve.

The blue and white have proved that they have a pitching staff that can take the team to new heights, but with the bulk of non-conference play over, adjustments have to come against familiar opponents.

One of the main reasons Penn State was successful in the young part of the season was because it relied on a full five-armed rotation to complete games. This depth hasn’t been as evident since the start of Big Ten play, but it might be just what the blue and white need.

The use of Penn State’s pitching rotation differed between its two conference series. In each of the first two games against Oregon, the Nittany Lions only went one deep into the bullpen, with McKenna Young coming in relief for Bridget Nemeth in Game 1 and Brevyn Kellepouris in Game 2.

However, in the series finale against the Ducks, four blue and white arms entered the circle as Abigail Britton and Mackenzie Duncan made their first appearances in Eugene.

The reason the difference in rotation between the first two games and the last is important is that in Game 3, Penn State took Oregon to the seventh inning, eventually losing on a walk-off. In the first two games, the Nittany Lions lost by a combined score of 18-3.

Flipping to the Wisconsin series, the blue and white used four arms in the first two games and three in the last, and this three-game set was much more competitive than the previous one against Oregon.

Penn State lost by a combined four runs in its two losses, while winning by six in its victory. Despite the series defeat, the improvement was definitely evident.

Every arm has its strength and the pitching staff has shown that it can build off one another and pick each other up. As the stakes get higher in Big Ten play, using all the tools in the toolbox will only make the attack from the circle better and this has been proven in the early parts of the season.

While it's understandable not to want opponents to see the same arms multiple times throughout the weekend, using all their power at once has brought the Nittany Lions success, so it's worth sticking to.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘Our spaces are for you’ | Campus groups aim to open conversations about reproductive health
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Reproductive health services and education have been at the forefront of national discourse, following the overturning of Roe v. Wade in 2022. As conversations ramp up about what constitutes sexual health care and whether it belongs in school curricula, campus organizations are aiming to provide resources and education at Penn State.

The Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity (CSGD), Relationship Violence Outreach, Intervention and Community Education (R-VOICE) and Days for Girls all provide reproductive health educational resources and supplies, such as free menstrual products.

Hailey Dermota, the vice president of Penn State’s Days for Girls chapter, said menstrual and reproductive health are “dramatically intertwined.” Days for Girls is a national organization that “works to eliminate the stigma and limitations associated with menstruation,” according to its website.

Penn State’s chapter is behind a number of period product initiatives on campus, including the inclusion of free period product dispensers in every newly renovated building on campus, which launched in 2023.

Currently, the organization is working on implementing the initiative in residence halls. A pilot program was launched in February to include these products in three West Halls dormitories — Watts, Thompson and Hamilton.

“Period poverty is here on campus as well,” Dermota said. “We've done a couple surveys, and we've seen some shocking numbers of how poverty affects individuals trying to get menstrual products.”

The club also helps campus offices get menstrual products to give out to students for free, including the Pride Pantry located at LL011 in the HUB-Robeson Center, CSGD and R-VOICE.

“Period products are too expensive in the market, so if that's the only place you can get it with a meal plan, it's too much,” Dermota said. “You don't always want to talk about how you're struggling financially, so it just gives a way for those students to access that with dignity, without having that shame and stigma that's always surrounded it.”

Dermota also emphasized how talking about reproductive health issues openly with peers, family and friends is critical as it helps people feel more confident in their health.

“You deserve to be helped and eased of your suffering and pain and whatever's going on that you know is not normal,” Dermota said. “Talk about menstruation openly. Talk about reproductive issues openly, because a lot of the time people are sharing similar experiences.”

Kate Rawson, assistant director of the CSGD, said there are a lot of misconceptions around who can receive assistance for reproductive healthcare.

“A lot of students within the queer and trans community do struggle with financial insecurity, food insecurity and housing insecurity, so as much as we can provide access to these things for free is wonderful,” Rawson said.

CSGD, which is in Room LL011 of the HUB-Robeson Center, and open Monday through Friday from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., hosts workshops centering queer-inclusive sexual education, consent education and helps students forge and find inclusive environments.

“Our spaces are for you,” Rawson said. “I think a lot of people think that you have to be a fully out queer-trans person to visit our center, or you have to look a certain way, or be a certain way. But our space is for everyone — allies as well. We are open to anyone, regardless of any identity, visiting our space.”

Similar to CSGD, the R-VOICE Center also gives out free products such as contraceptives and provides care for anyone who may have been affected by sexual violence.

“Our focus is all on doing outreach, education, prevention advocacy and then being able to support survivors,” Yvette Wilson, the director of R-VOICE, said. “The focus really is on meeting them where they’re at and helping them as they try to move forward, whatever that looks like.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball energized by Dollar Dog Night crowd in win over St. Bonaventure
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
The grills around Medlar Field at Lubrano Park were primed and ready, awaiting hungry Penn State fans. It was the first Dollar Dog Night of the season for the Nittany Lions with lines pouring into the ballpark, after the main gates opened, all with one goal in mind: getting a hot dog.

A crowd, full of 6,350 total hot dogs, accomplished its goal and then some. Backed by the support of 2,443 fans, the blue and white ended its first Dollar Dog Night with a 8-6 win over St. Bonaventure.

“I saw a lot of hot dogs going down (in the student section) tonight,” coach Mike Gambino said. “It’s Section 814, but it’s also the other 2,500 people in the park tonight. It’s not a warm night right now. I’ve said it over and over as we are trying to build what we think can turn into a championship-caliber baseball program.”

With the temperature at 40 degrees and a wind gust of 11 mph at first pitch, jackets and hoodies were scattered throughout the stands, even the Nittany Lion was dressed up in his Dollar Dog Night best.

Roll calls, from the newly formed student section, towards the Penn State players in the field filled the air around the ballpark as the first pitch crossed the plate.

Penn State used the first two innings to get runs across the plate. Bryce Molinaro singled in Jack Porter in the first inning, followed by a Preston Yaucher RBI single in the second. Two early runs in the first couple of innings only made a restless crowd more rowdy.

“It was great, and you see it in their dugout too, that when the crowd starts going, we score and the crowd starts going,” Gambino said. “You can just feel it, and then at the end, even though it got cold and not all the crowd stayed, it was loud when we were scoring at the end.”

Praises and bows surrounded the Nittany Lions as they stepped into the batter's box. Additionally, boo’s and heckles were thrown towards the Bonnies as they stepped up to the plate.

The Bonnies eventually got some runs, in the form of a five-run fourth inning. A two-run homer followed by a few RBIs gave St. Bonaventure the lead. Starting pitcher Frankie Sanchez was able to get through the first three innings unscathed, earning three strikeouts in the process.

Mason Horwat, Matthew VanOstenbridge and Robert Brown III were the arms out of the bullpen. The three relievers totaled eight strikeouts, not allowing an earned run.

“Horwat gives up the one (hit), but beats the barrel kind of an ‘excuse me’ single,” Gambino said. “Other than that, the guys were great tonight and then Robert Brown was really, really good. He was poised, had the breaking ball going, carrying the fastball tonight, he’s continuing to get better.”

The Penn State faithful had a reason to get riled up once again after catcher Avery Smith hit a 344-foot home run, cutting the deficit to one.

Following additional runs from St. Bonaventure, the crowd erupted after Molinaro hit a go-ahead two-run homer to give Penn State the lead 7-6. The Drums, Pennsylvania, native’s home run went 426 feet over the center field wall.

“Just trusting my plan, trusting my process,” Molinaro said. “Stuff hasn’t been going my way recently, but just not giving in and trusting what I do and what I do best, I think, takes me a long way.”

Those who braved the cold rose to their feet as the first Dollar Dog Night of the season came down to a final out. Maddox McDonald sent the fans home full and happy as he recorded the last out.

The Nittany Lions have little time to rest for their next game as they return to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park at 5:30 p.m. Wednesday against Bucknell.

title: SJP holds protest to ‘continue fighting until the full liberation’ of Palestine
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Chants filled the air outside Old Main as Penn State Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) organized a protest to call for the liberation of Palestine and Penn State divestment from Israel on Tuesday. The demonstration, titled “The Struggle Continues Until Liberation,” was part of SJP’s Week of Action, which is ongoing from Monday to Friday.

Members of SJP, the Muslim Student Association, the United Socialists at Penn State and Latino Caucus all gave speeches at the event. Alongside advocating for Palestinians, the groups protested in opposition of both the war in Iran and recent Israeli invasion of Lebanon.

“I think it's really important for things like the Week of Action to be able to encourage students to come together to meet each other and, of course, to get organized so we can strengthen our fight on campus and to achieve Palestinian liberation,” Sarah Dweik, an SJP executive board member, said.

Dweik, a postdoctoral teaching fellow in communication arts and sciences, said the week is a way to recommit to working toward the global liberation of Palestine.

The protest, which began at 6 p.m., saw demonstrators holding signs reading: “Hands off the Middle East,” “No Justice, No Peace” and “PSU funds genocide.”

Tahir Haideri, SJP’s speaker at the event, said protests across the country and on a global level have caused universities, companies and governments to begin divesting from Israel. He said divestment is possible at Penn State as well.

“There are people listening,” Haideri, a graduate student studying biomedical engineering, said. “It is getting bigger as time passes on and we're growing stronger, and it is our duty to continue fighting until the full liberation and return of the people of Palestine.”

The protest moved from Old Main to the Allen Street Gates, where attendees remained until dispersing around 7:15 p.m. Several cars honked their horns in solidarity throughout the protest.

Sergio Peña, an SJP board member, said it is important to continue bringing attention to Palestine, especially in light of Israel’s violations of an October 2025 ceasefire, which has resulted in the deaths of hundreds of Palestinians.

“We see how the genocide has continued to accelerate by Israel again denying humanitarian aid from entering,” Peña, a third-year PhD student studying workforce education and development, said. “When we say the struggle continues until liberation, we mean that until Palestine is free. Until the occupation has ended. Until the United States and Israel cease their genocidal violence against the Palestinian people.”

Peña said it is important that the Penn State community continues engaging with SJP and similar organizations. Becoming aware of individual power helps students better understand their collective power, according to Peña.

“The events that SJP puts on are not possible without all the amazing members we have — community members, students who see what our causes are and know that it is right and just,” Peña said. “That should hopefully give people more incentive and hope to engage in these kinds of struggles.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Penn State baseball defeats St. Bonaventure in first Dollar Dog Night of the season
author: Ava Aguiar | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions were back home in Medlar Field at Lubrano Park for the first Dollar Dog night of the 2026 season, where they took on the St. Bonaventure Bonnies.

Penn State brought home the win 8-6 in the top of the ninth, with strong performances across the board.

Starting pitcher Frankie Sanchez set Penn State up well in the first inning with zero runs on the board for the Bonnies, while Jack Porter scored Penn State's first run off Bryce Molinaro’s powerful single into right field.

St. Bonaventure couldn’t get the momentum going into the second inning. Nittany Lion Jayden Davis launched a ball into left center field, getting him to second base before running home on Preston Yaucher’s single to right field.

While Penn State didn’t score any runs in the third, they kept their dominance over St. Bonaventure with a strong pitching performance by Sanchez, striking out Joey Calabretti and Gavin Constantine, leaving Alex Hebenstreit stranded on first base before getting out on a run to second.

The Bonnies came in hot during the fourth inning, taking the lead 5-2 over the blue and white off a homerun by Antonio Cordeiro, letting in himself and Anthony Fernandez. Penn State’s Mason Horwat came in to relieve Sanchez, but the Bonnies remained strong, hitting in three more runs.

Avery Smith brought it back in the bottom of the fourth for Penn State, hammering the ball into left field for a home run double RBI, putting the score at 5-4.

The fifth remained quiet for both teams, as the Nittany Lions got a chance to take the lead to close out the sixth with the bases loaded. They fell just short as Saint Bonaventure’s shortstop Constantine threw a double out, leaving the blue and white just one run behind the Bonnies.

Second baseman Chris Hoalcraft had a solid seventh inning for St. Bonaventure, stealing second base and running home off of Fernandez’s hit into right field, leading the Nittany Lions to switch up their pitchers and throw the final out for the Bonnies this inning.

Determined to make a comeback, Penn State came in hot, taking the lead 7-6 in the seventh after Cole Mercado singled to second base off a throwing error, before running home off a power hit by Michael Anderson into center field. Molinaro closed it out, scoring the blue and white’s second home run of the game with a double RBI.

Penn State’s Maddox McDonald had a strong performance in the bottom of the eighth, stealing second base before scoring off Mercado’s fast hit into right field.

The Nittany Lions finished on top in their back-and-forth game with St. Bonaventure, with fourteen hits and two home runs, improving their record to 7-15.

Up Next

Penn State faces Bucknell at home at 5:30 p.m. Wednesday at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State notebook | Guy Gadowsky speaks on McKenna's Hobey Baker nomination, postseason prepapation
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State coach Guy Gadowsky was greeted with a wooden and plastic hockey stick at his press conference on Tuesday, as the first round of the NCAA Tournament awaits on Friday.

The wooden stick read “McKenna Magic.” The plastic one lists all the accomplishments and statistics of forward Gavin McKenna’s season. As the Hobey Baker top-three nominations are in full swing and fan voting is active, the Nittany Lions want as much coverage of McKenna as possible.

However, it is playoff time for Penn State, and there are much bigger things at stake. The Nittany Lions are set to play Minnesota-Duluth in the Albany Region Quarterfinal game of the NCAA Tournament at 9 p.m. on Friday.

Here is what Gadowsky said leading up to the most-anticipated postseason run in Penn State hockey history.

Gadowsky makes his case for McKenna for Hobey

McKenna has been on a preposterous run since his return from World Juniors in January. The freshman has dropped 33 points in 18 games since Jan. 9, a 1.83 points-per-game pace, which includes a program-best eight points against Ohio State in late February.

McKenna is second in the country in points per game at 1.50 — trailing only Quinnipiac’s Ethan Wyttenbach — and assists with 36, behind just Michigan’s Michael Hage.

“To play with 10 different line mates, which, because of necessity, of all the injuries that Fink has had, Dane (Dowiak) is out for the year. Chuck missed a long time… He's played with so many different guys, and to put up the numbers that he has, if you compare it to, for instance, last year's Hobey winner… blows that away,” Gadowsky said. “... So to do what he did in the toughest year in college hockey ever is absolutely incredible.”

Penn State’s schedule since loss to Michigan, preparations for Friday

The Nittany Lions have had a 10-day break and counting from game action since their loss to Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament semifinals on March 14. However, they did not treat the time off as if it were a bye week. Gadowsky confirmed Penn State is back on its “typical” schedule this week as it prepares to face second-seeded Minnesota-Duluth.

“We did not have a full week off,” Gadowsky said. “We did give extra time for healing because of where we're at as a team, health-wise. So obviously, (we) would have loved to have a shot at another Big Ten title, but if it didn't happen, the extra rest was very welcome. So we certainly didn't take an entire week off, but we certainly used it to get healthy and rested.”

Further, preparation-wise, Gadowsky and the team have started scouting Minnesota-Duluth. Typically, the team begins planning for opponents on Tuesday of a game week. That is not the case with the NCAA Tournament starting Friday.

“George Lewis is absolutely incredible,” Gadowsky said. “His title is Director of Operations. He handles analytics and video as well, and he has a team of very, very smart, intelligent, motivated student managers that love this program and are really, really smart. They've been working on different teams for well over a week. So Minnesota-Duluth, as well as four other teams — they've gone over them rather extensively.”

Gadowsky reveals the team identity

It is no secret that Penn State is an offense-oriented team. Gadowsky confirmed that to be the team’s identity on Tuesday, saying the Nittany Lions are a “high-octane rush offense.”

He said the team embraces it. However, one disadvantage to that style of play is a higher likelihood of turning the puck over. Gadowsky said the team knows the risks and is “comfortable” with it.

“We’ll make more great plays than turnovers,” Gadowsky said.

However, the turnovers come with the style of the offense that Gadowsky allows his players to play with. It's one that's more open, allowing their creativity to shine through.

“I don't think that's much of a surprise, but this is a team that loves to play offense, which we like here at Penn State,” Gadowsky said. “You can't do everything, but if you're going to be really, really good at one thing, I like the fact that that's it. Our stripes are that, we're very creative. We allow our team to be very creative. We believe in the creativity of our players to get it done… I think it's very fun hockey, I think it's very creative hockey. It's very fast. And we're comfortable with those… We're comfortable with that identity.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s basketball’s Maggie Mendelson will enter transfer portal
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Maggie Mendelson is one and done after basketball at Penn State.

Mendelson will enter the transfer portal after spending just one year with the Lady Lions, according to Talia Goodman of On3.

Penn State’s Maggie Mendelson has entered the transfer portal, sources told @On3. The 6-5 senior averaged 2 ppg this season. TRACKER: https://t.co/wYv1Ze6704 pic.twitter.com/kwbYrJJWJp— Talia Goodman (@TaliaGoodmanWBB) March 24, 2026

Following the hire of new coach Tanisha Wright on Thursday, Mendelson became the second athlete to look for a new home after Class of 2026 commit Chikae Desdunes reopened her recruitment earlier on Tuesday.

The North Ogden, Utah, native joined the Lady Lions in June after spending the previous year with the Nittany Lions’ volleyball team. She finished the 2025 season with the Nittany Lions before hitting the hardwood on Dec. 20 with coach Carolyn Kieger.

Mendelson started five games for Penn State, averaging 2.0 points and 3.3 rebounds per game.

Before joining the Lady Lions, Mendelson became a national champion with the Penn State women’s volleyball team in 2024, following two years with Nebraska.

In her first year with the Cornhuskers, Mendelson played both volleyball and basketball, but switched to just volleyball in Years 2 and 3 of her career.

Mendelson is out of volleyball eligibility and was recently signed by the Atlanta Vibe, but will spend the next year playing basketball with another school.

title: Penn State wrestling’s Mitchell Mesenbrink focused on bigger picture following NCAA Championship win
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
Saturday night, Mitchell Mesenbrink defeated long-time rival Mikey Caliendo of Iowa to win his second NCAA Championship. While for most people a national championship would be cause for celebration, for Mesenbrink, the victory opened his eyes to the place the sport of wrestling holds in his life.

The redshirt junior hesitated with his feelings after winning the title.

“Sometimes it takes attaining the goal to realize that the goal was not the thing to be attained,” Messenbrink said. “No matter what your goal is, it’s never as fulfilling as you think it is, and it definitely can't be the thing that you fill your soul with.”

Mesenbrink is dominant in a 165-pound weight class that looks like he’s lapped the field against. While the success itself hasn’t filled the cup of Mesenbrink, he retains motivation by seeing constant improvement in his game.

He told a story about lessons he learned from general manager Clay Steadman and his family. Steadman’s father was an artist and told Mesenbrink how tasks like homework are activities with a sent endpoint. This contrasts to how art and wrestling are evolving processes that can grow continuously.

“When you go into practice and want to enjoy it, you’re not focused on ‘when am I getting out of here,'" Mesenbrink said.

While Mesenbrink’s wrestling is still growing, he doesn’t want it to be all he focuses on. For the entire season, Mesenbrink has made it a point to describe wrestling as only a part of his life, rather than his defining trait.

He has delved into music, chess and social media expression through the course of the season as a way to show off a different side of his personality. Mesenbrink has been met with occasional pushback as he takes on these new journeys.

“I don’t really check comments, but the one time I checked the comments, they were like ‘shut up and stick to wrestling,”’ Mesenbrink said. “I’m way more than a wrestler, there’s so many other things in my life that can be important.”

The sentiment shared mirrors how the two-time national champion has spoken for the last two years. Mesenbrink’s faith and view of life shapes his focus past awards, wins or tangible items. He believes the world falsely praises these accolades as a fill in for a greater purpose in life.

“I think we’re meant to serve something greater than ourselves,” Mesenbrink said. “We get there and think ‘this is going to feel good for a day.’ But by Monday they’re going to be talking about who’s going to win it next year.”

A large portion of the program credits their success to their religion and Mesenbrink did the same following this victory. His state of mind that leads him to think differently than others, remain himself and be a dominant competitor, he credits to the freedom his faith provides.

"When you’re constantly like ‘I gotta do it this way, it’s gotta be my way or the highway,’ you are actually making yourself a prisoner in your own mind,” Mesenbrink said. “When you surrender to God and you understand it’s not your will, you actually gain all the control.”

Despite his success and creative ventures, Mesenbrink admitted to going through some personal struggles throughout the 2025-26 wrestling season. He again credits his faith and family for pulling him up through those times.

“This year hasn’t been easy at all and I think that’s when you need to pull those people that are closest to you,” Mesenbrink said. “I really love and appreciate all those people that I know God put into my life and maybe pulled out of my life too.”

Mesenbrink talked about his experience in Cleveland off the mat more importantly than the actual matches he competed in. During his time in Cleveland, the Hartland, Wisconsin native played his guitar and spent time with his family, practicing his faith to enjoy the vacation the sport of wrestling gave him.

Mitchell Mesenbrink is once again a national champion, but he is also an artist, a person and a family man. While the mainstream will look back on his success in Rocket Arena, his fondest memories will be more personal.

“When I look back on these times over this past year, I know it won’t be about bonus points or winning. It’ll be about sitting in my hotel room with my dad, my mom and my siblings,” Mesenbrink said. “If nothing went my way this weekend, those people were with me and are going to be there today as well.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘Violence is what justifies its own existence’ | Students for Justice in Palestine holds 'The Genocide is not over' display
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
Students for Justice in Palestine (SJP) held a display on the HUB lawn Monday to show solidarity for Palestinians.

This display is the first event in the SJP’s Week of Action, which is being held throughout the week to feature more informative displays and talks from speakers regarding the conflict.

“We want to convey to campus that, despite the so-called ceasefire that was enacted in October of 2025, the conditions of Gaza have continued to deteriorate significantly, especially since the US and Israel's war of aggression on Iran,” Sergio Peña, an SJP board member and graduate teaching assistant, said. “We’ve seen the conditions in Gaza worsen, especially because of the ways that Israel has continued its blockade of humanitarian aid.”

5 different “scenes” were set up around the lawn showcasing different views of current life in Palestine. Life in tents, by graves, by active warzones and by active military equipment were all displayed throughout the lawn. Despite the cold and rain, club members stood by the exhibit, ready to answer questions and inform the public.

“Every moment is worth it,” Ronnie Reiley, an SJP board member, said. “Regardless of what conditions we find ourselves in here in America, what the Palestinians are experiencing is these same conditions but worst with the active threat of genocide. So, it may be raining and it may be cold but I’m not living in a tent. I’m not living where there’s flooding, where there’s rats. I’m going to finish this day, and I’m gonna go home to my bed. And I think everyone should be able to do that.”

Reiley, a third year graduate student in the social vision and interpersonal perception lab, joined approximately 20 other students who were rotating shifts at the exhibit, including Peña and Jared Carvajal, an event volunteer. They shared drinks and snacks while withstanding the wind and rain.

“As part of SJP, it’s our job to inform the masses and tell them what’s going on, that the situation didn’t start October 7 — it started over 70 years ago,” said Carvajal, a fourth-year studying philosophy. “This is a problem. This is an issue that is long standing, and that we should never forget.”

While Reiley and Peña were at the demonstration out of outrage for the Israel-Palestine conflict, Carvajal was there for personal reasons.

“When I was growing up, I had a neighbor we called Sid,” Carvajal said. “In 1967, during the 7 day war, Israel displaced him and he was forced to leave. He lived in the US for the rest of his life, and he was buried here, but he hasn’t been able to go back. His body is still in a cemetery in the United States. And to me, that injustice, that inability to go back, really motivates me as a moral person who believes that you should try to operate in a way that does good in the world.”

Péna said improvement for Palestine means improvement for the whole world.

“We’ve seen how Israel oppresses Palestinians and forces apartheid and has committed genocide against Palestinians, which in many ways also reflects the sort of conditions we see politically in the US as well,” Peña said. “We, as a community, have the power to collectively stand up to the systems that create the conditions of violence, war, genocide, that these things are not bigger than us. They are a part of us, and we have every right to stand up to them when we think something isn’t right.”

The Week of Action will continue tomorrow with a protest on Old Main lawn, and further into the week with tabling and talks.

“Violence is what justifies its own existence," Carvajal said.

Penn State guard Melih Tunca will enter the transfer portal, his agent Tolga Beritan told DraftExpress. The 6'5 Turkish freshman averaged 7.8 points and 2.3 assists in 23 minutes per game. pic.twitter.com/RcdLgGY4w3— Jonathan Givony (@DraftExpress) March 24, 2026

title: Staff predictions | 4 bold predictions for Penn State entering spring ball
author: The Daily Collegian football staff
description:
Surprise, surprise, spring ball is here.

Matt Campbell began his first spring session in his new home on Tuesday.

Here’s some unexpected storylines that could emerge.

Avery Hill: Unfamiliar faces win key roles on defense

New coaches mean new everything. Matt Campbell has already started that process by bringing in an entirely new culture to Penn State. I think these changes especially ring true on the defensive side of the ball.

The expectation among many is the Iowa State starters immediately slot in the same role in Happy Valley, but I think with plenty of variety on the coaching staff defensively, both depth returning Penn Staters and non-Cyclone transfers get a clean slate to show what they’re capable of, and a few will emerge.

Namely, I expect safety Vaboue Toure, defensive end LaVar Arrington ll and defensive tackle Armstrong Nnodim to take increased roles, potentially challenging starters near the end of the spring session.

Toure has played a limited role in his two seasons as a Nittany Lion, totaling 19 tackles in 16 games, but totaled seven tackles and a sack against Clemson in the Pinstripe Bowl. The expected starters are currently Marcus Neal Jr. and Jeremiah Cooper, but the former 4-star recruit, per 247Sports Composite, could earn the third safety role for big nickel and other safety-heavy packages over Iowa State transfer Jamison Patton and others.

Nnodim, an Oklahoma State transfer, is already expected to be one of the first defensive tackles off the bench, but I think he could become a starter at some point in 2026.

In front of him is likely Siale Taupaki and Keanu Williams, both of whom defensive line coach Ikaika Malloe coached at UCLA, along with Utah’s Dallas Vakalahi. Nnodim, a redshirt sophomore with a sort of buff build at 319 pounds, should prove to be more versatile than the trio, naturally moving him up in the depth chart.

LaVar Arrington ll, who will play more defensive end in 2026 after joining Penn State as a 4-star linebacker last season, could see serious strides. I don't think that will be all, though, as I think players like linebacker Cam Smith could take a big step as well.

Lexie Linderman: Brock Riker takes a strong lead in the center battle

Penn State only has one starting offensive lineman returning from last season, leaving plenty of positions up for grabs on the front five.

One of those competitions is at center, where redshirt senior Dominic Rulli and redshirt sophomore Brock Riker are set to face off throughout the spring and summer ahead of Week 1.

Although Riker is younger and a bit smaller than Rulli, spring ball is the perfect opportunity for Riker to prove he can be the Nittany Lions’ starting center, and I predict he does just that.

The coaching staff has had plenty of praise for Riker, who transferred from Texas State this offseason. He was a freshman All-American per The Athletic last season after appearing in 12 games, showcasing the potential he has.

“I thought he was the best center in the world personally,” offensive line coach Ryan Clanton said Feb. 4. “That guy's raw talent and ability is more than enough, he was extremely, obviously, highly recruited, and everybody that was recruiting him, you knew why they were recruiting him too.”

If Riker reaches his potential, there’s no doubt he could win the starting center job over Rulli, who’s been waiting his turn to be the starting center for five years. But I don’t expect this position battle to be shored up any time soon, as both Riker and Rulli have a real chance at securing the spot.

Lyle Alenstein: Wide receivers see increased work

At Iowa State, offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser ran a lot of 13 personnel, which meant three tight ends were often on the field at once. When he departed the Cyclones to follow Matt Campbell to Penn State, a trio of tight ends in Benjamin Brahmer, Gabe Burkle and Cooper Alexander joined him in State College.

If Mouser were to follow the same offensive philosophy for the Nittany Lions, the projected starters in two-tight end sets would be Andrew Rappleyea and Brahmer, with Burkle likely being the third.

But Rappleyea and Burkle are out for spring practice with injuries, which will increase reps for Alexander, Brian Kortovich and Finn Furmanek, the only other tight ends on the roster. Despite other tight ends seeing more of a workload, there’s a possibility Mouser strays away from what he’s comfortable calling plays for and increases heavy wide receiver sets.

The projected wide receivers entering spring ball are Iowa State transfers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen, as well as Koby Howard. The injuries to the likely top pass catchers in the tight end room should give them more targets in spring ball.

On top of the aforementioned trio, it results in guys competing for their spot on the depth chart, and maybe even a starting opportunity. Names to keep an eye on are Grambling State transfer Keith Jones Jr., Iowa State transfer Zay Robinson and Peter Gonzalez.

Will Horstman: Quinton Martin Jr. separates himself as No. 1 running back

With Kaytron Allen and Nick Singleton off to the NFL, the No. 1 running back spot in 2026 is up for grabs heading into spring ball. One of the candidates to earn that job is redshirt sophomore Quinton Martin Jr., who could have a chance to separate himself from the pack throughout spring practices.

Martin rarely saw any action during his first two seasons as a depth piece behind Allen and Singleton. However, he got extended playing time in the Pinstripe Bowl win over Clemson, tallying 20 carries for 103 yards in his first time seeing double-digit touches in a game.

It was a flash of the former 4-star prospect’s potential, but for Martin to earn the No. 1 running back role next season, he’d have to beat out portal additions Carson Hansen and James Peoples.

Hansen transferred in from Iowa State, where he has proven production with 952 rushing yards in 2025. Peoples had a limited role with Ohio State the last two seasons, totaling 541 career rushing yards.

It’s a three-way battle for the top spot in the running back room, but Martin could be set up well to build on his Pinstripe Bowl performance in spring ball and become the starter.

title: CATA to launch tap-to-pay option in April
