The Nittany Lions will go on the road to face No. 3 Maryland on Sunday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: ‘It’s definitely different’ | Former players reflect on returning to Penn State after coaching change
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Lasch Building has seen plenty of change since it opened in 1999, but its most recent appearance had become the one players and coaches were accustomed to.

“1-0” was plastered throughout the building and on the practice field. What James Franklin considered Penn State’s core values were there as well, and familiar faces graced the halls and offices.

But now the walls on the practice field sit blank, and there are only a few of the same people left. The players who participated in pro day on Wednesday came back to a completely different Penn State football program.

“It's definitely different, but it's been good getting to know the staff,” Drew Allar said. “Things are reorganized and all that sort of stuff. But it's been good meeting everybody again, between staff, training staff, obviously all the players, and then catching up with all my old teammates that are still around.”

Although most of the staff is brand new, Allar and his former teammates have liked what they’ve seen and heard so far from Matt Campbell, who made an appearance at pro day and spoke at length with lettermen Olu Fashanu and Sean Clifford, among others.

Allar especially spoke highly of Campbell, as the former Nittany Lion said the two built a relationship when Iowa State recruited him out of high school.

“Honestly, Iowa State, when I was coming out of high school, was in my top three, so I had a lot of respect for him coming out of high school,” Allar said.

Former starting center Nick Dawkins also highlighted his respect for Penn State’s new head coach, as Dawkins watched from afar as Campbell turned Iowa State from one of the worst programs in history to a competitive Big 12 program.

But Campbell isn’t the only new face in the building. Dawkins barely recognizes over half the team now after 50 players left for the transfer portal and 55 players moved to Happy Valley from elsewhere.

“Even the guys, though, from last year, like there's not many guys from the team last year that are here now,” Dawkins said. “I'm sure there's a couple but I haven't ran into many.”

A few guys from the 2025 team were at pro day, though, as Tony Rojas, Audavion Collins, Cooper Cousins and Dominic Rulli were a few that came to support their former teammates.

Regardless, the former Nittany Lions at pro day can sense the future at Penn State is bright, and they’ll be supporting them from afar as they potentially represent their alma mater in the NFL.

“I'm a Penn Stater for life. So I just hope this place does well,” Dawkins said. “I hope they're able to get done the things that I so badly wanted to do, and I think they will. And I have trust and belief in the staff, and I have trust and belief in the administration for making this selection.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State rebounds from years of low free speech rankings, but FIRE warns its gains could be at risk
author: Tyler Nolt | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Part of the Collegian’s commitment to transparency is telling our readers when and how AI was used in our reporting. The writer used Microsoft Copilot to help sort and analyze data for this story. Click here for more details on how it was used and what we did to fact check it.

As conversations over free speech intensify in today’s political climate, Penn State has become the subject of complaints from free speech advocacy groups over its failure to uphold the First Amendment on campus.

Most recently, the university has come under fire for its ongoing investigation of a poster on campus depicting a U.S. Immigrations and Customs Enforcement officer hanging from a noose. An online image shows the poster on a pole with the caption “Dead ICE agents can’t kill.”

The nonprofit Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE) sent a letter to the university in February arguing the poster “does not fall into one of the narrow categories of speech unprotected by the First Amendment.”

The organization also launched a page on its site where people could send a letter to the university, asking it to drop the investigation. As of March 16, 247 people filled out the form online, according to FIRE.

Jacqueline Sheader, public information officer at Penn State University Police and Public Safety, told the Collegian that the investigation is still ongoing as of March 16. The university did not respond to follow-up questions about the contents or timeline of the investigation.

In a statement to the Centre Daily Times, university assistant vice president for strategic communications at Penn State Colleen Mastony said, “Penn State condemns this and any calls for violence or attempts to frighten or intimidate.”

FIRE’s Chief Research Advisor Sean Stevens said the ongoing investigation could cause Penn State to drop in the organization’s annual College Free Speech Rankings.

Penn State’s rankings over the years

FIRE, established in 1999, works to protect free speech on and off college campuses through student and faculty outreach, individual case advocacy, policy reform efforts and more.

In 2024, FIRE placed an ad in The Daily Collegian criticizing Penn State’s decision to remove newsstands after the paper placed a paid campaign advertisement for Kamala Harris on three of those newsstands, and voter registration ads on six. The university said the initial advertisements were in violation of its policy.

One of the organization’s most well-known endeavors is its annual ranking of U.S. colleges based on their free speech climate. Starting with the 2024 report, which reflects on 2023, each report covers the preceding year.

Since the first rankings in 2020, the number of surveyed colleges has grown from 55 to 257. Penn State, one of the original 55, has appeared in the rankings annually since its inception.

Penn State placed 25th in the first year, ranking in the top half of colleges. In the second year, the university dropped to 70th place following the addition of 100 new colleges. Still, Penn State advanced two ranks when only in comparison with the original 55.

Starting with the 2024 report, which reflects on 2023, each report covers the preceding year. Penn State’s rankings have continued to decline over the years, landing it in the bottom 10% of all institutions in the 2025 College Free Speech Rankings.

The survey has traditionally been conducted by College Pulse, an organization that surveys U.S. college students for businesses and nonprofits. For the 2026 College Free Speech Rankings, the survey began in early January 2025 and ended in June 2025. To participate, respondents were required to share their Penn State issued email addresses.

Institutions have occasionally made changes after the release of the College Free Speech Rankings. According to FIRE, their team has helped reform “more than 30 campus policies” through meetings with a number of institutional leaders in 2025.

In addition to the survey, a college’s speech policies and the outcomes of speech-related controversies on campus impact the final score as well.

In the 2025 College Free Speech Rankings, Penn State ranked 228th out of 251 surveyed universities, and placed 14th out of the 15 Pennsylvania-based colleges, and 15th out of the 18 Big Ten schools.

FIRE and the American Civil Liberties Union of Pennsylvania sent a letter to Penn State President Neeli Bendapudi in October 2024 claiming the university was exhibiting a “concerning pattern” of speech suppression leading up to the presidential election.

The letter threatened legal action and asked for a “substantive response” within three days. FIRE’s Student Press Counsel Marie McMullan said the organization didn’t receive a response. The university did not respond to The Daily Collegian’s request for comment on whether the school issued a response to FIRE.

Still, since then, Penn State rose 119 places in FIRE’s most recent report, making it the most improved college in Pennsylvania and the 3rd most out of the Big Ten schools. The university tied with American University for 10th most improved overall, and it was ranked 109 out of 257 colleges.

This is the first time since 2020 that the university had no new deplatforming attempts listed.

Penn State’s one recorded speech controversy in the report is the alleged deplatforming attempt of Turning Point USA speaker Riley Gaines in 2023, which has appeared in two reports since.

The former University of Kentucky swimmer, an activist who has been outspoken against transgender women’s participation in women’s sports, accused Penn State of canceling her planned speaking engagement. The university issued a statement saying they did not cancel the event and cited a problem with the booking of an indoor space. The statement said Turning Point missed the deadline for submitting the necessary documents. Gaines appeared on campus in a different location.

How FIRE determines free speech rankings

FIRE’s ranking methodology has evolved over the years. The most recent rankings were based on three types of assessments: Student perceptions of their campus’ speech climate, campus speech policies and speech controversy outcomes.

The university hasn’t earned a passing score since 2021 when they scored 59.63. The closest they’ve gotten since was in the most recent 2026 College Free Speech Rankings when the university was short 1.21 points.

Student perceptions — made up of 379 responses from full-time Penn State undergraduate students in 2025 — made up a majority of each overall score. The survey asked about a university’s openness, administrative support, comfort expressing ideas, disruptive conduct, self-censorship and political tolerance. On the latter, Penn State ranked 8th out of 257.​​

FIRE also looks at policies that regulate free expression. Penn State policies are currently categorized as code yellow, which FIRE determined means the university has “at least one ambiguous policy that too easily encourages administrative abuse and arbitrary application.”

According to FIRE, if Penn State revised two of its speech policies, it could have earned a “green light” rating and moved up 78 positions in the rankings.

Conflicting views highlight ongoing tensions

For some, like Sean Semanko, founder and CEO of Uncensored America, and Amari Saunders, political affairs chair for Penn State College , the reason for the university’s improvement in the FIRE rankings is unclear.

“Penn State, unfortunately, has not gotten better, and it's only gotten worse,” said Semanko, who alongside Uncensored America filed a lawsuit against the university in October over a separate free speech issue.

Uncensored America, a nonprofit emphasizing freedom of speech through hosting conversations with “controversial figures,” was established in 2020 by Semanko while he was a Penn State student. What began as a small on-campus effort has now gone nationwide. Their videos have amassed millions of views on YouTube and the organization has chapters at 11 universities.

Semanko described the university as “doing everything in their power to make (their) lives harder,” when scheduling events. Uncensored America has hosted seven on-campus events since 2021.

Uncensored America alleges in its lawsuit against the university that Penn State restricted their free speech via “denials of security and funding, forced relocation of events indoors, and outright cancellations.”

In the filing, the organization claims Penn State’s policies are unconstitutional, alleging that without intervention, conservatives will continue to be silenced by the university.

The lawsuit could impact the university’s overall score if it reveals an incident demonstrating suppression of free speech that isn’t already in FIRE’s database, Chief Research Advisor Stevens said in an email.

Saunders, political affairs chair for Penn State College , agreed that the university’s free speech climate hasn’t improved since FIRE’s letter in 2024.

He said he believes the university has since placed stricter rules on his and other advocates tabling efforts, echoing complaints FIRE received in 2024 that Penn State restricted students’ ability to engage in election-related expressive activities on campus.

“For political canvases, or people who are tabling for certain organizations or groups, it's kind of harder for them to get a table,” Saunders said. “There's so many rules that you have to follow … so it's kind of strict in the sense where you don't know really how you would advocate … without getting in trouble, just because the rules are kind of blurred.

The university did not respond to The Daily Collegian’s request for comment regarding these claims.

Penn State College Republicans President Tristin Kilgore said he doesn’t share Semanko and Saunders' concerns. Kilgore said the university is doing what it can to foster a free speech environment.

“Neeli Bendapudi and the administration is very supportive of free speech, and they have been the entire time I've been here,” Kilgore, a third-year studying political science and philosophy, said. “If you take care of the stuff you need to do — you reserve the space, you bring the speakers, you do everything the way you're supposed to do it — they will support you.”

Kilgore cited as an example the vigil for assassinated political activist Charlie Kirk organized by the College Republicans and Penn State’s Turning Point USA chapter in September 2024.

For Kilgore, it's the general student body that expresses a lack of support for free speech. He said there’s a “trend of violence” deterring students from having more honest civic discussions, referencing the ICE poster.

Kilgore and the College Republicans described the poster as a death threat, calling for an investigation in a statement posted on X.

It was shortly after that Centre Daily Times covered the university's initial response, and that FIRE sent the university a letter arguing the poster is protected speech.

FIRE also sent a letter to the Office of the Chancellor at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign the day prior. In this case, however, it was defending an Instagram post made by the school’s College Republicans chapter. The post read, “only traitors help invaders,” and according to FIRE’s description, the post included a “poster depicting the shooting of Alex Pretti.”

On Feb. 19, members of FIRE’s campus rights advocacy team came to Penn State to hand out campus protest guides and other free speech materials.

“Penn State has not yet responded to our advocacy efforts, although we hope Penn State will decide to engage and affirm its students’ free speech rights,” Charlotte Arneson, program counsel for FIRE’s advocacy team, told the Collegian in an email.

Shortly after, the College Republicans shared a photo on X showing what seems to be a second appearance of the poster, in a different location.

Both the College Republicans and College issued a joint statement on the matter through Instagram, condemning the poster.

Not long after, FIRE changed the case’s status from “active” to “closed” because the university still hadn’t addressed their advocacy efforts, according to Alex Griswold, communications campaign manager for FIRE. However, he specified that FIRE would reexamine the case if they “receive any updates from students involved or the university.”

Prior to this incident, FIRE sent another letter to Penn State, calling upon the university to “end its deferred recruitment policy banning first-semester students from joining fraternities and sororities.” It claims the university’s policy goes against the First Amendment's protection of students’ right to freedom of association.

In both of these cases, Stevens told the Collegian that each of the two cases will constitute “a loss of up to 2 points” in the College Free Speech Rankings, but that the exact penalty value has not yet been determined by their research team.

Surveys for the 2027 College Free Speech Rankings are underway and are likely to be released in September.

Amy Sanders, the John and Ann Curley professor of First Amendment Studies, began working at Penn State in January 2025 in search of a better free speech climate.

“I came to Penn State from Texas,” Sanders said. “I've only been here a year, and part of the reason that I left the University of Texas was because of the administration's response to protests on campus.”

Penn State and The University of Texas at Austin have been ranked against each other since 2020, and Penn State surpassed them each time.

“I think Penn State is doing a pretty good job in terms of freedom of expression,” Sanders said. “What we don't do well is transparency. If we don't know what's happening at our university, that's going to infringe on our free expression rights because we're not going to be able to evaluate what's happening here.”

MORE INVESTIGATIONS

title: Baseball, yoga, dancing | This week in State College
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
As the semester continues and Spring has officially started there are many events happening around the Penn State community for students, faculty members and the public to enjoy.

Here are some events to enjoy as State College begins to warm up.

Monday, March 23

CAMP: Campus Access to Movement

From noon to 4 p.m., Counseling and Psychological Services (CAPS) will be hosting an accessible introduction to resources offered by the Intramural (IM) Building. During this time there will also be a lunch-and-learn with Health Promotion and Wellness, Campus Rec guided facility tour and a light, inclusive yoga class.

The event is meant for students to feel more comfortable and supported in recreation areas.

Yoga and Meditation

From 5:30 to 6:30 p.m., there will be a yoga and meditation session happening at the Pasquerilla Spiritual Center room 107.

Anyone is welcome to let their mind and body fall into a rejuvenating yoga and meditation practice. All the sessions are led by certified instructors and everyone is welcome to come with experience or no experience.

Supplies are limited, so it is recommended to bring your own.

Tuesday, March 24

Study Smarter Session

From 11 a.m. to 1 p.m., Penn State Learning will be hosting a virtual interactive workshop that presents research-based evidence on how people learn and the predictable effects of the related choices people make which can help them transition to the demands of college-level courses.

During the workshop they will talk about how the brain stores information and will explore the six strategies that are most effective to the learning process. Students will also be given examples of how they can apply these strategies to their current classes. Registration for this workshop is required.

Salsa Lessons

The Penn State Social Dance Club will be teaching salsa lessons at the State College Municipal Building from 6 to 8:30 p.m. The lessons are open to all, from students and faculty to community members.

You can come with a partner or by yourself, as they will pair up solo members for lessons. The first 90 minutes will be lessons, and the remainder of the event will be for social dancing to music.

Malcolm Todd Concert

From 8 to 10 p.m., Malcolm Todd will be performing at the Bryce Jordan Center as part of a concert hosted by the Student Programming Association (SPA). Doors to the concert will open at 7 p.m.

Tickets can be found on Ticketmaster starting at $20.

Wednesday, March 25

Etiquette Lunch - Dine Like a Professional

The Civic Engagement Alliance and the Student Programming Association (SPA) will be hosting a workshop in the HUB-Robeson Center, Flex Theatre from noon - 1 p.m., for students to learn the valuable tips that will help them make a positive impression at any business event.

Students will learn about tradition etiquette rules for meals along with some general topics on how to make an impression on fellow professionals.

This event will help people stand out as a polished professional in their future endeavors. There is no dress code for this event.

Splatoon Club Weekly

The Splatoon Club at Penn State will be hosting their weekly meeting in Westgate Building from 7 to 9 p.m. to do a variety of fun activities. Students can join their discord to learn more.

Thursday, March 26

Booked for Murder: Mystery Book Club

From 6 to 7 p.m. stop by the Centre County Library and Historical Museum to join the Mystery Book Club where for the month of March they will be reading “The Honjin Murders” by Seishi Yokomozio.

“The Honjin Murders” is a classic Japanese locked-room mystery treat for fans of Golden Age “whodunits” and is an award-winning example of the honkaku (orthodox) mystery style.

Registration for this event is not required. People can sign up for monthly text reminders for the club by signing up online.

Books are available at the Bellefonte circulation desk.

Bachata Lessons

The Penn State Social Dance Club will be teaching bachata lessons at the State College Municipal Building from 6 to 8:30 p.m. The lessons are open to all, from students and faculty to community members.

You can come with a partner or by yourself, as they will pair up solo members for lessons. The first 90 minutes will be lessons, and the remainder of the event will be for social dancing to music.

Friday, March 27

Penn State vs. Illinois

Happening at 5:30 p.m., the Penn State baseball team will take on Illinois at Medlar Field at Lubrano Field. This is the first of a three game home series against Illinois with the other two games occurring March 28 and 29.

All Penn State baseball home games are open and free to the public.

Saturday, March 28

Happy Valley Animation Festival (HVAF) Screening

From 11 a.m. to 5 p.m., stop by the Palmer Art Museum to enjoy screenings from a selection of animated works submitted to the 2026 HVAF. HVAF aims to showcase animated works in a range of categories.

Registration for this event is not required.

MORE CAMPUS CONTENT

title: Column | Women need more representation behind the camera
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
In case you missed it, the 98th annual Academy Awards were on March 15. Normally, it’s categories like acting, directing and best picture that get a lot of buzz, but a more subtle category arose in prominence that night.

As Demi Moore announced Autumn Durald Arkapaw was the winner of best cinematography, for her work on the film “Sinners” — making her the woman ever to win the award — something in the room shifted.

During her acceptance speech, she asked all the women in the room to stand up. “I feel like I don’t get here without you guys,” she said as the women stood cheering.

Although it was a historic win met with celebration, it also brought a sobering revelation. It took 98 years for the Academy of Motion Pictures to award a woman the award for achievement in cinematography, not to mention she was just the fourth woman overall to be nominated in that category and also the first woman of color to be nominated.

While it seems like progress has been made, the reality is that the progress has been minuscule, as women continue to be underrepresented in roles behind the camera.

In 2025, women accounted for 26% of producers, 21% of executive producers, 20% of editors, 20% of writers, 10% of directors, 7% of cinematographers and 8% of composers in the top 100 films of the year according to San Diego State University’s Center for the Study of Women in Television & Film’s annual Celluloid Ceiling Report.

Of that figure, for directing, only 5.4% were women of color, while over a 19-year period, women of color represent 1.9% of top-grossing movie directors according to Variety.

It’s clear women are still not getting the opportunities to be in male-dominated roles. The director’s chair is a higher-profile position and the attention creates pressure to allow women to be at the helm.

Important roles such as editing and cinematography tend to fly under the radar, preventing awareness about the low number of women in those positions, ultimately allowing things to remain unchanged.

It’s important to note that in films with at least one female director, women were employed substantially in key roles behind the scenes. Women made up 71% of writers, 28% editors and 22% of cinematographers. Whereas films with male directors, women accounted for 11% of writers, 19% editors, and 5% of cinematographers, respectively, according to the same Celluloid Ceiling Report.

Providing a space where women can be represented in behind-the-scenes roles creates an expanded perspective because it’s a less homogenized point of view. We have all lived different experiences and have different stories to tell, which contribute to the authenticity of film. It helps to create female characters with depth and nuance that go beyond the role of a one-dimensional girlfriend type.

Though film is an escape, it should still depict female characters who feel real and are able to have their own agency. They shouldn’t be reduced to some trite stereotype to be viewed through a male lens.

One of the great things about film is that it can provide us with a different perspective, but how is that possible if there isn't a diversity of perspectives creating the film? Gender shouldn’t be a barrier in anything, yet it continues to be, even in the film industry. It’s clear that it’s more of a hiring issue, as women are represented well in film schools. Ignoring this issue only fuels gender bias by normalizing the inequality that continues today.

“I heard a phrase that said you need to see you,” Arkapaw told the Associated Press last year. By having women in these roles behind the camera, it helps set an example for younger generations. It shows young ladies they’re just as capable of being in these roles as anyone else.

In a time where Hollywood seems to be gravitating more towards sequels and remakes, perhaps it’s time to turn to female creatives to tell the stories they’ve been wanting to tell. While the current political administration may frown upon diverse stories, those opinions should be ignored. The whole point of creating art is to express oneself and connect with others and it should be done so with full artistic freedom.

In a similar vein to Arkapaw’s speech this year, Frances McDormand, during her speech for her Best Actress win in 2018, asked all the female nominees in every category to stand up.

“Look around ladies and gentlemen, because we all have stories to tell and projects we need financed,” McDormand said. Eight years after that speech, it’s a sentiment that continues to be true and won’t go away unless real change is made. I’ll end this column with the words Frances McDormand ended her speech with: inclusion rider.

MORE COLUMNS

title: 'We really appreciate it when people come out to support us' | Penn State women's lacrosse hosts Senior Day
author: Annie Alberts | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State took a loss to No. 6 Michigan on its Senior Day. The final score was 11-6.

Despite a previous 9-1 record, the Nittany Lions weren’t able to keep up with Michigan’s aggressive style of play. According to coach Kayla Treanor, the team just didn’t have a great day.

Offensively, Penn State struggled. There were many unforced errors, such as dropping the ball, stepping in the crease and being offside. But, it wasn’t like they didn’t score at all. Penn State successfully got six goals into the net, two of them even came back-to-back. Juniors Kelly MacKinney and Delaney Radin had a great day, both racking up three more points to their statistics. And, on top of that, it was a day to honor the upperclassmen.

“I’ve only been able to coach them for one year,” Treanor said. “But they're really a tremendous group of kids.”

The women’s lacrosse organization took the time to recognize the seven seniors on their team: Emily Bette, Maggie Driver, Maddie Greco, Erika Ho, Emma Kelly, Lauren Tinson and Payton Wainman. Wainman specifically has made massive contributions to the team this season. She scored two goals this afternoon, making her goal count heighten to 30. She leads her team in scoring goals, trailing in front of Rocquette Allen by five.

Three of the four captains on the team are seniors, giving them chances to take large leadership roles for the younger players. Treanor says they are all great leaders, but the girls on the team who aren’t seniors play large leadership roles as well. In addition to being senior day, today’s game fell on alumni weekend.

“We had right around 50 alums come back, so it was a great turnout,” Treanor said. “We really appreciate it when people come out to support us.”

Although the end result wasn’t what the Nittany Lions were hoping for, they believe they could bounce back in future games. Treanor expressed that the team is currently very disciplined defensively, but could work on their offensive skills.

“We gotta have better offensive possessions,” Treanor said. “We gotta execute on game day.”

Even though Penn State only had four fewer shots on goal than Michigan, Treanor said that doesn’t tell the whole story. The team’s offense tends to have spurts in which they score goals, according to their coach, which didn’t stand against Michigan’s rapid-fire goal technique.

“We just focus on, not the results, just playing to our standard,” Treanor said. “We have a great opponent coming up next. Hopefully, this fuels the team.”

Next week, Penn State faces off against No. 3 Maryland on its home turf in College Park, Maryland. It marks the highest ranked opponent of Penn State's schedule so far, and the blue and white are ramping up for the mental task.

“Everything we preach is playing to the Penn State standard, regardless of the scoreboard, regardless of our opponent, and every game, and every practice,” said Treanor.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's gymnastics falls to Ohio State on Senior Night
author: Caroline Riley | The Daily Collegian
description:
Senior Night faced off with Big Ten pressure as Penn State welcomed Ohio State to Rec Hall on Sunday afternoon. A back-and-forth meet with the lead changing every rotation, Ohio State eventually handed the Nittany Lions a 317.950-315.400 loss.

Rotation One

Penn State got off to a rough start in the first rotation, having to count two falls. Brayden Erickson started the rotation with a low 12.600. Kacper Garnczarek was able to get the team back on track with a 13.300 despite some hops on the landing.

However, Shaun Smith was not able to keep the momentum going, also suffering a fall in the third spot for a 12.650. Matt Underhill managed to finish the rotation strong with a rotation-high 13.450, but the damage was done.

Ohio State put together a 13.600, 13.800, 13.300, and a 13.400 for a big 54.100-52.000 lead.

Rotation Two

With fortunes reversed, the Buckeyes suffered two falls on the pommel horse, giving Penn State the chance to take the lead.

Bobby Alessio led off for Penn State with a solid 13.000. He had a lot of flair elements in the second and started to slow down toward the end from fatigue, but managed to get up to the handstand at the end.

Ricky Perez, looking to forget his last performance, nailed his spin on one hand after struggling on it the last two weeks. Despite a little bit of a fight to get to the handstand at the end, he scored a 13.550. A little bit of a fight to get to the handstand. 13.550

The third slot is where the problems began for the Buckeyes as Conlan Simpkins fell.

Garnczarek kept Penn State’s momentum going with a 13.400. He had a little trouble in his circles and was fighting through the routine at the end but hit the dismount.

Despite an incredibly high start value, Ohio State’s star Parker Thackston suffered two falls in the anchor position. For Penn State, Landon Simpson had a hit routine in the anchor position for a 13.500.

Taking advantage of mistakes, the Nittany Lions took a 105.650-104.550 lead.

Rotation Three

Penn State did not hold the lead for very long, suffering a big hit in the opening routine. Smith made a very rare error at the collegiate level, not nailing his handstand perfectly, causing him to fall the wrong direction and come off the rings.

Trying to finish the routine strongly, he suffered another fall on his dismount. With such a low difficulty score, Smith scored just a 10.050. Kellen Ryan got the team back on track with a huge stuck double-double dismount and a 12.700.

Akseli Karsikas was a little high on his Maltese and took a .300 step on the dismount for a 12.600. In the anchor position, needing to get a lot of points back, Matt Underhill delivered. He has a super difficult routine opening with an Azarian to Maltese but nailed it for a big 14.200.

Rotation Four

Penn State opened with two near-sticks from Ryan and Smith for a 13.950 and 14.050.

Ohio State suffered a fall from Chase Davenport-Mills on his Yurchenko 2.5, which hurt the Buckeyes' lead.

After two near sticks, Simpson and Underhill managed to do without the hops for two massive sticks for Penn State. They scored 14.050 and 13.900, respectively, helping the Nittany Lions regain a 211.150-210.450.

Rotation Five

With the lead fluctuating, Penn State had some troubles but also massive success on the parallel bars.

Alessio, looking to forget a fall on his dismount during his last time out, hit a strong routine with a .300 step on the dismount for a 12.600 in the leadoff spot.

Garnczarek struggled with his Makuts and a late right hand. He saved it, but then came off the bars shortly after and took a big step on his dismount for a low 11.400.

Trying to get the team back on track, Simpson put up a massive new career-high 14.150. With difficulty and clean form, he set up Karsikas for a solid 13.400 featuring a stuck double front half dismount.

Despite a strong performance at the end, Ohio State retook the lead 264.250-262.700.

Rotation Six

Ryan opened the rotation off in a huge way. He nailed his Cassina and Kolman as well as stuck his dismount for a solid 13.350.

Garnczarek kept the momentum up, hitting both his laid out Tkatchev and Kolman. He took a small hop on his landing for a 13.000.

Simpson had a great routine on the bar, but put his hands down on his dismount, which is a full point fall. The junior’s mistakes, alongside strong performances from Ohio State, sealed the deal on a loss for Penn State after a 12.450.

However, Karsikas put up a good routine in the anchor position for a 13.900, but it was too late.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball closes out trip to Purdue with first series win
author: Ashley Felsberg I The Daily Collegian
description:
On Sunday, Penn State looked to pick up a win after going winless in the first two games of its series against Purdue.

Prior to this match, Penn State struggled defensively, committing four errors in the second game and losing 2-1. This game offered a chance at a massive turnaround, as Penn State forced Purdue to switch pitchers a total of five times.

Penn State started the game batting against Purdue’s Jarvis Evans. Penn State’s Cohl Mercado got the first go, getting on base after being hit by a pitch.

Shortly after, Penn State hit to center field twice, once by Michael Anderson and once by Jack Porter. These hits sent Mercado home to give Penn State a 1-0 lead heading into the bottom of the first inning.

Purdue started off similarly, with Ali Banks getting hit by Isaiah Shayter’s pitch to get on base first. Following this, Purdue went on a three-run stretch as senior Sam Flores hit a home run, sending three players to score.

Penn State ended the inning on a double play after Jackson Bessette hit to third base. This halted the offensive momentum the Boilermakers were forming, and the inning ended with Purdue up 3-1.

To start the second inning, Evans returned to the mound.

Bryce Molinaro set the tone for the inning, hitting a triple to center field on the first at-bat. Evans continued to struggle for the remainder of the inning, after hitting a batter and then walking the next.

This gave Penn State runners on all bases, despite only having one hit in the inning.

Joey DeMucci hit a triple to center field, and three more runs were brought in for Penn State. Purdue called a timeout to bring in Austin Klug to take over the mound.

Following the switch, the blue and white refused to take their foot off the gas. Maddox McDonald slid a single past Purdue’s shortstop for an RBI. Penn State then led 5-3 and was brought back to the top of the lineup.

This offensive domination continued as Anderson hit a three-run home run, putting Penn State up 8-3.

Purdue was struggling to find the answer to terminating the blue and white’s batting drive. The Boilermarkers opted to change their pitcher for the second time, sending Graham Kollen in to take over the mound.

Despite the change, Purdue continued to dig itself a hole, and Penn State snuck in another run.

Molinaro continued his day of big hits with a home run over the right-center field fence, adding two more runs to the blue and white’s total.

Jacob Boland was subbed in to pitch for Purdue. Up until that moment, each Purdue pitcher failed to record a single out in the inning.

Quickly following the switch, Penn State kept the hits coming and Jayden Davis hit a triple to right field.

After nine consecutive hits, Purdue was finally able to record its first out off a fly-ball to center field. While it allowed Purdue to capture its first out, Davis still scored for Penn State, and the Nittany Lions’ lead extended to 12.

Boland then struck out DeMucci, recording Purdue’s second out and some sense of relief. Quickly after, Flores struck out McDonald to conclude the inning and 11-run stretch.

The Boilermakers were able to get a hit off the first at-bat, but Penn State quickly recovered with three straight outs, and Purdue remained scoreless in the inning.

Likewise, Penn State replicated the offensive drought, and the score remained 12-4 at the bottom of the fourth.

Boland continued pitching for Purdue in the fifth inning. He was a vital component in the halting of Penn State's momentum following their 11-run surge.

Finally, at the bottom of the fifth, Purdue’s Bessette slid a base hit to left field for a two-run double, splitting the Nittany Lions’ lead in half.

Penn State opted to switch out their pitching, sending Mason Butash to take over the mound, for the second switch of the inning. The Boilermakers were able to gather one more run, cutting the lead to five, to slowly chip away at the lead.

This chip-away ended shortly after the scoring of the seventh run, when McDonald grabbed the fly ball to pick up a third out.

As Penn State got to bat in the bottom of the fifth inning, Purdue switched their mound presence, welcoming a fifth pitcher into the game, this time, redshirt sophomore Thomas Howard.

After a quick back-and-forth for the preceding at-bats, the score remained unchanged going into the eighth inning. While both teams were able to get hits off, each one was quickly grounded out, to keep either team from achieving any sort of run stretch.

At the bottom of the eighth, dirt got on home plate for the first time in three innings. Flores hit to right field and sent Quincy Malbrough home to score and minimize the run margin to four.

Penn State decided to sub in sophomore Matthew VanOstenbridge to pitch for the remainder of the inning.

Aaron Manias continued to close the run gap with a hit to left field. While Manias got out, the hit sent Eli Anderson home to score Purdue’s ninth run.

VanOstenbridge was able to strike out Jimmy Dione to wrap up the inning. Purdue’s head coach, Greg Goff, disagreed with the call and was tossed as the game headed into the final inning.

At the top of the ninth, McDonald was able to score after a Porter hit to right field, and Mercado able to score after a Spencer Barnett double, offering two insurance runs to secure the 14-9 win.

Up Next

Penn State will travel back home to take on St. Bonaventure on Tuesday. Following this game, Penn State will play Bucknell on Wednesday and Illinois for a three-game series, all at Medlar Field at Lubrano Park.

title: ‘We’re a blessed program’ | Cael Sanderson reflects on Penn State wrestling’s successful season
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Penn State’s train continues to roll after winning its fifth straight national championship. The Nittany Lions earned the team title with four individual champions, breaking the points record set by last year’s Penn State squad.

All the work done in the offseason, and the dual season leading up to the tournament, culminated in another record-breaking performance for the Nittany Lions, something that has happened three years in a row.

“We work every day,” Cael Sanderson said. “We love what we do. We love each other, our staff. That’s what the kids in the program and the staff work with. That’s what it’s all about, seeing those guys every day is what motivates me, and just continue to learn and grow.”

Penn State once again won the NCAA Championships in dominating fashion, defeating second-place Oklahoma State by 50.5 points. On a technical standpoint, that was an improvement for the runner-up, as Nebraska was 60 points behind the Nittany Lions in 2025.

The wrestlers push for bonus points, even in the national tournament. Across the three days, five Penn Staters were in the top 10 for most points earned for their team. Levi Haines led the way with 26.5 points towards Penn State’s total, with Mitchell Mesenbrink, Josh Barr, Luke Lilledahl and Shayne Van Ness all inside the top 10 for total points scored.

“Our guys, they look to score points, and so they score points,” Sanderson said. “It's really that simple. But yeah, a lot of consistencies. Guys are tough. Good kids work hard.”

The tight-knit aspect of the team has been on full showcase, especially this season. On multiple occasions, the entire team has been on the side of the mat rooting on their teammate, including being matside for a majority of the championship matches at the Big Ten Championships.

That closeness comes from the top, with Sanderson, who’s created a family environment.

“Every one of these kids, it feels like they’re your own son,” Sanderson said. “So when you see him be successful, it’s really awesome. Obviously, when they aren’t as successful, it hurts a lot. But seeing them win the national championship is really cool.”

When some of those wrestlers don’t meet their goals, it becomes tough. For highly regarded freshmen PJ Duke and Marcus Blaze, they had high expectations entering the season, and even with finishing third and fourth, respectively, their own goals were a national title.

However, sometimes with those top wrestlers who are so used to winning all the time, losing is a blessing in disguise. The loss can help the wrestler grow more than a win might because there’s more obvious things to work on.

“It's a process, and sometimes the best thing that can happen to you is you got to learn whether you win or lose. Obviously, tough losses can change your career for the better and help you make those changes that will help you reach your ultimate goal at the highest level,” Sanderson said.

Penn State has won 13 of the last 15 national championships, including the current streak of five straight. The dynasty doesn’t appear to be slowing down anytime soon, with only Haines graduating from the starting lineup.

To Sanderson, the Nittany Lions are doing it right, following the rules, continuing to build the program and continue to keep the dynasty going. While that’s happening internally, other programs around the country may not be doing the same.

“We're a blessed program,” Sanderson said. “We follow the rules, right? There's not a lot of that going on right now, and that's one of the reasons we can sleep when the wind blows. We train hard and do the right things to the best of our ability and things will work out.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse falls to No. 6 Michigan on Senior Day
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coming off its first loss of the season, Penn State looked to get back in the win column in its toughest matchup of the year.

The No. 19 Nittany Lions hosted No. 6 Michigan for Senior Day and Alumni Day in their first game at Panzer Stadium this year, falling 11-6 for their second straight loss.

The Wolverines were first on the scoreboard as Cecei Stein found the back of the net. Less than a minute later, Michigan was lined up on the free position line and scored two straight goals off the foul, taking an early 3-0 lead.

“We have to be a much more disciplined group defensively,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “We got to play the whole game.”

After a pair of shots from the blue and white were unsuccessful, senior Payton Wainman put Penn State on the board with her 29th goal of the year.

Coming out of the media timeout, the Nittany Lions had a man-up situation following a challenged yellow card on Michigan. With the advantage, Kelly MacKinney found the net for her first goal of the game, cutting the deficit to just one goal.

Shortly after coming back to full strength, Penn State found itself with another opportunity to score with a shot on goal, but was ruled no goal despite a Penn State challenge and loud boos.

The Wolverines took the momentum from the no-call and stormed back down the field on offense, earning its third free position shot of the day to take a 4-2 lead after 15 minutes.

The second quarter continued to go Michigan’s way as it earned another goal off a man-up opportunity with senior Erika Ho earning a green card. Penn State’s offense was at a premium for much of the quarter, and the Wolverines capitalized off that with another goal to take a 6-2 lead.

“Truthfully, we had a lot of unforced errors. Dropping the ball, stepping in the crease, going offsides,” Treanor said. “I just think player for player, we didn’t have a great day.”

Over the next several minutes, Michigan continued its domination with three more goals to take a seven-goal lead. However, MacKinney was able to slow the bleeding with her second goal of the game.

Momentum began to swing in Penn State’s favor as it scored its second straight goal, this time by senior Emma Kelly in another man-up situation.

Both sides exchanged a handful of shots to start the second half, including a free position shot from freshman Rocquette Allen, but no one found any success offensively.

After 12 minutes of no scoring from either team, Michigan was back in the scoring column to take a 10-4 lead late in the third quarter.

“One highlight is Abby Rehfuss,” Treanor said. “(She) did an amazing job with the defense making adjustments. They only had two second half goals.”

The Nittany Lions opened the fourth quarter with another possible goal that was ruled invalid due to a last-second shot clock violation. However, the Wolverines came back for their 11th goal of the game with 8:07 left in the fourth quarter.

While Penn State failed to make the comeback, it added another pair of goals in the final minutes by freshman Hannah Hoyt and Wainman, falling to 9-2 on the year.

“Everything we preach is the Penn State standard regardless of the scoreboard, regardless of our opponent,” Treanor said. “Today was a day we didn’t play to our Penn State standard.”

Injury report

After seeing limited action over the past month, Ho returned to the starting lineup on Sunday.

Up next

The Nittany Lions hit the road for a game against No. 3 Maryland at noon on Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball suffers defeat behind late collapse, closing out first Big Ten series against Wisconsin
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After recording its first conference win of the season, Penn State faced Wisconsin in the rubber match on Sunday afternoon. Unlike the first two games of the series, game three was a pitching duel between a pair of right-handers, Abigail Britton and Shelby Jacobson.

The blue and white couldn’t preserve its one-run lead in the seventh and dropped its second straight Big Ten series in a close 7-4 loss to Wisconsin.

The Badgers went down quietly to start the game, but the Nittany Lions applied some early pressure on the basepaths. Second baseman Michela Barbanente drew a leadoff walk, yet she was caught stealing. However, right fielder Breanna Hanik successfully walked and stole second.

Jacobson didn’t crumble under pressure and retired the heart of the blue and white’s lineup to end the first scoreless. While Penn State couldn’t capitalize with a runner in scoring position, Wisconsin plated the first run of the game in the second.

Kendra Lewis knocked a single into right field and Emma King pinch ran and swiped second. A few batters later, Kaylie Whidden sneaked a two-out RBI single through the left side to give the Badgers a 1-0 lead.

While Jacobson cruised through the first few innings, Britton kept the blue and white in the game with another scoreless frame in the third. Penn State’s first hit didn’t come until later in the inning when Hanik sent a dribbler back up to Jacobson on the mound.

Third baseman Brooke Klosowicz had an opportunity to drive in runs with runners on first and second, but grounded out to end the inning. Wisconsin threatened in the fourth after Danielle Lucey sent a leadoff double to left, but Britton swiftly retired the next three Badgers.

Though the Nittany Lions still couldn’t break through in the bottom half of the fourth, Wisconsin added to its lead in the fifth. Jaclyn Showalter launched a solo shot to left center and put the Badgers up 2-0.

However, Penn State finally cracked Jacobson later in the frame and plated four runs. First baseman Gabby Gradishar drew a leadoff walk and then Bridget Nemeth was hit. Barbanente laid down a routine sacrifice bunt, but Showalter at first made a costly error and Gradishar scored.

Klosowicz has been raking on offense lately and that streak continued with a two-run triple to right. Cara Bohner plated Klosowicz from third off the second error of the inning by Showalter and Penn State was suddenly up 4-2 late.

The Badgers got a run back in the sixth. Alivia Bark walked and Kaylie Whidden scored her off a single into left center. After a scoreless bottom half, Nemeth looked to close out the win in the seventh, but the inning unraveled and Wisconsin scored four runs in its last breath.

Showalter led off with a walk and then Hannah Conger tapped a single back up to the pitcher’s mound. With some momentum, Emily Bojan smacked an RBI single into center field and Nemeth also now faced two runners in scoring position after an error by Macy Chamberlin at home.

The score was now knotted at four and coach Clarisa Crowell brought in left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris to stop the bleeding. However, the Badgers took the lead off a fielder’s choice to second. Bark then added on insurance runs with a two-run double to right and extended Wisconsin’s lead, 7-4.

While the blue and white put together a rally and had bases loaded with two outs, Jacobson held Penn State scoreless in the bottom half of the seventh and secured the series win for the Badgers. With the loss, the Nittany Lions fell to 20-11 on the season and 1-5 in Big Ten play.

Up Next

Penn State hits the road and faces Rutgers for a three-game series. The series opener is slated for 6 p.m. Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘It feels amazing’ | Luke Lilledahl’s growth from last season earns NCAA Championship in sophomore year
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — March 18, 2025, Luke Lilledahl sat in front of the local media fresh off a Big Ten title, radiating with confidence. The freshman at the time was excited for the NCAA Championships to see if could prove how much better he was than everyone else.

However, Lilledahl fell short of the goal of being a national champion, finishing in third place. Fast forward one year and the Nittany Lion is now the 2026, 125-pound national champion, maintaining his undefeated record.

“This year for me, obviously wanted to win the nationals, but I wanted to put a stamp on that 125 is my weight class for the next two years, and I feel like I've done that,” Lilledahl said.

The confidence of the St. Charles, Missouri, native never wavered. He entered the 2025-26 season with just as much confidence as he had entering the 2025 NCAA Championships.

It was a mix of two different styles of matches for the wrestler donned the nickname “lightning Luke,” for his fast-paced style of wrestling.

Lilledahl used his fast pace to secure takedowns, oftentimes needing one takedown in the tournament to advance. The growth was showcased when he advanced to the semifinals, defeating Iowa’s Dean Peterson in tiebreakers in the quarterfinals.

That spot is where Lilledahl fell to Lehigh’s Sheldon Seymour in the 2025 NCAA Championships.

“Just having the ability to stay patient and stay poised in those tight matches is a big skill, and I think I've done a better job at that this year than last year,” Lilledahl said. “So that was one of the things that going into this year, I knew I was going to have to maybe sharpen a bit, and I did that.”

Lilledahl utilized that patience in a 2-1 championship victory over Princeton’s Marc-Anthony McGowan. He never overattacked and let the points come to him, similar to how he did the first time around with McGowan during the regular season in Rec Hall.

The patience was something that was worked on in the offseason. Lilledahl was a wrestler last season that was full throttle, pedal to the medal with his lightning-quick attacks. That came back to bite him, but this season was different, showcasing a clear sign of growth not only physically on the mat, but mentally as well.

“Hopefully this is a stepping stone to what I'm going to do in the future,” Lilledahl said. “So at the same time, it's nice to win, but I'm already thinking about freestyle and thinking about next year.”

Lilledahl became the first USA wrestler to win a U17, U20 and U23 gold medal at the World Championships in the summer of 2025, and will have opportunities to earn gold medals once again.

While that is around the corner, Lilledahl’s stepping stone from the summer was to stand at the top of the podium. As he received his trophy, with the gold medal plate on it, he held up the trophy and had a big smile on his face, showcasing what that moment truly meant.

“It feels amazing,” Lilledahl said. "It kind of solidifies that I've been doing everything right and having trusted my coaches and trusted my training.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling's PJ Duke earns 2026 NCAA Championships Gorriaran Award
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — PJ Duke brings home hardware.

Duke earns the 2026 NCAA Championships Gorriaran Award for most falls in the least amount of time.

https://x.com/pennstatewrest/status/2035540724616175827?s=46&t=nhrOgcJkOmitItha4sE_qA

Duke earned the award for two falls in the opening two rounds in 1:56 and 1:38, for an average of 1:47 in the two matches.

The true freshman ended his first year with the Nittany Lions with a third-place finish, with two falls, two tech falls and a decision.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling's Mitchell Mesenbrink earns pair of accolades after winning national title
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Mitchell Mesenbrink keeps bringing in accolades.

The 165-pound wrestler was named the Most Outstanding Wrestler of the Tournament and Most Dominant Wrestler for the 2026 NCAA Championships.

https://x.com/pennstateWREST/status/2035541305741169106

https://x.com/pennstateWREST/status/2035539255393435963

Mesenbrink cruised to his second individual title, earning three tech falls, a major decision and a decision. The most commanding of the three was his championship tech fall over Iowa's Mikey Caliendo, defeating him 20-4 in 5:13.

Mesenbrink is one of the Hodge Trophy favorites, which is given to the best wrestler in the country, and likely be in competition with Ohio State's 141-pounder Jesse Mendez, who lost his title match to Oklahoma State's Sergio Vega.

Mesenbrink went 27-0 in 2025-26, with a 96% bonus rate, dominating the field en route to the title.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Luke Lilledahl earns Penn State's final national title win of NCAA Championships
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Behind the curtain of VOM 4, one of the entrances for the wrestlers at Rocket Arena, Luke Lilledahl sat on a chair next to his coach Cael Sanderson, AirPods in his ears as the heavyweight bout rolled on inside the bowl.

As the preceding match came to an end, the 125-pounder rose to his feet and shook out before heading to the mat. The last face he saw before his music hit — Josh Barr carrying his national championship trophy.

He went on to earn one of his own, defeating No. 10 Marc Anthony-McGowan of Princenton, who he stalled out in the regular season, by a score of 2-1.

McGowan started the match using the same strategy as he did in the dual, backing off of Lilledahl throughout the first period and riding his knee for an extended portion of Period 1.

Lilledahl began on the top for Period 2, riding McGowan for 15 seconds before conceding the escape point. McGowan received his first stall warning a minute later while leading 1-0. The stalemate continued through two resets and time expired on the frame.

Lilledahl stood up five seconds into Period 3, knotting the score at 1-1 for the final two minutes of regulation. Forty seconds into the scuffle, McGowan was charged with a second stall call, giving Lilledahl the lead, 2-1. The crowd erupted in a chorus of boos, but McGowan still abstained from attacking until the clock hit 30 seconds.

Lilledahl held onto the lead to win Penn State’s fourth national championship of the night and the first of his career.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling breaks their own record again for most team points in NCAA Championships
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Penn State has broken another record.

The 2025-26 Nittany Lions now hold the spot for the most team points at the NCAA Championships of all-time with 177.5 points following five titles bouts.

Penn State previously broke its own record last season with 177 last season, and set the record the season prior with 172.5.

Following Luke Lilledahl clinching the national title, the Nittany Lions' record sits at 181.5
