title: Letter from the Editor: How we’re navigating AI in the Collegian newsroom
author: Mercedes Hamilton | Editor-in-Chief
description:
Our AI policy, effective Jan. 27, 2026 and updated March 20, 2026, is embedded at the bottom of this letter. It’s a living document that will evolve as we continue to discuss, evaluate and research AI’s role in our newsroom. We would greatly appreciate it if you fill out this form and provide us with feedback.

Generative artificial intelligence has worked its way into every industry, and journalism is no exception.

The Daily Collegian instated its first AI policy during the 2024-25 school year, written by former editor-in-chief, and current business manager, Amy Schafer. The five-clause policy outlined acceptable uses: generating ideas for headlines, interview questions, promotional materials, and AI‑generated alt text. We prohibit using AI to write an article or generate visual content.

Schafer’s early action anticipated incoming challenges and set a solid foundation for the outlet.

Following suspected cases of AI misuse and an increase in curiosity from some of our staffers this academic year, I set out to create something more robust.

In creating a new policy, our priorities continue to be providing our readers with ethical, accurate, human-centered news produced by people — and being transparent about how we gather information and create content.

At the core of this initiative was intense research. I looked for the policies of a number of student publications and connected with some other Big Ten and state schools to see how they were approaching this unprecedented time. Very few had publicly available policies, and others had private ones they were willing to share. Many either appeared to have no policy at all or told me directly they had nothing in place.

I then moved on to some professional publications. I read through how the New York Times approaches AI, including principles for using generative tools and the AI section of its ethical journalism handbook. I’m also part of the 2024 Times Corps class, a mentorship program for early-career journalists, where Associate Editorial Director of AI Initiatives Rubina‬‭ Fillion taught us how to use the technology transparently, effectively and responsibly, primarily for investigative work.

I also consulted Newsday’s AI committee, where I interned this summer, to learn more about their approach and received helpful insight on maintaining our ethical standards and protecting the privacy of our newsroom and sources.

My primary resource was the Poynter Institute, which provides training programs and resources on the technology, many of which we’ve added to the newsroom’s digital “AI library” for staff. To create our policy, I used its template for newsroom generative AI policy.

I didn’t want to be the only person making the call. We all have very different viewpoints on the technology, and it may show up differently in all of our roles.

Joining me on the The Daily Collegian’s very first AI committee is: Managing Editor and 2026-27 EIC-elect Kahlie Wray; Digital Managing Editor Avery Totora; News Editor Mia Debelevich; Sports Editors Andrew Deal; Multimedia Editors Zeyda Frye; Lifestyle Editor Nadya Solometo; Videographer Jailyn Sharpe; Photographer Aayan Hamdani; Data Reporter Tyler Nolt; DEI Chair and Social Media Content Creator Victoria Pugh and Crime and Courts Reporter Taylor McGillvray.

The committee’s primary goals include monitoring content management systems for updates that may include AI tools, writing clear guidance about how you will or will not use AI in content-generation and gathering insight about their needs and concerns. Our next initiative is training editors to detect AI misuse.

Before creating any policy, our committee had an open conversation about the ways in which people may be misusing AI, what we’ve seen professional newsrooms use it for, where they believe it could be useful in their work and our non-negotiables.

We still strictly prohibit the use of AI to write articles or generate images. Anyone found doing so will be dismissed immediately.

To protect the privacy of our sources, we strictly prohibit feeding interview transcripts into an AI platform.

Sometimes things slip through the cracks. On a few occasions, we discovered after publication that a writer used AI to write their story. These reporters are no longer at the Collegian.

Previously, our policy has been to remove the content from our website. Now, content suspected to have been created through the unauthorized use of AI will be re-fact checked by two editors and the editor-in-chief, and an editor’s note will be added to the top notifying readers.

Our new policy also expands our usage guidelines to allow staff members to use AI to search and assemble data, sort through and organize large amounts of public records and use Adobe features like Denoise. Staff members must first submit a request, explaining how they plan to use AI and have it cleared by the committee before use.

Prior to publication, editors will review prompts and any other inputs used. The reporter, the editor-in-chief, the managing editor and the two section editors will all reproduce the output to verify its accuracy.

Our policy asks that staff members don’t use AI to perform tasks easily performed by oneself, such as generating interview questions and headlines, as the technology is not human and cannot replicate human conversation, judgment or nuance. We additionally want to avoid exacerbating its environmental impacts, ensure our stories don’t reflect systemic biases and prevent our team from developing essential journalistic skills.

Our hope is that this policy can prepare student journalists at the Collegian to adapt to industry changes and make lengthy tasks, such as analyzing hefty data sets or court documents, quicker. Tasks completed with AI will always be disclosed to readers with details on their use and our rigorous fact checking process.

These decisions have been made in alignment with industry changes, professional insight and staff feedback.

Delivering accurate, ethical news and maintaining readership trust remains at the core of our mission. In addition to filling out the form in our editor’s note, I invite you to share your questions and concerns with me directly at editorinchief@psucollegian.com. We appreciate your support.

title: Penn State wrestling’s Josh Barr cruises to first national title, beats Oklahoma State’s Cody Merrill
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Top-seed Josh Barr looked for title-match revenge after falling short in last year’s 197-pound finals match. Barr took on No. 7 Cody Merrill of Oklahoma State and the Nittany Lion avenged his loss last year with a 6-3 decision over Merrill.

With the victory, Barr finished with a 24-0 record, earning his first national title of his career.

Barr and Merrill met for the first time in their collegiate careers. The match also featured Cael Sanderson against his former assistant coach David Taylor, who is the head coach of the Cowboys, battling in each corner.

With time winding down in the first period, Barr got through on Merrill and took him down for the first points of the match. Merrill escaped with three seconds remaining, making the score 3-1 in favor of Barr.

Neither wrestler did much in the second period, with Merrill earning the lone point on an escape. Barr had the chance to do the same in the third period, but instead reversed Merrill. The reversal also gave Barr an extra point from an illegal hold, giving the Nittany Lion a 6-2 lead.

Merrill escaped the hold of Barr and went on the attack, but Barr fended off every shot, backing himself away from any attack. The Davison, Michigan, native cruised to a 6-3 victory earning his first national title.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball adjusts Sunday first pitch time
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the second time this weekend, Penn State has changed its game start time due to inclement weather. On Friday, there was a delay, but Sunday's game is being moved up two hours, now starting at 11 a.m.

Due to forecasted inclement weather on Sunday in Central Pennsylvania, our weekend finale against Wisconsin has been moved up to 11 a.m. (ET) on Sunday, March 22.Can’t wait to see everyone back at Beard for our Cancer Awareness game! Remember, admission is 🆓#WeAre | #AllIn pic.twitter.com/ug4vujhDOL— Penn State Softball (@PennStateSB) March 22, 2026

The Nittany Lions' Game 3 against Wisconsin marks the rubber match of the series. The blue and white were victorious over the Badgers on Saturday with a 14-8 win.

Penn State will look to close out its first home series on a high note, while also avoiding potential storms.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Rocco Welsh loses to Minnesota’s Max McEnelly in championship bout
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — No. 1-seed Rocco Welsh entered the mat for the national championship for the first time since he lost to Carter Starocci in the 2024 174-pound title match. Welsh took on No. 3 Max McEnelly and fell to the Gopher by a 4-3 decision in the 184-pound title match.

With the loss, Welsh dropped to 24-1 and finished with his second runner-up status of his career. The two wrestlers faced off two weeks prior, ending in controversial fashion, with Welsh winning in tiebreakers at the Big Ten Championships, but this time it was McEnelly who had the upperhand.

Around two minutes into the match, McEnelly got a single leg on Welsh and finished through it to secure a takedown. Moments later, Welsh escaped and ended the period with a 3-1 score.

To begin the second period, McEnelly escaped from bottom position to extend his lead, but neither wrestler took competitive shots the rest of the period.

Trailing 4-1, Welsh escaped to make it a two-point match and looked to go on the attack to take the lead. With each attack came a strong defense from McEnelly.

With 20 seconds remaining, Welsh turned the offense up to full throttle, but with minimal time left, McEnelly fended off the pursuit and earned the title with a 4-3 decision.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State track and field opens outdoor season with Penn Challenge
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
While the indoor season wrapped up just last weekend with the NCAA championship, Penn State is back for the outdoor season.

The Nittany Lions kicked off the season with the Penn Challenge on Saturday, starting the year with a strong start.

Sophomore multi-event athlete Angelina Massey got things started in the women’s 100-meter hurdles, where she posted her first collegiate time of 16.10 seconds in the event.

The field portion of the meet kicked off with the men’s discus throw, where the blue and white took second and third. Graduate student Sam Engebretson led the way with a throw of 51.26 meters, followed by sophomore Matthew Sarnowski with 50.06 meters.

The hammer throw saw even greater success as three Nittany Lions finished in the top five, including Collin Burkhart’s second-place finish with 66.17 meters — the fifth-best mark in Penn State history. Engebretson finished third in 57.77 meters and Sarnowski finished fifth in 53.03 meters.

Similar things happened in the women’s hammer throw, with senior Alex Pancoast throwing 54.66 meters for third place while Gabi Deglau finished fifth in 53.50 meters.

Senior Gwen Cudmore earned the first victory for Penn State in her final collegiate opening meet, jumping 1.71 meters in the women’s high jump. Hannah Riolo finished second in 1.66 meters, followed by Jenna Young in fourth with the same height.

The blue and white had top finishes in both the men’s and women’s javelin, as freshman Nolan Carey finished second in the men’s with a throw of 65.10 meters, with senior Audrey Friedman throwing 43.15 meters in the women’s.

Junior Joe White earned the third and final win for the Nittany Lions in the men’s long jump, jumping 6.98 meters.

Sophomore Brady Mider wrapped up the day in the men’s shot put with a personal best throw of 17.25 meters for second place.

Up next

The Nittany Lions continue their outdoor season with the Raleigh Relays and Knights Invite next weekend.

MORE TRACK COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Levi Haines wins national championship at 174 pounds
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Levi Haines ended his Penn State career on top as a national champion at two weight classes, an undefeated record and his third victory this season over No. 2 Christopher Minto of Nebraska, beating him 2-1 by decision.

Both wrestlers started off stagnant, but Minto was called for an early stalling. There was a lot of hand fighting between the two, and a few takedown attempts by Haines, but neither wrestler earned points in the first period.

Minto began the second period on top, resulting in Haines escaping quickly. Minto was then called for stalling, which gave Haines a point, so the senior took a 2-0 lead. Even with a 2-0 lead Haines wasn’t that active either, but he was approaching Minto more.

Minto escaped quickly to cut the lead to 2-1 to start the third period. The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native got a hold of Minto’s leg, and couldn’t get much more as the match continued to be at a slow pace.

Minto attacked Haines three times and Haines got called for stalling, but it was too late and Haines got the 2-1 decision victory.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Mitchell Mesenbrink becomes back-to-back national champion at 165 pounds
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Mitchell Mesenbrink hopped, skipped and jumped his way onto the mat to defend his national championship in a rematch of last year’s final against No. 3 Mikey Caliendo of Iowa.

The result remained the same as Mesenbrink got the better of Caliendo, beating him for the ninth time in his career, this time by a score of 20-4 tech-fall victory.

In the opening 15 seconds, Mesenbrink secured the first takedown of the match. Fifteen seconds later, Mesenbrink got another takedown and held a 6-2 lead. The junior continued to be the aggressor with four takedowns in the first two minutes of the match.

Caliendo had no answer and struggled to stand upright on the mat. To end the first period, the Hartland, Wisconsin, native led 12-4.

Caliendo had an opportunity to get Mesenbrink, but couldn’t prevail and it turned into Mesenbrink’s fifth takedown of the match. Caliendo got called with stalling twice and gave Mesenbrink an 18-4 lead.

The junior got the reversal to take a 20-4 lead and a tech-fall victory to win his second national championship.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling's Shayne Van Ness loses 149-pound title match at NCAA Championships
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Top-seeded Shayne Van Ness slowly sauntered to the mat in Rocket Arena for the first national championship match of his career, to face No. 10 Aden Valencia of Stanford, but couldn’t overcome the Cardinal in an extra period of wrestling.

Both 149-pounders were aggressive to start the bout, and after a series of rolls, Valencia recorded the first takedown by cradling Van Ness. After Van Ness spun out of the circle, a blood-time stoppage saw a challenge for near fall points by the Stanford corner.

The challenge was unsuccessful and Van Ness escaped, but was hit with a stalling after Valencia continued to attack. Van Ness nearly scored his first takedown near the end of Period 1, but Valencia continued to avoid being taken off his feet, holding a 3-1 advantage.

Valencia escaped to begin Period 2, to which Van Ness responded by throwing him onto his back, but no takedown was awarded before Valencia returned the favor and a stalemate was called while Van Ness was avoiding the seat bet. After the reset, Van Ness secured his first takedown of the match to knot the score at 4-4.

Valencia took the 5-4 lead with an escape before a fourth-blood stoppage was called. Van Ness tackled Valencia again with 16 seconds left, but the Cardinal grabbed his leg and held on as time expired on Period 2 to avoid the takedown.

Van Ness drew even at 5-5 by escaping Valencia seconds into Period 3. Valencia picked Van Ness’ leg in the first attack of the period, to which Van Ness locked his arms around Valencia, leading to a stalemate with 48 seconds on the clock. Valencia sent another shot attempt with 10 seconds remaining, leading to a stalemate and sudden-victory overtime.

Before the period could begin, Van Ness got sent to the corner for blood again, prompting boos from a hyped-up Cleveland crowd. Valencia shot onto Van Ness, lifted him over his head and secured both legs to get awarded a takedown.

Penn State challenged the call and after review, the takedown was confirmed. Valencia defeated Van Ness 8-5 to win his first national championship.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: 'We’ve been very blessed' | Record-setting Panzer Stadium crowd fuels No. 9 Penn State’s White Out win
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Panzer Stadium was packed on Saturday afternoon as the No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse team hosted the previous year’s national runner-ups in the Big Ten opener. Coincidentally, the game was also the season’s official “White Out”, Penn State’s signature event where fans all dress in a sea of white clothes.

The noise level in the outdoor stadium was deafening as the Penn State faithful cheered the home team on against No. 11 Maryland, a program that the blue and white had never beaten at Panzer Stadium.

That is, until the record-setting 2,532 screaming fans helped propel the Nittany Lions to a 10-6 victory over the Terrapins, the fifth victory all-time against Maryland in program history.

Both spectator areas — including the main bleachers underneath the press box and the opposite-side stands right behind the Maryland bench — were full. In addition, the hill behind the scoreboard saw many young lacrosse fans holding sticks of their own, passing and practicing their craft.

“Since 2019, when Panzer Stadium was constructed, we’ve been very blessed,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “And grateful for the support that we have.”

So far, the entire weekend has been historic. The wrestling team captured its fifth-consecutive national championship, the women’s ice hockey team hosted the Frozen Four across the street from the men’s lacrosse team, which drew the largest crowd in Panzer Stadium history.

Lacrosse, the premier event of the day, coupled with gorgeous temperate weather, stole the show to kick off the two-day break from classes.

“A beautiful day,” Tambroni said. “We get little sunshine out here in State College in the spring; gotta take advantage of it. The players were excited with the energy, the student section behind us, so it made a significant difference.”

Penn State’s “White Out” game is known across all sports for being the loudest game of a team’s season. Down the road at Beaver Stadium, it’s known as one of the most difficult environments to play in as the visiting football team.

“It was whited out everywhere,” Hawkins said. “It was so cool. The crowd was there all day. They helped our team. It fueled our win.”

The Panzer Stadium crowd didn’t hesitate in its spirit after every Nittany Lion goal, Preston Hawkins save or Maryland turnover.

This included chanting.

At the end of the third quarter, Hawkins — Penn State’s true-freshman starting goaltender — made a save on Eric Spanos’ point-blank shot on the doorstep to maintain the Nittany Lions’ three-goal lead heading into the fourth quarter. Panzer Stadium erupted. Fans began a symphony, chanting the young netminder’s name.

And that wasn’t the only instance. Midway through the fourth quarter, Hawkins continuously came up big for his team and yet again heard his name play on repeat amongst the spectators.

“It was definitely super cool,” Hawkins said. “You know, you're still mid-game, so you’ve still got to try and tune it out, but it was a good feeling. I definitely heard it.”

Penn State added yet another keystone victory this season, now having defeated both teams that played in the 2025 National Championship at Gillette Stadium. The Nittany Lions, backed by the support of thousands, successfully avenged their Big Ten semifinal loss to the Terrapins and their Final Four defeat to Cornell.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: ‘I’m out there for a reason’ | Hawkins’ career day fuels victory over No. 11 Maryland
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
On February 1, coach Jeff Tambroni pulled goaltender Preston Hawkins out of Penn State’s season-opener against Colgate after the freshman allowed four goals in the opening moments of his collegiate debut.

Fast forward to Saturday, and Hawkins’ name was being chanted by a record-breaking crowd at Panzer Stadium during the No. 9 Nittany Lions’ win over No. 11 Maryland.

“Watching him out there today says a lot about his resolve,” Tambroni said. “That’s not something that comes easy to a freshman. He’s only been here for eight games, and he continues to get better every day, not just every game.”

The Powell, Ohio, native made a career-high 14 saves in the victory over Maryland, powering his defense to a season-low six goals allowed.

“Building the trust of my teammates and coaches,” Hawkins said, “knowing that I’m out there for a reason and that they believe in me, it helps me play with that confidence.”

Hawkins started the game off with two saves in the first quarter, while also allowing two goals in the frame. A huge second quarter saw him keep five Terrapin shots out of the net, allowing just a single goal to bring the matchup into halftime with the Nittany Lions holding a 7-3 advantage.

The third quarter was when the day really started to get special for Hawkins.

Penn State couldn’t find the back of the net in the frame, but it would come up with two huge saves to keep the blue and white ahead throughout. After one of his biggest of the game came with just seven seconds until the quarter break, the chants began.

“It was super cool,” Hawkins said. “You’re still mid-game, so you’ve still got to try and tune it out, but it was a good feeling.”

2,532 fans, a record at Panzer Stadium, made up an obstreperous crowd that was behind the first-year goaltender all the way. Early in the fourth quarter, Hawkins made his 11th save of the day to tie his career high set in an early-season win over Princeton.

“I think it really did help the guys,” Tambroni said. “They were excited with the energy, with the student section behind us and the crowd (as a whole.) It made a significant difference.”

The chants returned with six minutes remaining in the contest, after Hawkins came up with a huge stop that sparked a Nittany Lion fastbreak. Just seconds after the save, Michael Faraone found himself open on the crease and dunked on the Terrapin goaltender to the delight of the thousands of white-wearing spectators in Panzer.

“It was white out everywhere,” Hawkins said, “it was so cool. The crowd was there all day. They fueled us to our win.”

While the final seconds bled off the clock, Maryland held possession in Penn State’s defensive zone. A Terrapin player wound up and fired the ball into the net, but not before the buzzer sounded, securing the win for the blue and white.

As the shot hit the nylon, Hawkins extended his arms above his head to declare his opinion that the shot wasn’t going to count, mirroring the referee who did the same just feet away from him.

The Penn State bench overflowed onto the field and engulfed Hawkins, as if he’d just scored a game-winning goal.

“They get me really pumped up,” Hawkins said, “they keep me going.”

Hawkins’ collegiate debut didn’t end how he imagined, but that ending was juxtaposed in his Big Ten opener: instead embraced by his team after playing the key role in the program’s first-ever win at home against Maryland.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse outlasts No. 11 Maryland’s second-half surge for Big Ten Opener win
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse drew a Panzer Stadium record-setting 2,532 spectators for the Nittany Lions’ Big Ten Opener and White Out game against No. 11 Maryland at 3 p.m. on Saturday.

In a battle between two of the four teams in the 2025 Final Four, the blue and white outlasted a second-half comeback bid to open Big Ten play with a 10-6 victory. It was Penn State’s first victory over Maryland at Panzer Stadium and fifth all-time.

“Since 2019, when Panzer Stadium was constructed, we’ve been very blessed,” coach Jeff Tambroni said. “And grateful for the support that we have. The players were excited with the energy, the student section behind us, so it made a significant difference.”

First half

Penn State kicked off the game with a flurry of offense, dominating possession time that led to the ball often firing towards goaltender Brian Ruppel. After Ruppel made three consecutive saves, Chase Robertson and John Jude Considine struck the back of the net to get the White Out Panzer Stadium crowd on their feet.

Maryland answered back on the man-advantage — as Peri DiBartolomeo took a technical foul — giving the ball to Braden Erksa, who sniped short-side past goaltender Preston Hawkins to cut Penn State’s lead to one. It was later followed by Leo Johnson to tie the game.

After the short hiccup, Hawkins heated up. Throughout the first half, he made seven saves on 10 shots on goal to aid Penn State in widening its gap. Hawkins made a career-high 14 saves on 20 shots for a .700 save percentage against the previous national runner-ups. He improved to 4-3 on the season.

“Building the trust of my teammates and my coaches, just knowing that I'm out there for a reason and that they believe in me, so it helps to play with that confidence,” Hawkins said. “And then the guys in front of me are outstanding, so it helps me do my job a lot easier.”

Mark Watters and Robertson regained the two-goal lead, with the latter’s goal aided by Kyle Lehman’s relentless pressing of Maryland’s Peter Laake.

Jack Iannantuono scored his first goal of the game with 8.1 seconds remaining, weaving between a cluster of Terrapin defenders to beat Ruppel for a 5-2 lead.

Mac Hobbs tallied the first goal of his season less than three minutes into the second quarter, scoring while falling. Luke Walstrum increased the lead to five after Michael Faraone found him streaking unguarded on the doorstep.

After the scoring pace noticeably slowed due to Hawkins’ and Ruppel’s goaltending, Maryland scored its third goal of the game with 1:26 on the clock. Long-stick midfielder AJ Larkin scored from distance in transition for a 7-3 score at the end of the half. However, the momentum swung back in Penn State’s favor as Hawkins made a save on Eric Spanos’ shot with three seconds remaining.

“Thinking about the way he started the season against Colgate, getting pulled after about 17 minutes, and watching him out here today says a lot about his resolve,” Tambroni said. “And that's not something that comes easy to a freshman.”

Second half

Maryland started the second half with vengeance as Chris Lyons scored with 10:29 on the clock. Tambroni challenged the goal for Lyons stepping in the crease, but the call on the field stood and the Terrapins cut Penn State’s lead to three.

Hawkins continued to shine at the end of the third quarter, making a save on a point-blank bounce shot after two Penn State defenders dove and missed as Spanos dodged his way into position.

Although the Nittany Lions were held scoreless in the third quarter, they weren’t without chances. Lehman had two opportunities to contribute to the scoreboard, but an interior pass after Maryland’s failed clearance was intercepted in front of the crease. His next shot struck the iron.

“I think the mindset of our offense shifted and I thought in that first half, they were really aggressive, they were bold with their decisions, playing with a great deal of confidence,” Tambroni said. “Thankfully, Preston and the defense did a good job holding them off until we could throw a couple in the net there in the fourth quarter.”

Zach Whittier cut the lead to two early in the fourth quarter and the Terrapins immediately pressed on the gas. Hawkins made his 11th save with his body on Whittier’s next shot.

In crunch time, the teams traded goals to set the score at 8-6 with just over six minutes remaining in the game. Larkin cut the lead to one before Hobbs shot from mid-range for his second of the game to regain the cushion.

Walstrum found Faraone on the crease to give Penn State a three-goal lead with 5:14 remaining in the game. Lehman found the cage at last with 3:55 left to play, diving into the crease while shooting top corner to close the game with a 10-6 win.

Injury Report

Attackman Hunter Aquino — the reigning Big Ten Freshman of the Year — wasn’t dressed due to an undisclosed injury, per Tambroni.

Up Next

Penn State is headed on a two-game Big Ten road trip, first taking on No. 7 Ohio State on Saturday at 4 p.m. in Columbus, Ohio.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball earns first Big Ten win, defeats Wisconsin
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a close 6-5 loss in its home opener, Penn State looked to snap its four-game losing streak and record its first Big Ten win of the season on Saturday afternoon. Unlike the first game of the series, the Nittany Lions ran away with the game early and poured on the runs.

Penn State’s offense caught fire and improved to 20-10 on the season with its first Big Ten win, 14-8 over Wisconsin.

Junior Bridget Nemeth bounced back after her rocky previous start against Oregon and delivered a solid performance. Emily Bojan led off with a double to left center, but the blue and white should’ve escaped the first with no damage. However, first baseman Gabby Gradishar committed an error and the Badgers plated the first run.

Yet Wisconsin didn’t hold a lead for long. In the bottom half, second baseman Michela Barbanente reached on an error by shortstop Hannah Conger. Right fielder Breanna Hanik laid down a bunt down the third base line and all the runners were safe.

The hottest bat of Penn State’s lineup currently is third baseman Brooke Klosowicz and the junior came through once again. The Johnsburg, Illinois, native lined a single into left-center and tied the game.

Star freshman Allison Oneacre then launched a double to right center and plated Hanik to give the Nittany Lions a 2-1 lead. Center fielder Natalie Lieto drew a walk and then Nemeth helped herself in the batter’s box. The Glendora, California, native knotted a single to center and the bases were loaded with just one out.

Designated player Jordan George cleared the bases and smashed her first home run as a Nittany Lion over the center field wall. Right-hander Kendra Lewis was pulled early and finished after recording just one out and was responsible for four earned runs.

Nemeth retired the badgers in order and Penn State added another run onto its 6-1 lead in the second. Hanik, Klosowicz and Oneacre knocked three singles together and the bases were loaded for Lieto with just one out. The sophomore scored a run from third off a groundout to first.

Bar a single, Nemeth cruised through the Badgers again in the third, while the blue and white plated three more runs in the inning. George and catcher Madison Seidel both singled and the Nittany Lions scored a run from third off a wild pitch.

Barbanente drew a walk and left-hander Jordan Felci made a costly error on Hanik’s routine sacrifice bunt. Both runners scored on the play and Penn State was up 10-1 after just three frames.

After a few quiet offensive innings, the Badgers plated four in the fourth. Danielle Lucey powered a double to left center and Jaclyn Showalter brought her home off a single to the left side. Right-hander McKenna Young came in for relief, but the scoring continued.

Wisconsin had runners in scoring position a few batters later and Bojan delivered with a two-run single to left, but Penn State still held a five-run lead. Penn State held the Badgers scoreless in the top of the fifth and added onto its lead in the bottom half.

Hanik lifted a solo dinger to left and extended the Nittany Lions’ lead, 11-5. However, Wisconsin plated three runs on left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris in the sixth.

Kaylie Whidden walked then Bojan reached base off an error at shortstop. With two runners on, Addison Blomberg came through with a two-run double to center. The scoring continued as Alivia Bark ripped a double down the left field line, bringing in another run and making the game closer at 11-8.

However, George responded right back in with her second home run of the day, deep into center. The power continued in the frame with Seidel’s double to center and Hanik smashed the second dinger of the inning to center.

Right-hander Abigail Britton closed out the game in the seventh and secured Penn State’s first conference victory of the campaign.

Up Next

Penn State wraps up its series against Wisconsin at 1 p.m. Sunday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s lacrosse sets Panzer Stadium attendance record vs Maryland
author: Elliott Inscho | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 9 Penn State men’s lacrosse set a new attendance record on Saturday at Panzer Stadium.

2,532 fans came through the gates to witness the Nittany Lions’ matchup with No. 11 Maryland on Saturday.

You brought it today, Nittany Nation 🙌 pic.twitter.com/AF4FeFGVWJ— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) March 21, 2026

The previous Panzer Stadium record stood at 2,301 when the blue and white took down then No. 7 Ohio State in 2019.

Saturday’s mark is the most in stadium history, but not the most ever for a program's home game. 3,208 fans attended a game in 2017 at the Penn State Lacrosse Field against Rutgers.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball drops second game against Purdue
author: Dominic Megats I The Daily Collegian
description:
After opening its second Big Ten series of the season with a loss to Purdue, Penn State looked to rebound and add its second win to the conference record.

In what was a tightly contested game, the blue and white were only able to get one run across the plate, leading to a 2-1 Boilermakers win.

The Nittany Lions were sent down in order to open the game, turning the ball over to the righty Ben Hudson. The Medford, New Jersey, native has struggled in his last two games, giving up a total of 18 runs. However, Hudson was able to get out of the first inning unscathed, striking out the last batter.

Following a duel between both pitchers in the first two innings, the blue and white earned the first run of the game off a wild pitch. Boilermakers starting pitcher Zach Erdman threw a pitch in the dirt that was too fast for catcher Ross Highfill to block.

Hudson quickly found himself in trouble with runners at the corners and no outs in the bottom half of the third inning. However, a flyout and a quick double play eliminated any threat to the scoreboard.

Both defenses continued to shine through five innings. The two sides totaled four double plays up to that point, shutting down several scoring chances.

That was until Purdue used the bottom of the fifth to get a run on the board. Dylan Drake doubled in a run after he powered the ball to the center field wall. Hudson managed to escape the inning without allowing any additional damage.

Hudson’s outing came to an end in the sixth. The sophomore finished the day with three strikeouts and allowed one run.

Kyle Emmons took over and gave up a home run to his first batter. Ali Banks cleared the wall in left-center field, giving the Boilermakers a 2-1 lead. The Pasadena, Maryland, native responded to the solo shot with a pair of strikeouts to end the sixth.

Both defenses took the spotlight once again in the game through the seventh and eighth innings, leaving Penn State down to its last three outs.

The Nittany Lions were sent down in order to close out the game. A strong display of defense wasn’t matched with much offense for Penn State.

Up Next

Both teams will return to Alexander Field for the series finale tomorrow at 1 p.m.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Rocco Welsh feeling more mature, grown up entering another go at title match
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Two years ago at Ohio State, Rocco Welsh entered the 2024 National Championships as a No. 6 seed after going 26-5 in the Big Ten.

Despite his young status and the lower ranking, Welsh went on a march through the tournament that saw him upset former national champion Ed Ruth, while working through a bracket torn apart by upsets. Welsh faced the No. 9 in the finals, but unfortunately for him that 9th seed was Carter Starocci, who defeated Welsh in the bout.

“My freshman year was a freaking movie,” Welsh said. “That bracket was loaded and I didn’t really have any expectations.”

This year, Rocco Welsh directed his own path back to the national final as the No. 1 seed. While the outcome has been the same so far, this time Welsh is known as the top dog and has the expectation of taking home the national title tomorrow night.

“I was kind of starstruck (in 2024),” Welsh said. “Now, I’m just really confident.”

Despite the first-year success, Welsh spent his sophomore year redshirting in the Ohio State wrestling room. Upon leaving the program and transferring to Penn State, Welsh had jumped up from 174 to 184 and found immediate success, but he didn't harbor ill will towards his former program.

“Last year was pretty tough sitting out and watching everyone,” Welsh said. “But it was God telling me to wait and he had something bigger for me.”

That plan had him go undefeated in his first regular season in the blue and white before winning a Big Ten championship and being ranked as the top 184-pounder entering the NCAA Championships.

Training in the Penn State wrestling room has been key to Welsh’s jump and his preparedness coming into a title bout for the second time. Welsh believes the wrestlers around the Lorenzo Wrestling Center have set him apart from his 32 competitors this weekend.

“I mean, we have the best partners in the world, Olympic champs, Olympic medalists, NCAA champions and obviously the best coaches,” Welsh said. “None of these other teams are doing that. They may have good partners, just not like us.”

Welsh’s first two bouts showed his separation from the class as he secured bonus points in both of the first two rounds, while the following two were lower scoring Big Ten rematches. Despite the victories, Welsh has more he’d like to improve on entering another rematch with Minnesota’s Max McEnelly.

“I need to open up a little bit,” Welsh said. “I gotta be confident and trust in God’s plan.”

As he’s gone through the tournament, the biggest difference Welsh has felt is his mental growth since his freshman year. On top of his faith, Welsh has grown more mature as he heads into his second NCAA Championship match.

“I diet, sleep, stay off social media and take care of all the small things,” Welsh said. “I stay motivated because there’s a payoff down the road, which is right now.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Levi Haines ‘challenging’ himself in final NCAA Championships appearance
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Levi Haines has already taken home a national championship at 157 pounds, but finished in third place at 174 last season. In the national championship match, Haines will attempt to become a two-time national champion at two different weight classes.

As far as what music Haines will rock with for his walkout, it’s still unknown. So far this season, the senior has been going to battle with “Dinosaur” by Hank Williams Jr., and has been successful with it, but there’s a chance that might not be featured for the national championship.

“I haven’t decided yet. I’ve always been the wait and see till the last minute kind of guy,” Haines said.

On the mat, the Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native has been the opposite of indecisive like with his choice of music. Haines has gotten right to work in all of his matches at the NCAA Championships. He began the tournament with a pinfall victory, and has tech-falled the rest of his opponents.

The senior has shown pure domination and the reason why he’s the No. 1 seed in the weight class.

“I’m trying to challenge myself and do better every time I step on the mat,” Haines said. “I think I’ve just done a good job this tournament being myself and competing the way I want to compete. It’s a quick tournament that comes and goes fast, so I think being present and competing the way I want to is really helpful.”

Haines will take on Christopher Minto of Nebraska in the finals, who is a familiar opponent that he has faced this season. Once was in the regular season at the Bryce Jordan Center, with the other time being in the Big Ten Championship. Haines was victorious in both matches, and in both matches, they have been the No. 1 and No. 2 seeds.

“I think we’ve both probably made some adjustments in our game, really appreciate guys like Christopher always forcing me to get better and kind of adjust your wrestling,” Haines said. “I expect that we both made some adjustments from the last time we’ve met, and think it’s gonna be a fun matchup.”

The bout will be Haines’ last match in a Nittany Lion singlet. There’s a chance he could still be in the wrestling room though as a member of the Nittany Lion Wrestling Club, which he hinted at after his semifinal match and before the tournament started.

“I just think being grateful for the time I’ve had at Penn State, and I don’t plan on going anywhere anytime soon, so I think that helps as well,” Haines said. “Coach Cael (Sanderson), coach Casey (Cunningham), coach Cody (Sanderson), coach Nick Lee, they’ve all done a lot to help me achieve my goals and keep me getting better. Just trying to not think about it too much I guess, but mostly being grateful for the time that I’ve had. Looking forward to sticking around, and helping the next group of guys come up through and get better,” Haines said.

Haines isn’t letting the emotions get to him as he faces Minto for his final time in college and wearing the blue and white singlet. Instead he’s preparing for the match and a potential lengthy stay in State College.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State's Mitchell Mesenbrink finding similarities in wrestling and chess, propels him to title match
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Despite being on two completely different landscapes, the mental aspect of chess and wrestling has more similarities than one might initially imagine.

For Mitchell Mesenbrink, he approaches wrestling as if it’s a game of chess, adding that the mental aspect of wrestling is also key in chess.

“I think stylistically and strategically and technically speaking, chess is very similar to wrestling,” Mesenbrink said. “You got guys that maybe are very passive and they'll wrestle like that, or you got guys that will attack and you'll get it. And you can see that in chess. You can see that in sports as a whole.”

Mesenbrink had to take more of a mental approach to his quarterfinal matchup. He couldn’t go full attack mode against North Carolina’s Bryce Hepner. Hepner wrestled a more defensive style, preventing Mesenbrink from scoring double-digit points, and ending his 100% bonus rate on the year.

However, Mesenbrink scored in different ways, tallying four-nearfall points in the second period after starting on the top. He also built up enough riding time to get the extra point when the match ended.

“I like wrestling because for the most part, I can keep pretty much everybody else's opinion out of it,” Mesenbrink said. “That's how I wrestle, too. So the refs don't really have to, ‘oh, is it blank, or was it takedown or not?’ I'm going to make sure it's there.”

With another matchup against Iowa’s Mikey Caliendo on the horizon in the championship bout, Mesenbrink could seal another national title, and cement himself as one of the favorites for the Hodge Trophy if he beats Caliendo, which he’s done eight straight times.

However, the Hodge Trophy isn’t a focus for the Hartland, Wisconsin, native. To him, it’s something that will “sit around and collect dust.” But the honor would solidify him as one of the greats in Penn State wrestling history, as only six Nittany Lions have earned the honor.

He’d join the likes of Kerry McCoy in 1997, David Taylor in 2012 and 2014, Zain Retherford in 2017 and 2018, Bo Nickal in 2019 and Aaron Brooks in 2024 as Hodge Trophy winners who’ve donned the blue and white singlet.

“With the Hodge, it’s such an opinionated thing at the end of the day,” Mesenbrink said. “It's such a futile kind of thing that people vote on. I'm not going to put my hope or well being into something that people are going to vote and think about. So it's a cool thing. But yet again, I think it's interesting because come Sunday night, Monday night, or even when they crown the Hodge trophy, then they're going to be talking about who's going to get it the next time.”

Not only could Mesenbrink make history of his own, he’s likely to make history with his team. Penn State has a chance to break its own points record from 2025, and could also break the number of individual champions if the Nittany Lions go a perfect 6-for-6 in the final session.

“It's not unfair to say that we've assembled a super team of savages and people that can take people out,” Mesenbrink said.

The stuff that doesn’t get seen all the time is the comradery within the wrestling room. Mesenbrink is one of the leaders of the team and the team has continued to take a different look at wrestling.

It’s one that doesn’t see a championship bout as necessarily the most important thing at the moment, but rather enjoying themselves on the mat.

“I think the biggest thing is that we enjoy, we have fun, and again, like I said, we know that this isn't the only thing in our lives or in life,” Mesenbrink said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling’s Marcus Blaze, PJ Duke wrap up freshman year achieving All-American status
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — After falling in the semifinals of the championship bracket, Marcus Blaze and PJ Duke were sentenced to an early session of action, beginning at 11 a.m. on Saturday.

The results were a mixed bag for the pair of Penn State freshmen, with Blaze securing a fourth-place finish while Duke earned third.

Here’s how it happened.

133 pounds

No. 3 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs No. 15 Tyler Knox (VT)

After the lights came up and the cameras turned on, Tyler Knox quickly went down, as Blaze opened this morning bout with a surge of offense, getting awarded his first takedown 27 seconds into the session.

One minute later, he secured a second takedown by nearly securing a cradle one minute later. With thirty seconds left in the opening period, Blaze secured his third takedown of the frame by trapping Knox’s hips as he tried to work his way out of bounds.

After selecting the bottom position, Blaze was stuck under Knox for most of the second period before reversing for two points. Knox was then called for stalling three times, awarding another point to Blaze. Despite the takedown, Blaze recorded a final takedown before Period 2 expired.

Blaze got a second reversal in the third period, then took Knox down one final time to secure the 20-4 tech fall.

133 pounds — third-place match: No. 3 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs No. 4 Aaron Seidel (VT)

Blaze and Aaron Seidel faced off in a Pennsylvania high school alum matchup that looked evenly matched throughout. The first period was a stalemate with neither side securing any points.

Seidel gained the advantage in Period 2 as he rode Blaze out for the entire two minutes. Seidel then escaped in two seconds at the start of Period 3, then taking over the bout.

Seidel got the first takedown with a minute remaining, sparking a “let’s go Hokies” chant from the crowd. He rode Blaze out for the rest of the contest, placing Blaze fourth in his freshman year national championship run after the 4-1 defeat.

157 pounds

No. 1 PJ Duke (PSU) vs No. 7 Kannon Webster (ILL)

Duke entered the floor of Rocket Arena with a straight face that he carried onto the mat before he locked up with Kannon Webster. When the whistle blew, Duke started pressuring his opponent from the jump.

About a minute into the contest, Duke picked Webster's leg, holding it as Webster bounced around the mat for 30 seconds before completing the takedown on the edge of the circle. Webster’s escape ended Period 1 with a 3-1 score in favor of the Nittany Lion.

Duke opened Period 2 with a traditional tackle takedown, overpowering Webster for a double leg. He topped a minute of riding time before Webster escaped his grasp. Duke could not catch Webster in the final minute of the period, despite repeated shots at the retreating Illini.

After Blaze escaped to start the third, Webster caught Duke in a tie up and killed off half the time left on the clock before two-blood time stoppages. After the breaks, Webster spent the final 30 seconds scrambling to score a takedown on Duke, but the Nittany Lion stalled out the clock to earn the 8-3 decision.

157 pounds — third-place match: No. 1 PJ Duke (PSU) vs No. 8 Brandon Cannon (OSU)

Duke’s second match of the session was his third of the year against Brandon Cannon, and second of the tournament. Duke won both times.

Much like his first match of the day, Duke wasted no time and secured a takedown in the first 30 seconds. Duke then locked Cannon in a cradle, but was not awarded a takedown before Cannon went out of bounds. Cannon locked Duke up for a minute after the first takedown, but the Penn State freshman slammed him down for a second takedown and two-nearfall points.

At the start of Period 2, Cannon fell near his bench and left the floor for injury time. When he returned to the mat, Duke returned to scoring with another takedown, extending his lead to 11-2.

In Period 3, Duke kept the pressure, and took down Cannon twice more to secure the 20-4 tech fall and place third in the country.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling clinches 5th straight NCAA Championship
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Another year, another Penn State team championship.

The Nittany Lions clinched the team title with Marcus Blaze's victory over Stanford's Tyler Knox.

It's Penn State's fifth straight championship, securing 13-of-15 championships in the last 16 years. It is the 14th championship in program history as well as the 13th with Cael Sanderson as the head coach.

The Nittany Lions needed 6.5 points entering the day to clinch, entering Saturday with 153 points.

Penn State has six wrestlers in the NCAA Championship matches, tying an NCAA record. Luke Lilledahl, Shayne Van Ness, Mitchell Mesenbrink, Levi Haines, Rocco Welsh and Josh Barr all will wrestle for the top spot on the podium later this evening.

If all six win, it'll break its own record for most national champions, with five both in 2017 and 2022, which holds a tie with Iowa in 1986 and 1997 and Oklahoma State in 2005.

The final session begins at 6:30 p.m. on Saturday.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball brings high production from heavy hitters despite loss
author: Gianna Dowling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State suffered a heartbreaking home-opening loss at the hands of Wisconsin on Friday night. The Nittany Lions fell 6-5 on a Badgers game-winning sacrifice fly in the top of the seventh inning.

Despite the loss, the blue and white saw strong production from its bats, particularly the heart of its heart. Two, three and four hitters, Michela Barbanente, Allison Oneacre and Brooke Klosowicz were responsible for bringing in all of Penn State’s runs.

Down 2-0 with two outs in the bottom of the third inning, the Nittany Lions rallied for four runs and the bat that started the run production was that of Barbanente.

With a runner on first base, the junior second baseman faced a 1-1 count. Barbanente took the offering and knocked it off the scoreboard over the left field fence to even the game at two. This marked the first home run at Beard Field in 2026 and the third of the season for Barbanente.

Kloswowicz was the next batter up for Penn State and is certainly no stranger to being a high producer of the Nittany Lions’ offense. The junior is the owner of the blue and white’s single-season doubles and RBI records, both of which were set a year ago.

Entering Friday night's matchup, the Johnsburg, Illinois, native led Penn State’s lineup with a .362 batting average.

Facing a 0-2 count, Klosowicz did what she does best and ripped her second double of the game to left center field. This brought up Oneacre, the Nittany Lions’ hard hitting freshman.

Oneacre has been no stranger to the spotlight in her rookie campaign, leading the blue and with 37 RBIs and tied with Klosowicz for a team-high 10 home runs. Well, both those numbers increased in the Alpharetta, Georgia, native’s third inning at-bat.

Oneacre watched two balls go by, followed by two strikes, but the fourth pitch was sent over the left field fence as the freshman knocked her first long ball in her home ballpark.

This two-run blast from Oneacre kept her on pace to break Klosowicz’s single-season record of 53 RBIs.

Klosowicz brought in the fifth and final Penn State run of the game in the fifth inning on a single up the middle.

In addition to bringing home all of the Nittany Lions’ runs, the strong slugging trio combined for six of the blue and white’s nine hits, but five of these came from Barbanente and Klosowicz. The two juniors were the only Penn State batters with multi-hit outings. Klosowicz finished the night 3-for-4 and Barbanente 2-for-3 with a walk.

Aside from Barbanente, Oneacre and Klosowicz, freshman Gabby Gradishar was the only other Nittany Lions’ batter with an extra base hit.

Forgetting about the loss, the ability for the middle of coach Clarisa Crowell’s lineup to produce the way they did on Friday night is a key component of the blue and white’s game.

Oneacre and Klosowicz have been the two most effective bats for Penn State and they continued to show why in Game 1 against Wisconsin.

While Barbanente may not produce the same batting numbers, her presence is definitely seen, especially on the basepaths, where the junior is tied for a team-high nine steals.

Even in a tough loss, Penn State showed its offense was dangerous when the middle of the lineup is producing at a high level.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: 'One of the best atmospheres I’ve ever played in' | Penn State women's hockey fueled by fan support in defeat
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State woke up on Friday morning with a single goal: beat the defending National Champions in its first Frozen Four appearance in program history.

The Nittany Lions, the hosts of the 2026 NCAA Women’s Frozen Four, had to face No. 2 Wisconsin in the semifinals. In the most important game in program history, the blue and white drew a record-setting 5,176 spectators, a program record and the highest attendance in Women’s Frozen Four history.

“That atmosphere was electric,” coach Jeff Kampersal said. “We showed people what Penn State is all about. When (Tessa Janecke) scored the first goal, the crowd carried us a lot.”

Being Penn State’s first appearance in the Women’s Frozen Four, only a year after the men’s team played in its first Frozen Four in St. Louis, Missouri, the pre-game setup was altered to fit the occasion.

On any given afternoon throughout the season, Pegula Ice Arena looked entirely different. Half of the spectator areas were blocked off, the Roar Zone was a sea of empty bleachers and the arena wasn't decked out in specialized decals made specifically for the top-4 teams in the nation.

Prior to puck drop, the blue and white were received at Pegula Ice Arena’s Gate A for a “welcome party”. Fans, the Nittany Lion and the men’s hockey team showed their support as the players walked into the rink.

“Everything that led up to this game was a first for us,” Katelyn Roberts said. “So there was so much excitement around it, to teeter between practice, the media and the hype around school, being in the moment. But then, once the puck dropped, we realized that it is just a game that we’ve all played for so long.”

The lineups were announced in a neutral, equally loud tone by the PA announcer, with Penn State going first as the lower-seeded team is given the “away” moniker. Instead of asking Pegula Ice Arena if it is ready for “some Penn State hockey”, the PA announcer removed the host program from mention.

As soon as the puck dropped, the “home game” atmosphere kicked in for Penn State.

Charged. Energized. Chaotic. Those are the words to describe the game’s opening as the Roar Zone — dressed in the university’s signature all-white clothing — fired up the Nittany Lions, making a huge difference as Penn State flew out of the gates with two power-play goals in the first period.

“I couldn’t believe how packed it was,” Janecke said. “But we don’t just want that in a Frozen Four. We want that at every game, and I think we deserve it. You can see how much it helps the team on the ice when (the Roar Zone) is there supporting.”

All of the regularly-scheduled chants made an appearance, but this time with additional volume due to the game’s record-setting attendance. The crowd set not only Penn State’s single-game record, but the Women’s Frozen Four record.

Pegula Ice Arena reached its peak volume as Janecke broke free from the defense on a clear breakaway. She faked a shot, moved to her backhand and tucked the puck past Ava McNaughton’s pad to tie the game, 3-3. Every fan of the Nittany Lions leapt to their feet, screaming for joy, causing the building to shake.

“We had a stadium game this year, we had a cool event (in Gloucester, Massachusetts) around Thanksgiving, but playing in front of a crowd like tonight was next level,” Kampersal said. “Like Janecke’s game: next level.”

Despite the effort, Penn State was unable to advance to the National Championship, as a shot from Kirsten Simms found the back of the net to end the game.

At the conclusion of the game, the home crowd gave the Nittany Lions a standing ovation after Wisconsin’s band played its alma mater. A bitter moment for Penn State’s seniors and graduate students as the goal put an end to their collegiate careers.

However, the implications of the record-setting crowd and the support the players got from the fans — both students and non-students alike — made Penn State’s first Frozen Four an unforgettable game.

“The crowd tonight was one of the best atmospheres I’ve ever played in,” Janecke said. “I wish for these girls that it continues next year and just in general. The sport of women’s hockey is growing every day and I want that to continue when we’re gone. We did our job here and it wasn’t about the wins; we grew women’s hockey in Hockey Valley. So if we could inspire one girl to want to play, then we did our job.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Penn State wrestling sends 6 wrestlers to NCAA Championship finals in Session 4
author: AJ Piazza & Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — Penn State had an opportunity at more history, with eight wrestlers in the semifinals.

The Nittany Lions went 6-of-8 in the semifinals and continued to hold their lead in the team race with 153 points. Oklahoma State sat in second with 111.5 points heading into the final day. The six finalists tied an NCAA record for most finalists from a single team.

Here’s how the Penn State wrestlers did in Session 4.

125 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Luke Lilledahl (PSU) vs No. 5 Troy Spratley (OKST)

Luke Lilledahl’s nickname “Lightning” seemed very fitting for the start the sophomore had against Troy Spratley. Lilledahl got in on a single leg and secured the takedown over Spratley.

After letting Spratley escape, Lilledahl was right back on the attack, but this time had Spratley on his back to earn two nearfall points in addition to the takedown.

It was cruise control from there for Lilledahl, who rolled to the 8-3 victory, advancing to the title match for the first time in his young career.

133 pounds

Semifinals: No. 3 Marcus Blaze (PSU) vs. No. 2 Ben Davino (OHST)

Marcus Blaze and Ben Davino met for the third time this season, with the series being split 1-1. The two had a much slower start than their counterparts on the mat next to them — Oklahoma State’s Jax Forrest and Virginia Tech’s Aaron Seidel.

Blaze and Davino were deadlocked at 0-0 entering the second period. Both wrestlers earned an escape point and went to sudden victory for the third time this season. Sudden victory caused some chaos, as Davino was in on an attack, but despite the crowd’s disbelief, no takedown was rewarded.

The two went to tiebreakers again, and Blaze rode Davino for 21 seconds, meaning he needed to escape in less than 21 seconds in the second tiebreaker. Davino rode out Blaze, ending Blaze’s chance at redemption and a chance at the championship, as the Buckeye won 2-1.

Blaze will have a chance to wrestle back for third place beginning at 11 a.m. Saturday.

149 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Shayne Van Ness (PSU) vs No. 20 Chance Lamer (NEB)

Shayne Van Ness and Chance Lamer were set for their second match of the season, with Van Ness being victorious in the first match.

The junior got right to work in this match with the first points. Van Ness continued to dominate Lamer in all aspects of the match. The Sommerville, New Jersey, native got two more takedowns and four near-fall points to take a 15-1 lead.

Van Ness secured his fourth takedown and got the 18-1 victory to advance to his first national championship.

157 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 PJ Duke (PSU) vs No. 5 Landon Robideau (OKST)

PJ Duke and Landon Robideau started off stagnant. Neither wrestler did anything in the opening period. It wasn’t until Robideau escaped from Duke, but other than that, it was a lot of hand fighting through the first two periods.

Duke escaped from Robideau and both wrestlers didn’t make any attempts until the end, which sent the match to sudden victory. It wasn’t until sudden victory that things got interesting. Robideau had a chance to take down Duke, but Duke turned it into an opportunity of his own that was originally called a takedown, but got reversed.

Robideau put the pressure on Duke, but the Cowboy showed stellar defense to not allow a takedown. Robideau got a reversal against Duke and took a 3-1 lead in tiebreakers and closed out the match with a 3-1 victory, fending off any final attacks from Duke.

165 Pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Mitchell Mesenbrink (PSU) vs No. 12 Cesar Alvan (COL)

Mitchell Mesebrink started the match as he starts every match, with a lead. Mesenbrink looked like he had a pinfall for about a minute, but got four near-fall points and took a 7-0 lead.

The junior continued to add points and showed dominance in the match with more near-fall points to advance to the national championship with a 15-0 tech fall victory.

174 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Levi Haines (PSU) vs. No. 5 Patrick Kennedy (IOWA)

Levi Haines is 3-0 against Patrick Kennedy in their previous matchups. Haines started this match as the more aggressive wrestler. The senior scored the only takedown of the first period and took a 3-0 lead.

Haines continued to dominate with another takedown and stood tall with a 7-1 lead. Kennedy didn’t display any offense as his only points came from escape. After Kennedy escaped, Haines got another takedown and near-fall points to take a commanding 17-3 lead.

Haines advanced to the national championship with an 18-3 tech fall victory.

184 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Rocco Welsh (PSU) vs. No. 5 Brock Mantanona (MICH)

Rocco Welsh versus Brock Mantanona was a very dull match through the first two periods. There was a lot of hand fighting, but both wrestlers acquired escape points and did nothing else offensively.

Until the third period, when Welsh got the only takedown of the match to garner a 4-1 lead. Mantanona escaped and cut the lead to two. Welsh got called with a stalling so the victory was 4-3 by decision.

197 pounds

Semifinals: No. 1 Josh Barr (PSU) vs. No. 5 Joey Novak (WYO)

Josh Barr continued the trend of starting the match with the lead. Barr got the takedown against Joey Novak. Novak responded with a reversal to make it 3-2. Despite Barr controlling the match, Novak didn’t go away; he still attempted to take Barr down, but was just unsuccessful.

The sophomore secured his third takedown to make it 10-3. Barr got his fourth takedown to make it 13-3. The sophomore got the victory by a 14-3 major decision. Barr will try to avenge his loss in the national championship last season by winning his first national title this year.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball falls to Wisconsin in home opener despite late comeback bid
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a slew of road games to start the campaign, Penn State finally hosted its first game at Nittany Lion Softball Park and Beard Field against Wisconsin on Friday night. Both squads looked to snap three-game losing streaks and their first Big Ten victory of the season.

Under rainy skies, Penn State dropped its home opener 6-5 against Wisconsin as the Badgers rallied in the final frame and played the winning run.

Similar to last weekend’s sweep, the Nittany Lions found themselves in an early hole. In the first frame, right-hander Mackenzie Duncan surrendered back-to-back leadoff singles, but retired the next three Badgers.

However, the blue and white were also kept quiet in the bottom half of the inning, despite third baseman Brooke Klosowicz continuing her hot streak with a two-out double to left. Though Wisconsin left two runners stranded in the previous inning, the Badgers struck in the second and plated two runs.

Mackenzie Foster drew a leadoff walk and then Duncan hit Kaylie Whidden with one out. Duncan struck out Hannah Conger, but Emily Bojan delivered a two-out RBI single into center to give Wisconsin a 1-0 lead. The two-out rally didn’t stop there as Hilary Blomberg also knocked an RBI single right back into center.

Coach Clarisa Crowell went to the bullpen and brought in right-hander McKenna Young for relief. The junior recorded the last out on a groundout, but Penn State was forced to play catch-up again.

Right-hander Berritt Herr allowed a leadoff hit by right fielder Breana Hanik, but swiftly retired the next few Nittany Lions she faced as the blue and white’s bats started slow. Wisconsin lined two singles into the outfield in the third, but Young held the Badgers scoreless.

After a few quiet innings, Penn State’s offense came alive later in the inning and took the lead off four runs. Herr was cruising until she hit center fielder Natalie Lieto with two outs. Second baseman Michela Barbanente took advantage of Herr’s mistake and launched a two-run shot to left field.

The contest was now tied, but the Nittany Lions continued their two-out rally. Klosowicz smacked her second double of the night into left center and shortstop Allison Oneacre showed the Happy Valley crowd her power. The freshman blasted her tenth home run of the campaign to left and gave Penn State its first lead, 4-2.

Yet, Wisconsin cut into the blue and white’s two-run lead in the fourth. Despite Young totaling two strikeouts after Conger shot a leadoff single up the middle, the Badgers put together some two-out magic of their own.

Kendra Lewis roped a double into left-center and plated Conger to make the score 4-3. The sophomore kept the Badgers close on both sides of the ball, delivering a shutdown inning in the bottom half.

Wisconsin knotted the game back up at four in the fifth frame. Left-hander Brevyn Kellepouris walked leadoff Danielle Lucey and the Badgers put together a similar rally from the previous frame. After two strikeouts, Kaylie Whidden smashed a timely double into right-center and plated Lucey.

Though the game didn’t stay tied for long, as the Nittany Lions regained the lead in their bottom half. With a 0-2 advantage, Lewis hit leadoff left fielder Frankee Flesher. A few batters later, Barbanente chopped an infield single to shortstop.

The Badgers felt the pressure and tried to keep the score tied, but right-hander Shelby Jacobson couldn’t escape the inning. The senior threw a wild pitch and both runners advanced into scoring position. Klosowicz then delivered with an RBI single up the middle and Penn State regained the lead, 5-4.

However, the game continued to go back and forth. Blomberg lifted a solo dinger over the wall in left-center and knotted the score at five. The blue and white couldn’t drive in first baseman Gabby Gradishar from second, so the game was tied entering the seventh.

Right-hander Abigail Britton was brought in for the final frame, but the Badgers plated a run. Lucey knocked a leadoff single up the middle and Foster then drew a walk. Whidden then brought home the go-ahead run off a sacrifice fly into center.

Penn State had to mount a comeback in its last breath, but was kept from scoring despite having a few baserunners. With the loss, the Nittany Lions fell to 19-10 on the season and 0-4 in conference play.

Up Next

Penn State continues its first home series against Wisconsin at 2 p.m. Saturday. The game will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description: Former Penn State football players work out at Holuba Hall for the 2026 Pro Day, giving themselves the opportunity to impress NFL scouts.The players entering the draft participate in multiple drills including the 40-yard dash and broad jump.Prospects such as quarterback Drew Allar and safety Zakee Wheatley discuss the process and the work they've put in leading up to the NFL Draft.
