Penn State hits the road again and travels to West Lafayette for a three-game series against Purdue after losing seven of its last eight games. Away games have been a struggle this season, with a road record of just 1-7.

Meanwhile, the 12-6 Boilermakers are fresh off a conference series victory over Maryland and are raking on offense. Most of the Nittany Lions' losses haven’t been close, so the squad must limit runs in this series as they are still searching for their first series win of the campaign.

Purdue’s offensive power

The blue and white’s pitching will be tested facing Purdue, who ranks within the upper half of many offensive categories in the Big Ten, such as batting average (0.301) and home runs (23).

While Penn State’s pitching staff ranks in the bottom three for many key stats, including a conference-worst team ERA at 8.49, this would be a good series to build confidence off of and turn the pitching woes around. For the second straight series, the Nittany Lions will face a complete lineup.

Junior Ali Banks spearheads the Boilermakers’ explosive offense. The Stone Mountain, Georgia, native holds a .341 average, with 14 hits, two home runs and seven RBI in 37 plate appearances. A pair of upperclassmen, Sam Flores and CJ Richmond, power Purdue’s bats, each sharing a team-leading five home runs.

Flores also leads the squad in hits with 26 and RBI with 16. Overall, Penn State must deliver quality tarts and provide good bullpen relief because the Boilermakers are capable of putting up crooked numbers.

On the mound

Penn State’s ace Colin Fitzgerald is likely to start on the mound this weekend. The redshirt senior bounced back after a few rough outings and delivered a great performance in the blue and white’s 11-6 win over Iowa. The Lititz, Pennsylvania, native surrendered just four hits and three earned runs and tallied seven strikeouts over seven innings of work.

The Nittany Lions will need a similar outing against Purdue to pull off the upset. Sophomore Ben Hudson will also probably start and Penn State needs a better performance from the right-hander this weekend.

The Medford, New Jersey, native had a solid few first outings earlier in the season, but lately has not been able to last long in starts. In the 13-4 loss against the Hawkeyes, Hudson allowed seven earned runs and 12 hits over just 4.2 innings.

On the flip side, Purdue ranks in the middle of the Big Ten with a 5.30 ERA. While Penn State’s pitching is a big storyline to its rocky start, the blue and white’s offense also hasn’t been very productive.

Senior Michael Anderson and redshirt junior Bryce Molinaro are the few bright spots in the lineup this campaign, but the Nittany Lions need steady production from other players. Anderson is tied for a team-best 20 hits and leads the squad in home runs with eight and RBI with 22.

Molinaro is heating up as he homered in both games against Georgetown last week and delivered four multi-hit games. Outside of those two hitters, senior Jayden Davis batted 2.99 and totaled 20 hits, two home runs, and eight RBi in 67 at-bats.

Penn State will likely face senior Zach Erdman. The left-hander holds the third-lowest ERA on the Boilermakers at 4.44, surrendering 13 earned runs, 29 hits and just six walks over 26.1 innings. Erdman has also totaled 31 strikeouts.

Left-hander Jarvis Evans could also start this weekend. The senior enters the series with a 5.48 ERA, allowing 14 earned runs, 21 hits and 10 walks over 23 innings.

Overall, the Nittany Lions can’t fall into large deficits and must score runs early and often against an offensive powerhouse like Purdue.

Friday’s game is slated at 4 p.m., game two is Saturday at 2 p.m. and the series finale is Sunday at 1 p.m. All games will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Get to know Penn State women's basketball's new head coach Tanisha Wright
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Less than two weeks after Penn State fired former coach Carolyn Kieger, it found its new leader.

The Lady Lions will reportedly hire former WNBA assistant coach and Penn State alum Tanisha Wright to be the seventh-ever head coach in Lady Lion history, according to Matt Fortuna.

While Wright doesn't have any collegiate head coaching experience, here's a look at what she has to offer to the blue and white.

Collegiate experience

Wright graduated from Penn State in 2005, marking the first head coach in Lady Lion history to both play and coach in Happy Valley.

With the blue and white, Wright became one of the best defensive players in Lady Lion history — if not, Big Ten history — earning three Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year honors.

The West Mifflin, Pennsylvania, native played a big role for Penn State out of the gate. In Year 1, Wright started in 27 games and averaged 10.5 points per game. However, that was just the start of Wright’s success with the blue and white, as she earned nearly a dozen awards over the next three years.

The 5-foot-11 guard’s career took off in her sophomore year, as she played in all 35 games and averaged 16 points per game.

The 2002-03 season marked the first of back-to-back-to-back years of Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year honors, as well as her first of three AP All-American honorable mentions. Wright was named a unanimous Big Ten first teamer, which she repeated her senior year.

While her sophomore year alone earned more honors than many athletes do their whole career, she came back hungry for more in her junior and senior years, earning more defensive player of the year honors, All-American honors and led the league in scoring with 19.6 points per game in the 2004-05 season.

Wright helped lead her squad to the NCAA tournament each of her four seasons, including a career-best NCAA tournament regional final appearance in her junior year.

WNBA career

Wright is one of 16 former Lady Lions who’ve been drafted to the WNBA, and is the highest-drafted athlete. In the 2005 WNBA Draft, Wright went No. 12 to the Seattle Storm, where she spent the first 10 years of her professional career.

The Penn State alum played a smaller role with the Storm over the first four seasons, starting in less than half of their games, where she averaged just 4.9 points per game.

Starting in 2009, Wright began seeing the starting lineup for almost all of her team’s matchups, averaging double-digit scoring for three seasons. In 2010, Wright helped Seattle to its second WNBA title in team history after losing in the conference semifinals each of her other years in the league.

Wright spent the next four seasons with the Storm before going to the New York Liberty for two years, where she played under Detroit Pistons legend Bill Laimbeer.

While Wright stepped away from the WNBA in 2017, she returned for one season with the Minnesota Lynx in 2018 and another year with the Liberty in 2019. In her 14 seasons in the WNBA, Wright finished in the top 10 in the league in blocks every year.

Coaching career

Despite coming to Happy Valley as the newest head coach, Wright only has a few years of collegiate coaching experience, serving as the assistant coach at Charlotte from 2017-2020.

After retiring from the WNBA in 2019, the former Lady Lion transitioned in the league to a coaching role, where she started out with the Las Vegas Aces in a WNBA title runner-up season.

Wright spent just one year out west before being hired as the head coach for the Atlanta Dream in 2022, where she spent three seasons.

The Dream recorded a 48-68 under Wright, including back-to-back first-round playoff exits in her last two years. After dropping from 19 wins in the 2023 season to 15 wins the following year, Wright was released from Atlanta.

Wright spent her final year in the WNBA with the Chicago Sky, where they recorded a 10-34 record last season.

Now, she’ll come to the NCAA for just her second collegiate coaching season as she becomes the seventh head coach in Penn State history.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State safety Zakee Wheatley embracing versatile role to better his chances in NFL
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
During his time at Penn State, Zakee Wheatley was a ball magnet in the secondary, accounting for nine turnovers and being dubbed the “Takeaway King” for leading the team in takeaways during the summer.

After starting his career as a cornerback, Wheatley played safety the next four years, with mixed in looks at linebacker in certain packages. His versatility, and ability to find the ball in his hands on defense, has NFL squads intrigued about what he brings to the table.

“I definitely look at myself as a defensive back. I played corner and safety my whole life, and even sometimes being that box safety, playing like a big nickel,” Wheatley said. “Talking to teams, that's something they definitely love about me, being able to move me across the field and make plays.”

In addition to giving teams a chess piece to move around on defense, Wheatley boosted his stock by being the lone Nittany Lion to participate in on-field drills at both the 2026 NFL Scouting Combine and at Penn State Pro Day.

That included a 40-yard dash time of 4.62 seconds in Holuba Hall after not running at the combine.

“I felt like the combine, I could have put out better numbers,” Wheatley said. “And I was training hard for the last three months. So the combine, I put out some decent numbers, but I feel like I can come back and better those numbers.”

In the end, he was happy with how he performed.

“I'm satisfied. I put it all on the line. I trained a lot for this,” Wheatley said. “Been doing this since I was five years old, and been training hard for the last few months. So whatever I put out here I feel satisfied with it. All glory to God to bless me with this, and looking forward to keep getting better.”

While Wheatley is being viewed as a player who can be a jack-of-all-trades, he’d be next in line to join the recent run of Penn State safeties to hear their name called in the NFL draft. That group consists of Jaquan Brisker, Ji’Ayir Brown, Kevin Winston Jr. and Jaylen Reed.

Wheatley was teammates with all of them, and learned a lot as a result through the years. He specifically was asked about Brisker and cornerback Joey Porter Jr., and the role the tandem has played as he goes through the draft process.

“My brothers, blood brothers, and I've been with them for a long time,” Wheatley said. “From the minute I came out here as a freshman in 2021, until now, we've been best buds. They my brothers. Being able to hit them up anytime, who went through this process and who are in the NFL meant a lot.”

Wheatley said the last time he didn’t have a football in his hand was when he was 2 years old. He’s been dreaming of making it to the NFL a long time, and that dream is going to be a reality quite soon.

“Been a lot of work to get here, a lot of efforts from my family, friends, trainers, just as much as it is for me as my family,” Wheatley said. “There's been a lot of sacrifices my way as well as my family. So knowing I can finally get the chance to achieve that dream and get my career started means a lot.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Report: Penn State women’s basketball hires WNBA assistant Tanisha Wright as next head coach
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After spending the last two decades in the WNBA, Penn State alum Tanisha Wright is coming home.

Wright was reported as the seventh head coach in Lady Lion history on March 19 by Matt Fortuna of The Inside Zone, following the firing of former coach Carolyn Kieger on March 5.

Source: Penn State women’s basketball is hiring Chicago Sky assistant coach and former WNBA standout Tanisha Wright as its next head coach.Wright was a three-time Big Ten defensive player of the year with the Lady Lions during her college playing days. pic.twitter.com/d16hq2W5H6— Matt Fortuna (@Matt_Fortuna) March 19, 2026

The Brooklyn native spent her four collegiate years with the blue and white, where she recorded 1,995 points, currently sitting eighth in program history.

During her time in Happy Valley, Wright was named a three-time Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year and All-Big Ten First Teamer.

Wright was drafted 12th overall in the 2005 WNBA Draft by the Seattle Storm before bouncing around with the Storm, the New York Liberty and Minnesota Lynx. Through 13 years, Wright was named to the First Team All-Defensive squad five times and helping the Storm to the WNBA title in 2010.

After retiring in 2020, she served as an assistant coach with the Las Vegas Aces before being named the head coach for the Atlanta Dream.

In her second and third seasons with the Dream, Wright’s squad combined for 34 wins and fell in the first round of the playoffs both years.

After being let go at the end of the 2024 season, Wright joined the Chicago Sky as an assistant coach — a position she held until being hired at Penn State.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘I’m different, man’ | Penn State RB Kaytron Allen looks to seize opportunity in NFL
author: Avery Hill | Football Editor
description:
Growing up in Norfolk, Virginia, Kaytron Allen has always had a chip on his shoulder.

“A lot of different things go on,” Allen said. “And it's hard to get out of Norfolk for real.”

Allen has thrived outside of his hometown so far, spending time in Florida at powerhouse IMG Academy in high school before being a top running back all of his time at Penn State. Now, he’s looking to take his success even further by hearing his name called in the NFL draft in April.

“It don’t stop now,” Allen said. “(Penn State) was just one step of the way. So it's another level I got to reach now to be better than I was the last four years.”

He joins his backfield partner Nick Singleton as one of the top backs on draft boards. As part, he was invited to the NFL Scouting Combine, but he only participated in on-field drills. At pro day inside Holuba Hall, the only time he participated was catching passes from Drew Allar, citing his agent’s advice.

But Allen spent the last few months training in Miami, noting improvements with his speed and hands. While he showed glimpses of his athleticism and receiving abilities while at Penn State, they were clearly secondary to his calling card as a gritty and powerful running back.

Bursting through the hole and often running through defenders for sizable gains, Allen managed a stellar career in Happy Valley, which was capped off with him becoming the program’s all-time leading rusher with 4,180 yards.

“It meant a lot because I put it down on my record board, something that I wanted to do,” Allen said. “And I told my aunties that I wanted to do it. They passed — unfortunately, they couldn't see it, but I told them I wanted to do it.”

The success Allen has had in the past four years is clear, even if he doesn’t think others truly get it.

“I don't think people really respect what I've done here,” Allen said.

The rushing record wasn’t the only accomplishment from Allen at Penn State. In 2025, he was named an AFCA All-American for the second team and made the Associated Press’ third team. He also finished third in program history for career rushing touchdowns at 39.

But the lack of recognition doesn’t bother Allen. He always aspired to leave the impact he left at Penn State, and he maintains a firm belief in who he is.

“I’m different, man,” Allen said. “I feel like I can do everything.”

But as Allen moves on to a new chapter, he still wants fans to be able to remember his impact. In his words, it’s the “dirty work” and his love for football that the Happy Valley faithful should look back at.

As he moves forward, that likely extends to his hometown audience as well.

While he doesn’t know what his emotions will be like when he finally gets his moment, Allen’s ready to put on for his city.

As his exact stock varies between Day 2 and 3 by various draft experts, there’s no true idea for when Allen will get his name called. He said he’s talked to “a lot” of teams, and at the combine, he confirmed talking to the Houston Texans.

Regardless of who calls his name, the kid from Norfolk is ready to take his shot at making an impact that very few have made out of the area.

“I've been dreaming about it so long, and it's like it's within arm's reach,” Allen said. “I just got to, whatever happens, take advantage of it. All I need is an opportunity.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: ‘I could do it all’ | Former Penn State WR Trebor Peña wants NFL scouts to know he's versatile
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State Pro Day on Wednesday wasn’t the first time that wide receiver Trebor Peña played in a setting like that — He caught passes for former teammate and quarterback Kyle McCord during Syracuse Pro Day last year.

Peña, who transferred to Penn State after five seasons with the Orange, said he spoke to a few of his ex-teammates at Syracuse who are now in the NFL. This all prepared him to showcase his talents in front of scouts as he’s set to enter the next chapter.

“I kind of knew how (pro day) was going to be, but I wasn't actually doing it (with Syracuse),” Peña said Wednesday. “It's definitely a different feeling doing it, but I feel like they helped me out, and it got me ready for this.”

Peña displayed a diverse route tree while hauling in passes from quarterback Drew Allar, including a few receptions that saw him receive a pass near the sideline before bursting upfield.

Peña, who’s listed at 5-foot-10 and 187 pounds, participated in athletic testing during pro day as well. He recorded a 10-foot-3 broad jump, a 4.11-second pro shuttle, a 6.90-second 3-cone drill, a 35.5-inch vertical and 19 reps in the bench press.

But in addition to those other tests, Peña prioritized his speed while preparing for pro day. When it was his turn to run the 40-yard dash, he took off from his stance and clocked in at 4.52 seconds.

“I feel like the biggest thing for the pro day I was working on was the 40,” Peña said. “I was focused on trying to be as fast as possible. That's very important to show how you can move. So that's the main focus, but I was working on everything, all the drills.”

Peña was a versatile asset in the Nittany Lions’ offense last year, catching 49 passes for 552 yards and two touchdowns in addition to 12 rushing attempts for 73 yards. He capped off his year with five receptions for 100 yards and a touchdown in the Pinstripe Bowl win over Clemson, earning himself MVP honors.

Penn State had national championship aspirations last season, which was partly due to adding players out of the transfer portal like Peña, who was an All-ACC second-teamer in 2024. While the Nittany Lions ended the year far from what was expected, it’s an experience he’ll use to his advantage as he looks forward to an NFL career.

“I feel like I learned a lot of things,” Peña said. “You kind of have expectations going into a season, and when things don't go your way, it's how you respond, so I feel like we all banded together and responded the right way, even though it didn't go our way, but I feel like that just makes you stronger as a person.”

With the 2026 NFL Draft just over a month away, Peña said he’s spoken with the San Francisco 49ers, Washington Commanders, New York Jets and Green Bay Packers. He said those conversations were mainly just “talking and getting to know each other.”

While speaking to NFL teams, Peña said he wants to make sure his versatility is known.

“Mental toughness, physical toughness, play-making ability, all the things that you can do, I feel like you got to put that on display for them to know,” Peña said. “I feel like I'm a good receiver, outside, inside. Could be used in the backfield, kick return, punt return. I feel like I could do it all.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: How the Collegian used AI for data work in a free speech investigation
author: Mercedes Hamilton | Editor-in-chief; and Tyler Nolt | Data reporter
description:
Part of the Collegian’s commitment to transparency is telling our readers when and how AI was used in our reporting. Click here for a letter from our editor and to take a look at our AI policy.

Our data reporter Tyler Nolt used Microsoft Copilot, an AI-powered digital tool, to help sort and analyze data for his investigation on free speech.

This is the first time under our updated policy that we’ve permitted a reporter to use AI to assist with any part of the content creation process. No portion of the story was written using AI, which our guidelines strictly prohibit.

Nolt had been keeping an eye on FIRE’s annual College Free Speech Rankings for a while. When the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE) released its most recent report in September 2025, he noticed Penn State’s significant improvement and wanted to understand this shift over the course of a few years, as well as how it compared with other institutions.

He began by creating a spreadsheet with FIRE’s 2025 and 2026 College Free Speech Ranking. He then calculated the year-to-year change for each of the 251 schools.

This revealed that Penn State tied for 10th most improved overall, and upon using Microsoft Copilot to help identify Pennsylvania’s institutions, it was the most improved in the state and third most improved out of the Big Ten schools.

To understand longer-term trends in a timely manner, Nolt asked Copilot to subtract each institution’s ranking from one year to the next using five years of FIRE data. To prevent errors, Nolt created a separate spreadsheet to ensure each college’s name and placement matched consistently across all years.

Our fact checking process

Before the story was published, five people independently fact checked and replicated Copilot’s work to ensure there were no errors.

The author and three editors each manually verified all of the numbers by cross referencing Nolt’s spreadsheet with FIRE’s raw data.

Then, a fourth editor recreated the spreadsheet in Microsoft Excel and used a formula to calculate the difference between each year’s College Free Speech Ranking. This is how we verified the math Copilot had done to determine the number of spots each college rose or fell from one year to the next.

We then used Excel’s sorting functions to organize the colleges in descending order based on the year-to-year changes to double check each institution’s placement.

Finally, the author sorted both the raw data and data manipulated by Copilot into alphabetical order to ensure consistency across the board. All of the data matched, and every calculation was confirmed to be correct.

Thank you for your continued support and readership. If you have any questions or feedback about our process, we invite you to share them anonymously here.

title: Preview | No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey set to host No. 2 Wisconsin in program-first Frozen Four
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
History is in the making in Hockey Valley.

Following a 3-0 victory over No. 6 Connecticut in the University Park Regional Final on Saturday afternoon, No. 3 Penn State is set to make its debut in the NCAA Women’s Frozen Four against the defending national champions, No. 2 Wisconsin, at Pegula Ice Arena at 7:30 p.m. on Friday.

Tale of the tape

The Nittany Lions enter their first Frozen Four as the four-time AHA Tournament Champions, taking down No. 12 Mercyhurst, 3-2, in the AHA Championship game held at Pegula Ice Arena. With a 33-5 overall record, the blue and white earned the right to host the Huskies and No. 9 Princeton for one of the four regionals.

Meanwhile, the Badgers — although holding the No. 1 ranking for the majority of the regular season — lost the spot to conference-rival Ohio State when the Buckeyes claimed a 2-1 victory in the WCHA Final FaceOff. Wisconsin played in the previous three national championship games, each time facing Ohio State, and won two of the three contests.

Wisconsin has a similar record to Penn State, currently 33-4-2. The Badgers, however, maintain a strong, 9-1 nonconference record. Wisconsin defeated No. 7 Quinnipiac, 6-0, in the Madison Regional Final game to move on to its fourth-consecutive Frozen Four.

Olympic gold medalists

Wisconsin sent five current players to the Olympic Games, four of whom took home the gold medal for Team USA alongside Tessa Janecke. The Nittany Lions sent three players to the 2026 Milano-Cortina Olympic Games: Janecke, Team Sweden’s Nicole Hall and Team Italy’s Matilde Fantin.

Janecke and her Olympic teammate Caroline Harvey are two of the top-three finalists for the 2026 Patty Kazmaier Memorial Award for the nation’s top player.

Harvey has tallied 62 points for the Badgers, split between 18 goals and 44 assists, sitting only behind Lacey Eden — who didn’t travel overseas — who’s accumulated 72 points. Janecke, Penn State’s top offensive producer, scored 24 goals and added 22 assists for 46 points this season.

Fellow skating Olympians, Team USA’s Kirsten Simms, Laila Edwards and Team Czechia’s Adela Sapovalivova rank in the top-seven point producers. Simms sits in third with 56 points while Edwards and Sapovalivova have tallied 42 and 32 points, respectively.

Wisconsin’s starting goaltender, Ava McNaughton, filled Team USA’s third-string role.

McNaughton and Penn State’s Katie DeSa are tied for first in the nation, each with a 1.27 goals-against average, while McNaughton holds a slight edge in save percentage with a .941 compared to DeSa’s .939. However, DeSa leads the nation with 12 shutouts in 30 games, while McNaughton put up eight shutouts in 30 games.

Underdogs take on blue bloods

Penn State has a short history in the national tournament, previously falling in the Regional Semifinals for the past three seasons. This season, with a mature senior class featuring Janecke, the Nittany Lions won their first NCAA Tournament game to advance to their first Frozen Four appearance.

The blue and white have only faced a single team from the WCHA this year: Ohio State. Inside the OSU Ice Rink in Columbus, Ohio, the Nittany Lions struggled to find their stride and were swept, 5-1 in Game 1 and 4-1 in Game 2, respectively.

While Penn State’s new standard became winning AHA Championships, the national success is a recent occurrence. On the other hand, with eight national championships, Wisconsin is the winningest program in collegiate women’s hockey history.

The Badgers have a history of the world’s top talent, dating back to the 2005-06 season when Wisconsin won its first national championship. The pool of talented alumni includes countless professionals such as Hilary Knight, who helped the Badgers take home the 2011 National Championship in Erie, Pennsylvania.

Penn State’s seeking to become a “blue blood” program, among the likes of Wisconsin, Minnesota and Ohio State, with a senior class that redefined the team’s standard.

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Steal her style | What we can learn from different female style icons
author: Avery Pelletier I The Daily Collegian
description:
Fashion is more than just an item of clothing. It’s been a means of self expression that transcends any language or cultural barrier throughout history.

Something as simple as a tailored dress, funky pair of shoes or the matching sweat-set you may see on the way to class can be a statement of agency, confidence or reinvention.

In honor of Women’s History Month, here are some of the most fashionable women of all time and how Penn State students can try and mimic their style.

Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis - More than a wife

Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis may have been the wife to one of the most famous presidents in history, but her role as a first lady went far beyond.

With her perfectly tailored suits, pill box hats and sets of pearls, Onassis’ style was one that captured the eyes of Americans when she stepped into the spotlight. With soft pastels and neutrals, every perfectly tailored piece embodied elegance.

While her pieces may have been beautiful, Onassis’ style was rooted in the fact that the simplicity of her pieces never took the attention off of her, giving her agency in how she was perceived.

While Onassis might have had access to some of the finest pieces, her style was more than just a brand or store. What we can learn from her fashion sense is that clothes do not make a person, but a person makes the clothes.

Stores like Lock downtown offer great classic pieces that allow a buyer to build up their wardrobe with staples, and someone who wants to emulate Onassis’ style should also focus on the importance of tailoring these pieces.

Eartha Kitt - Style meets sultry

This 1960s American singer may have been primarily known for her voice, but also for how she incorporated her vocal charisma into her style.

Kitt’s outfits were more than just form- fitting or traditionally risqué, but used color and materials to catch the attention of her audience. Often posed in items like fur coats, low cut dresses and iconic lurex bodysuits, her choices attracted focus in many rooms.

Her style choices were more than leopard and cheetah fur, they were statements made to show she was in control. Every “sultry” piece Kitt wore was a declaration of her self-expression.

What we can learn from Kitt’s style is that bold fashion choices can sometimes be what creates a persona.

Urban Outfitters has everything from form-fitting corsets to leather tops, and is one of many stores downtown that would allow a Penn State student to emulate this look.

Diana, Former Princess of Wales - Grace beyond just lace

Perhaps more than any of the women on this list, Diana, former Princess of Wales, shows it’s not what you wear that matters, but how you wear it. As for the late princess, the grace shown through her style went beyond just the lace she wore.

In her earlier time in the public’s eye, Princess Diana tended to stick to softer styles that were much more neutral and romantic. Her dresses, while modest, highlighted her softer beauty and made her seem more approachable to the children’s charities she participated in. Her outfits never overshadowed but instead amplified her compassion.

Princess Diana’s style teaches how to introduce our personalities to the world and be conscious of what this introduction says.

While there are many stores downtown that may have similar soft patterns as some of her dresses, to emulate her style it would be in Diana fashion to go to some sort of charity event or volunteering instead.

Sarah Jessica Parker - Model of maximalism

With a just as fashionable TV persona in Carrie Bradshaw, Sarah Jessica Parker’s bold ‘90s and 2000s style served as a vision for viewers that showed personal style can be tied into the city you’re from.

Unlike many of the other fashion models of her time, Parker’s style was defined by total maximalism. Much of her dresses, skirts and shirts were patterned and oftentimes clashed in a way that would turn many away. Paired with a statement necklace, funky bag or an unforgettable pair of shoes, Parker’s style continues to be so memorable because of all the risks she was willing to take.

Parker’s style shows boldness can be better, and sometimes fashion risks do have their payoffs.

Since her style is rooted in unique pieces that fit together like a puzzle, the best place downtown to try and achieve this style is in the racks at Uptown Cheapskate.

Rihanna - If you can’t break it, make it

The most modern style icon on the list, Rihanna’s fashion is like most trends today — everchanging.

In her early career, her style was very playful and tended to drift towards trendy low-rise jeans and traditional streetwear. As she started to gain more attention through her music, Rihanna pushed back against trends and created her own fashion moments.

Later in her career, Rihanna sought to fill a hole in the lingerie industry, due to the lack of diversity in the models and styles. She launched Savage X Fenty to allow as many women as possible to be reflected in their clothing.

Rihanna's style is an example to never be afraid to push back against trends or take control and create something new.

Stores like Target offer affordable pieces for those who want to experiment with their own style but, for those who are inspired by Savage X Fenty, Scraps & Skeins offers a variety of materials for original designs.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: ‘It requires a mix of empathy’ | Penn State student activists discuss their motivations
author: Annabella Chaklos I For the Collegian
description:
As political tensions grow across the United States, student activists have become motivated more than ever to advocate for their communities, their values and their country.

College campuses have historically been a central hub of student activism, Penn State is no exception. Immigration, the Israel-Hamas war and the Trump administration’s initiatives are among the central topics of student activism.

Keegan Jolly, the vice president of the PSU College Democrats, said she’s motivated to create spaces for students to become politically active however they can.

“I think our generation is really scared sometimes to be politically involved,” Jolly, a third-year studying criminology, said. “I want to make people aware that being politically active is okay, and actually, really important. The things that have been happening under this administration affects all of us.”

The College Democrats are committed to providing a space where students can safely express their beliefs and learn about political activism through hosting speakers, holding voter registration and supporting Democratic campaigns, according to Jolly.

Jolly said she’s been affected by the passing of The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which includes a revised student loan policy with fewer repayment options. She said can no longer pursue law school in the U.S. as a result.

As an aspiring disability rights lawyer, Jolly said she’s also concerned with resource cuts in the special education unit of the Department of Education, such as Individualized Education Programs (IEPs), as well as other legal processes that disabled students and their families have to face.

“My brother is on the autism spectrum, so I’ve seen a lot of the struggles my parents have had to go through,” Jolly said. “There is such a lack of empathy for people who are different. If we just provide resources, people can reach their full potential. We just need to meet them where they’re at and give them the best resources and tools to do that.”

Jolly also created an initiative within the College Democrats called the Community Organizer Voting Engagement Network (C.O.V.E.N.). It's an opportunity for students to protest through service if they are uncomfortable attending actual organized events themselves. The organization makes posters for protesters, holds food drives and participates in community service events.

“Being a student activist means everything,” Jolly said. “If I’m not going to do it, who is? I think we all have a special interest, an important topic, something in our hearts that is near and dear to us. I don’t expect to change the United States through my passion, but I can make a difference in at least one person’s life and that means everything.”

Collin Jones, the press secretary for the PSU College Republicans, said he believes being an activist is a responsibility of the younger generations.

“I think it is meaningful that anyone is willing to do this work, and to have iterations across the political spectrum,” Jones, a fourth-year studying political science, said. “Our generation is getting old enough to keep up the fight. Not everything is handed to us.”

Jones said he values educating others and keeping an open dialogue between opposing views, striving to keep a good and collaborative relationship with organizations like the College Democrats.

“The biggest takeaway is that all people should be willing to compromise,” Jones said. “Not one’s beliefs, but in finding a middle ground for policy and action.”

Steven McDonald, a member of Students for Justice in Palestine’s divestment team, said the most fulfilling thing about being an activist is seeing people’s willingness to come out and raise their voices against the rise of facism.

“Being a student activist means refusing to accept ‘That’s just how things are’ as a final answer,” McDonald, a first-year studying nursing, said. “It requires a mix of empathy for those being unheard and the courage to challenge the corrupt institutions that harm minorities when it’s uncomfortable.”

McDonald said the biggest action his organization has recently taken was a public comment campaign urging the Board of Trustees at Penn State to stop funding the Applied Research Laboratory which they allege contribute to the development of weapons.

“You don’t have to be a political science major to make a difference,” McDonald said. “The best way to start is to look to the intersection of what you love and what you believe. Using my own example, I’m a nursing student, and I believe that all people deserve health care, including Gazan citizens.”

McDonald said there’s immense value in being a worker bee who helps with flyers, outreach, research, asking questions and listening to people who have been doing the work.

“Activism is a marathon powered by the collaboration of people, not a solo sprint,” McDonald said. “All you need to start is a heart and some courage.”

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Women-focused STEM clubs provide safe spaces, remind students what they’re ‘capable of’
author: Darina Ayazbayeva I For the Collegian
description:
Penn State students pursuing degrees in STEM fields often encounter demanding coursework, competitive academic environments and challenging career paths. For many women studying in these fields, there’s the added experience of navigating classrooms and laboratories where men are often the majority.

While representation of women in STEM has improved over the years, gender disparities remain present in many areas.

“A lot of STEM majors are still very male dominated,” Aria Smith, Women in Stem’s president and co-founder, said. “We wanted to create a safe space where women in STEM could see that there are other girls on campus doing the same things.”

Women in STEM is a student-run club that brings together female students from a wide range of technical majors. The organization meets weekly and has grown significantly in recent years, now bringing together more than 200 members, according to Smith.

While Smith said representation of Women in STEM is gradually improving, she said student organizations play an important role in helping students feel more comfortable in environments where they may still be underrepresented.

“It’s important not only to point out where inequality exists, but also provide opportunities that help create more equality,” Smith said.

For many students, these communities provide reassurance that they’re not navigating their academic paths alone. Being surrounded by peers who share similar academic goals can help students feel more comfortable asking questions, seeking advice and discussing challenges.

Another organization working toward similar goals is the Society of Woman Engineers (SWE), which focuses on connecting women and studying engineering disciplines across campus.

Adelaide Orsetti, SWE’s director of equity recruitment, said the organization plays a crucial role in helping women feel supported in fields where they may sometimes sense they’re overlooked.

“Growing up, my brother was always seen as the ‘math person,’” Orsetti, a third-year studying industrial engineering, said. “Teachers would encourage him to take more technical classes, while I was often encouraged to take English or writing classes, even though I was good at math too.”

Experiences like these, she said, are one more reason organizations supporting women in STEM remain important. She said the clubs allow women to worry about the problem at hand rather than if they belong.

“In group projects sometimes you have to speak up a little more so your ideas are heard,” Orsetti said. “I feel like it’s important to spread awareness that these kinds of clubs exist and women know that there is a safe space for them.”

Today, SWE unites more than a thousand women in STEM fields on Penn State’s campus, according to Orsetti.

While some organizations focus broadly on supporting women in STEM fields, others aim to provide resources for students interested in specific disciplines, particularly within technology and computer science — like Girls Who Code College Loop, a student organization founded in June 2025 to support women and non-binary students interested in technology careers.

The Association of Women in Computing (AWC), focused on professional development, mentorship and community building for women studying computer science and related disciplines is another student group helping to bridge the gap.

Marilyn Jin, AWC’s president, said she’s personally experienced situations where being a woman in technology affected how her ideas were received.

“I have faced situations where I felt inferior and my voice wasn't accounted for in group projects,” Jin, a third-year studying computer science, said. “This really affected my self-confidence and made me question whether I was made out to be in the technology field, but I pushed through and am glad I stuck with it despite these setbacks.”

Through weekly meetings and events, AWC provides both social support and professional development opportunities for its 60 active members. Despite gradual progress in representation, Jin said gender inequality remains noticeable in many STEM classrooms and workplaces.

“There are so many times still that when I walk into a STEM class, there are at most three or four other women,” Jin said.

She also said highlighting gender inequality on campus is important because it helps raise awareness about the challenges women still face in technical fields.

“Unfortunately, we still face many scenarios in school and in professional life that make us feel inferior and unheard,” Jim said. “Having a safe space outside of those places can act as a reminder of what we are truly capable of.”

MORE CAMPUS CONTET

title: Ballot Breakdown: Four candidates up for Penn State trustees alumni election
author: Paloma Munoz | The Daily Collegian
description:
The upcoming Penn State Board of Trustees (BoT) alumni election will feature four candidates, following their approval by a board subcommittee in February. There are three open positions.

Voting begins at 9 a.m. on April 20 and ends on May 7 at 9 a.m. Penn State alumni will receive an email with the ballot as long as they have a valid email address on file with the university. They can also access the ballot at PennStateVotes.com.

Here’s a look at the candidates running.

Joseph S. DeRenzo

DeRenzo is the director of orthopedic anesthesia, director of quality improvement and chief of anesthesiology at the UPMC Magee Women’s Hospital.

If elected, DeRenzo plans to focus on improving Penn State’s standing in areas such as strategic planning, financial health, advocacy for students and alumni and educational oversight, according to candidate materials uploaded on trustees.psu.edu.

He received his bachelor's degree in 1997 and medical degree in 2001 from Penn State, and is currently part of the Eberly College of Science Mentorship Program. DeRenzo intends to use his health care background to bring “diverse perspectives” to solve challenges.

Karen Keller

Keller is currently a partner at Shaw Keller LLP. She received her bachelor's degree in biochemistry and molecular biology at Penn State in 2000 before attending law school at Temple University in 2003.

Keller’s materials state she wants to improve faculty success, and by proxy improve student success, by supporting competitive strategies and innovative teaching methods. For students, she wants to focus on more comprehensive support, educational opportunities and robust mental health resources.

Alexandra Krieger

Alexandra Krieger is one of the incumbent candidates on the ballot. Krieger is a former professional soccer player and a current ESPN analyst.

Krieger's candidate materials emphasize providing a safe learning environment for students, alongside pursuing fair wages and safe workplaces for employees.

Furthermore, her materials state she believes Penn State must support renewable energy education and research programs “guarantee academic excellence in the 21st century.”

She received degrees in public relations, advertising and applied communication at Penn State in 2007.

Joseph “Jay” V Paterno Jr.

Jay Paterno, an incumbent member of the BoT, wants to focus on restoring “reputational academic excellence,” according to his candidate materials.

Paterno is the president of Blue Line 409 LLC, a consulting and business venture firm. His platform is centered on being an independent voice that “raises hard questions about complex problems.” He wants to focus on Penn State’s legacy after his time, and advocates for the future of college sports.

MORE ADMINISTRATION COVERAGE

title: The types of students in every Penn State group project
author: Lexi Cannan | The Daily Collegian
description:
There’s a specific energy that takes over a classroom the second your professor announces a group project. It doesn’t matter if it’s a major course or a random Gen Ed you picked because it fit between two other classes. The mood shifts immediately.

You look around the room trying to make accidental eye contact with someone you could tolerate for the next month. Someone else is already packing up like they did not hear the words “group presentation.” Within 24 hours, a group chat is made and everyone’s role becomes clear.

Here are the personalities you’ll meet in every Penn State group project.

The overachiever

Before you even leave the classroom, this person has already created a Google Drive folder with labeled sections, a suggested outline and a working thesis statement. By that night, they’ve already found a few sources and made a plan for who should do what.

They say they’ve written a rough draft intro for the group, but it already sounds like a final product. It’s formatted in MLA or APA, perfectly cited and way more put together than expected. While everyone else is still confused about the assignment, they’re already going through the rubric to make sure everything is included.

They’re not trying to intimidate anyone. They just genuinely function like this. You feel slightly stressed, a little impressed and mostly relieved that someone is taking control. Without them, the project might fall apart. With them, you’re almost guaranteed at least a 95.

The in-person advocate

The second the group chat is created, this person suggests meeting in-person. Not once, not twice, but constantly. They send messages like, “Maybe we should meet at the HUB-Robeson Center to go over ideas,” “Should we meet in the library to review the outline?” and “Do you guys want to meet again before we submit?”

At first it feels organized, but after a few ideas, the group goes silent. Everyone’s schedules are different, so the chat turns into trying to find a time that works.

Most of the time, the meeting doesn’t happen, or only two people come. The irony is that the entire project could have been finished in the document the whole time.

The neutral party

When it’s time to divide the work, this person leans back and says they’re fine with anything. They truly have no preference. They don’t care if they write the introduction, handle the research or design the slides. At first, this feels like the easiest group member to have. There are no arguments and no dramatic debates over who gets which section.

After a few minutes, you realize they’re waiting for very specific instructions. If you say, “Can you do part two?” they’ll agree immediately, but part two could mean five different things. They need the word count, the number of sources and possibly a mini outline. Without details, they’re calm but confused.

They’ll complete their section and upload it right before the deadline with a short message letting everyone know it’s done. The work isn’t bad and it’s not groundbreaking. It gets the job done.

They’re not chaotic and not intense. They’re just there, contributing enough to secure the grade and trusting the group project will somehow work itself out.

The ghost

Every group project has one. The ghost starts off strong, replying in the group chat within seconds and agreeing to every plan. They react to messages, say they’re excited to get started and maybe even suggest meeting up at the HUB. For a brief moment, you think this might be the most communicative group you’ve ever had.

Then suddenly, they disappear. Messages sit unread, the shared document shows no new edits under their name and their last contribution was adding their name to the title slide. At some point, someone in the group quietly asks, “Wait, is this person still in our class?”

They usually reappear the night before the deadline with an apology and a paragraph clearly written in five minutes. The message usually includes something about being busy or missing the messages.

Somehow, they’re surprised the project is due, even though it’s been on the syllabus since the first week.

The real MVP

This is the most underrated role. They are combining everyone's sections into one document, fixing the formatting and making sure half the paper is not in Times New Roman while the other half is in Arial. They check citations, adjust spacing and fix the moment when someone accidentally writes their first section in first person.

They don’t stand out, but they catch all the small mistakes. Without them, it would look like everyone just threw their parts together.

By the end of the semester, group projects feel less like assignments and more like social experiments. You start as random classmates and somehow learn everyone’s habits and how late they procrastinate.

It’s chaotic, slightly disorganized and almost always includes a late-night message asking if everyone finished their part — usually sent about three hours before the deadline.

MORE BLOGS

title: Penn State women’s gymnastics hits road for Big Ten Championships
author: Sami Altmire | The Daily Collegian
description:
End of season is right around the corner with the Big Ten Championships just on the horizon, taking place in Champaign, Illinois, on Saturday. This season seemed to fly by at a rapid pace for the Nittany Lions, with quad and tri-meets happening almost every week, and massive matchups with gymnastic giants like LSU.

This meet will be a mix of teams that are just as strong as Penn State and have just as much talent needed to win a title. The Nittany Lions will face Iowa, Maryland and Ohio State on Saturday, and out of the three, the blue and white has lost to two of them.

Penn State recently fell to the Buckeyes, losing 197.150-196.550 on March 8. A loss to Iowa came earlier in the season, falling 197.075-196.525 on Feb. 21. A win against Maryland is the only cushion going into this meet that the Nittany Lions have, so a reevaluation of what the team looked like against those two losses is necessary.

Ohio State came out on top of the last two tri-meets it's been a part of, and it has fared well against other Big Ten teams. As of now, the Buckeyes have a 10-6 overall record and a 4-5 record within the conference.

No. 23 Ohio State has a loss against Iowa. The Buckeyes have been working on staying consistent throughout the season in regard to their top performances, and events like the beam could be their strongest event of the meet. Beam has historically not been the Nittany Lions' strongest apparatus, so Ohio State could use that to stay on top of Penn State.

Iowa is No. 26 in the rankings, and the only team in this meet that has not lost to a fellow team. The Hawkeyes have a 14-6 overall record, only losing in the conference to Michigan, Minnesota and UCLA.

Iowa has had consistently strong showings on the bars this season, with team scores being pretty solid and consistent all season long. Bars are usually where Penn State shines the brightest, so this could mean that there will be some competitive spirit around that apparatus on Saturday.

The final part of this meet, Maryland, is the only team partaking in this weekend that the Nittany Lions have a leg up on. The Terrapins have been involved in quite a few tri-meets over the past few weeks, coming out on top in all of them. However, none of these teams are fellow Big Ten members. Maryland tends to struggle inside the conference, so this weekend might be a mountain for​​ them.

Maryland, sitting at No. 27 and having a record of 19-6 overall and 3-6 in conference, could benefit from truly laying it all out there this weekend. The floor and vault seem to be comfortable for the Terrapins with pretty consistent numbers all season. Maryland has a chance to switch things up and create some chances for itself.

This weekend could be a total toss-up with the Nittany Lions being ranked the highest, but also facing some struggles throughout the season. The blue and white are not the strongest team heading into the Big Ten Championships, but it has the capability to persevere and end up with a title.

MORE GYMNASTICS COVERAGE

title: Key position battles on Penn State’s offense headed into spring ball
author: Lyle Alenstein | Wrestling Editor
description:
It’s a new look offense for Penn State for the 2026 season, compared to last season, the quarterback, running backs, wide receivers and four of the five offensive linemen from the starting lineup are gone.

With this in mind, there’s a trio of positions that project to have competition ahead of spring practice.

Running back

For the first time in four years, Penn State will have starting backs not named Nick Singleton and Kaytron Allen running the show in the backfield.

In turn, the Nittany Lions turned to the portal to bring in Iowa State transfer Carson Hansen and James Peoples from Ohio State. Matt Campbell said at his National Signing Day media availability that the tandem can be a “great one-two punch.”

Hansen is a two-time Gatorade Player of the Year in Minnesota and was the starting tailback for the Cyclones the past two seasons, accumulating 1,769 yards and 19 touchdowns on the ground. In 2025, he was a second-team All-Big 12 selection and ran for over 100 yards in the final five games of the year.

“Just got to go into it one day at a time, and you got to keep preparing like you're going to be the guy,” Hansen said. “Everyone in the running back room, that’s the goal. You want to be the guy, and that's what we're going to strive to do.”

Peoples gets a fresh start in Happy Valley after playing behind Bo Jackson and West Virginia transfer CJ Donaldson at Ohio State. He was still efficient with his touches last season, notching 61 carries for 344 yards and three touchdowns.

“I think in my game I can do everything,” Peoples said at the end of February. “I don’t think there’s no weakness in my game. But there is absolutely everywhere I can definitely improve, and that’s what I look forward to doing.”

While the duo received buzz from Campbell, he made it clear there’s another running back still in the mix for the starting job: Quinton Martin Jr., who finished the year with 20 carries for 101 yards in the Pinstripe Bowl despite not receiving a single carry in the regular season.

Campbell said he’s “determined to do special things.” He is also challenging him to go from 195 pounds to 220 by the season — he’s currently listed at 205 on the roster page.

“I love Penn State. I bleed Penn State,” Martin said. “I've been here for two years now, and I love everything about it.”

Center

After two years of Nick Dawkins starting at center, Penn State will need to find his replacement. The two names in contention for the job are Texas State transfer Brock Riker and returner Dominic Rulli.

Riker is coming off a successful 2025 campaign, being named a freshman All-American by The Athletic. He was then highly sought after in the transfer portal, receiving offers from Alabama, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Utah and more, per Steve Wiltfong of On3.

“I'm scrappy. I play within the rules, but I'm scrappy,” Riker said. “I'm gonna outwill you … I'm gonna be the same level of intensity, urgency on and off the field.”

Rulli enters 2026 having been the backup to Dawkins last year in addition to serving as the punt protector on special teams.

While it could appear that Riker was brought in to man the starting spot, Rulli isn’t giving up that easily. He started in his first game at center in the Pinstripe Bowl and played well. He believes he can return to that role.

“I want to be the starter. I don't think to anybody that's a shock for me to say that,” Rulli said. “I want to compete. I want to prove it. I don't want to be given anything and say it's yours. I want to prove that I'm the guy.”

Left tackle

Drew Shelton is gone, so Penn State needs to find someone to protect Rocco Becht’s blindside.

Anthony Donkoh, who has experience starting at right guard and right tackle, will likely slot in at right tackle this year as he and Garrett Sexton discussed at the THON Explorers Program in February.

As a result of this, that leaves the left tackle competition between Sexton and Owen Aliciene.

Sexton was the backup right tackle last year behind Nolan Rucci, who appeared in all 13 games last year between special teams. He saw some work at left tackle in game action early in the year, and spent some time training there at practice occasionally.

“I'm more familiar with everything now, especially being a guy that came back, I know how things work,” Sexton said. “What I've been doing lately, kind of trying to settle in now, covering things and trying to step into more of a leadership role.”

Aliciene appeared in one game during his freshman campaign, and was the third-string left tackle behind Shelton and Eagan Boyer, who has since transferred to Florida.

While Aliciene was in a limited role, there was praise for him throughout his first year as a Nittany Lion, and he could take advantage of the turnover in the offensive line room.

All of us have been working our asses off recently, all winter,” Aliciene said. “I definitely think that there's room for me to capitalize on that opportunity.”

MORE FOOTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball hosts first home weekend series against Wisconsin
author: Brielle Gari I The Daily Collegian
description:
After almost two months into the 2026 softball season, Penn State will play at home at Nittany Softball Park and Beard Field for its Big Ten home opener. The blue and white face off against Wisconsin over the course of the weekend, with three games.

The Nittany Lions are currently 19-9 overall so far this season. They went on a three-game losing streak after facing No. 18 Oregon for their first Big Ten away weekend series.

Before facing a Big Ten powerhouse, the blue and white traveled to Salem, Virginia, to take on four different nonconference opponents in the Hokie Invite, after the original home series was canceled. Going 2-2 over the course of the weekend, Penn State fell short to James Madiso and then-No. 11 Virginia Tech.

The Nittany Lions will take on Wisconsin for a three-game home series, with the first pitch of Game 1 set for 5 p.m. Friday.

Penn State continues its second weekend of Big Ten play against the Badgers.

Wisconsin currently sits at the bottom of the Big Ten conference, with an 0-3 record and a 14-10 overall record for its 2026 campaign. The blue and white are tied at the bottom of the standings, also with a 0-3 record in Big Ten play.

Holding a better overall record, Penn State has also suffered three consecutive losses.

Friday’s game will be the 59th meeting between the programs, with the Nittany Lions leading the Badgers 32-27. The last time the teams met was for a series in 2024. The blue and white went 2-1in their trip to Madison, Wisconsin

Leading the Badgers' offense is sophomore Kendra Lewis, with a .437 batting average. Wisconsin’s catcher, Hilary Blomberg, takes the top slot in homers with seven on the season, also being one of the top producers with 28 RBI.

In the circle, freshman right-handed pitcher Berritt Herr has led the Badgers with 45 strikeouts so far this season. Herr entered in the third game against No. 8 UCLA, silencing three straight batters and allowing zero runs.

Penn State has recently struggled to maintain its early leads. For the Nittany Lions, superstar freshman third baseman Allison Onearce has been the one to start an offensive fire. She’s tied with junior infielder Brooke Klosowicz with ten home runs this season.

Klosowicz leads the blue and white with a .362 batting average. She looks to have a chance to follow up on her historic sophomore season.

After a lengthy road start to the season, the Nittany Lions return home with hopes to crawl up the Big Ten charts and to get back in the top 25 rankings. It all comes down to how their performance will be against a Big Ten foe with a home crowd and how the Nittany Lions perform for the first time in Happy Valley.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Off the record | Sexism in the city
author: Miss Behave | The Daily Collegian
description:
Editor’s Note: Off the record is a weekly column written by a third-year Collegian staffer writing under an alias. It aims to promote a space for honest conversations about love, sex and relationships. Those interested in sharing their experiences or submitting a topic are invited to do so through this anonymous form.

Dearest reader,

Welcome back to State College. I hope your spring break was equal parts sunshine, questionable decisions and stories that will live forever in the group chat.

Get the Instagram photo dump post out of your system. Soft-launch the tan. Pretend you didn’t just spend half the week horizontal tanning — sleeping or participating in other activities.

This week, I have a special treat. One of you slid into my inbox. So naturally, I’m stepping into my role as your resident guide on all things love and sex to share my perspective.

Samantha says she enjoys dating freely as men do, but is frustrated by the double standard and feels she’s judged too harshly for engaging in behavior that would be accepted if she were a man.

First of all, welcome to your Samantha era! For those of you who haven’t had the pleasure of meeting Samantha or Carrie from the hit show “Sex and the City,” here’s the rundown.

The difference between being a Carrie and being a Samantha mostly comes down to time management. Carrie spends three days wondering if she should text him back. Samantha spends those same three days going on two better dates and forgetting the last name of the guy who couldn’t even give a decent answer to her most basic questions.

In a Samantha era, you stop asking “Does he like me?” and start asking, “Do I like him enough to put on jeans for this?” In other words, Samantha has little patience for nonsense. She’s the main character, fully in charge of her own love life and exploring all that love, life and sex have to offer.

With that being said, I have a couple of thoughts to share.

You’re not imagining the double standard. When men date around, it’s considered “playing the field” or “being a player,” or even “impressive.” But when women date around, it’s often framed as “desperate” or “slut behavior” or even labeling one as a “whore.”

The truth is, a lot of people are very comfortable with women being desirable; that’s how we’re supposed to present ourselves. But people are far less comfortable when women choose to act on that desirability.

And here’s the funny part: half the people judging you would probably be doing the same thing if they had the opportunity or the confidence you do.

Also, a quick reminder — the same society that labels women harshly for dating freely also produces an entire economy of reality dating shows, romantic comedies and wedding Pinterest boards. So clearly, everyone is fascinated by the love process. They just get weird when women are honest about participating in it.

So, my advice? Date. Be kind. Be clear about your intentions. And if someone calls you a “whore” for doing exactly what men do, feel free to remind them that if you were a man, they’d probably be asking for tips or giving you a high five.

The double standard that exists is wild. When a man sleeps around, he’s called a player. When a woman behaves in a similar way, she’s judged for it. Same behavior, completely different narrative. The difference is in who society decided to hand the trophy to.

Personally, I say the field is wide open. Play the game. There’s nothing wrong with letting a few new players step into tryouts and seeing who you really click with. Take your time, enjoy the tension. And hey, if the opportunity feels right, maybe even switch teams for a night and see if you hit a home run.

Samantha, thank you for writing to me and I hope I was able to offer you some advice, or at least some comedic relief. I sympathize with you and the judgment you’re facing. Remember, you have the right to play the field if that’s what you wish to do. Just be safe, respectful and responsible while doing it.

After all, every player needs a teammate.

Yours truly,

Miss Behave

MORE COLUMNS

title: Local activist groups protest ICE activity at Centre County Courthouse
author: Jacob Godshall | The Daily Collegian
description:
A large group of protesters gathered outside the Centre County Courthouse in Bellefonte to protest United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) activity in the area.

The event was organized by People’s Defense Front, Student Committee for Defense and Solidarity and Centre County Rapid Response Network, in response to an incident that occurred March 4, when a worker was taken by ICE outside of the courthouse while waiting for his hearing.

“I think we need to make our thoughts known that what is happening with ICE is not okay,” Brenda Palmer, a Bellefonte resident, said. “You know, I think we all need to defend the Constitution and the whole idea that the government is all of a sudden anti-Constitution is ridiculous.”

Protesters came together on the steps of the Centre County Courthouse, chanting and holding signs with slogans like “Melt ICE,” “Democracy dies in silence” and “shut down Moshannon,” an ICE facility in nearby Clearfield County.

Palmer attended the protest wielding an umbrella with words that read “ICE: Lawless murderous thugs.”

Palmer also said protesting in local areas is very important, as community members should do whatever they can to support these kinds of movements.

“Look what happened in Minnesota,” Palmer said. “It wasn't just Minneapolis, it was out in the outer areas also. I mean I work, and I just got off of work. This is where I could go.”

Similarly, community member Laura Stephenson said she’s “fed up” with both ICE and the current federal administration.

“We feel very strongly about the administration right now, and we very much disagree absolutely with what they are doing, what Trump is doing, and very much oppose the illegal actions of ICE, who unfortunately don't seem to be trained well enough and are disregarding due process and the rights of citizens,” Stephenson said.

Stephenson said it’s important for anybody who disagrees to “get out,” and make their feelings, and their local communities’ feelings, known.

“We can't go to Washington every day and protest, so this is our way of doing something,” Stephenson said. “We must do something absolutely to show our opposition.”

Stephenson was joined by fellow community member Joe Thomas, who said ICE needs to stay out of Bellefonte, and across America.

“It's gone way too far, and it's resulted in deaths, and it's now got the entire country riled up to the point where, I'm retired, I should be home watching the TV, but I'm out here because we need to be out here,” Thomas said. “We the people need to be out here.”

The protest stayed at the courthouse for some time, giving speeches and chanting as cars rolled by, with many drivers honking in support of the protesters. The organizers led the group into the street, marching down W High Street and across Veterans Bridge into a grass lot next to the bridge.

During the march, two police cruisers pulled up behind the protest, lights on, and told the protest to leave the streets and move to the sidewalk. The organizers yelled that they would do so in a “timely manner,” and marched down the street and across the bridge before leaving the road.

In the grass, protesters continued to chant as speakers criticized the local police for allegedly assisting ICE operations in Bellefonte. The two police cruisers stayed parked next to the grass for some time before one left, and the other pulled down a side street and watched from a different area.

Another Bellefonte resident, Nathanael Keats, has had enough of ICE activity in Bellefonte and the surrounding area.

“I think, in my beliefs, it's gotten to a head to where I feel like I need to get involved, not just with voting, but like showing public support for the opposition, essentially, of what happened here in Bellefonte,” Keats said.

Keats also wants to make his community feel more safe and unified.

He said it’s tricky to point to a specific action he wants local government and law enforcement to pursue, because many of his grievances are with the federal government, but called for less cooperation for the local police with ICE agents regardless. He said he would like to see police officers “not just blindly following orders.”

He added he was protesting in opposition of local workers being "ripped out" from the community.

“Being out here as a community to show support and for the people who live here, I think that's important,” Keats said. “I think it's important for the local community to have a sense of belonging and that we can feel safe.”

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Wrestling coaches across country discuss NIL ahead of NCAA Championships
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
CLEVELAND — One sunrise before the first whistle blows to start the NCAA Championships, four head coaches sat at a podium with cameras in their faces, discussing the sport that unites them. All four coaches sit in drastically different tiers within the world of wrestling and in different parts of the country, but they share a similar disgruntledness about the rise of NIL (name, image and likeness) and the lack of regulation disrupting the sport.

Ohio State’s Tom Ryan has been coaching the Buckeyes for two decades. In that time, he has watched the college sports world shift to the current marketplace. Back in the 2000s, college athletes were barely allowed to be paid for any activities, let alone receive the large endorsements they do now.

While Ryan supported allowing athletes to be compensated for work, the system has shifted too far the other way for his liking.

“There was a time when I was fighting for our student athletes to be able to earn more pay at summer camps,” Ryan said. “Now, it’s here, and having a tremendous impact on the sport.”

Each coach at the table had a drastically different view on the subject. Kent State’s Josh Moore hasn’t had to handle much NIL during his coaching tenures at his current school and the now defunct Cleveland State program. His goal for coaching at small schools that will be unable to invest heavily like the programs sitting beside him is to find local kids, and give them the best college experience possible to keep a sustainable system in place.

“These teams are competing for national championships,” Moore said. “I’m competing to get my guys here and set them up for life, to build a relationship and put them in a better spot than when they arrived.”

Oregon State’s Chris Pendleton lies closer to Moore than Ryan or Taylor on the NIL front, coming from a program lacking the resources of either of the aforementioned schools, but still facing the pressures of a larger university and its athletic department.

“I think a lot of times the stress of winning and performing and producing, appeasing donors, alumni, administration, has ripped bare what we're here to do,” Pendleton said. “We're here to impact kids' lives and give them better opportunities and help them get their degrees and have a great experience.”

Pendleton has had a relatively negative experience dealing with NIL this season. In his opening statement, Pendleton mentioned not expecting to lose an All-American from his roster, an apparent dig at Ohio State that Ryan addressed during his answer.

Ethan Stiles transferred to Ohio State to move closer to home, according to Ryan. Since that time, he went 15-5 en route to the No. 7 seed in this weekend’s tournament. While addressing the situation, Ryan referenced Rocco Welsh transferring to Penn State under similar circumstances. Ryan described the transfer situations as “unfortunate.”

As the discussion continued, Day emphasized the importance of loyalty, revealing that No. 1 141-pounder Jesse Mendez received multiple transfer offers to leave the Buckeyes, but chose to stay home for his senior season.

“This makes (Mendez) an incredible leader and very loyal, not to behoove anybody because they gave you a lot of money,” Ryan said.

Out of the four coaches, the one most inclined to using the transfer portal is Oklahoma State’s David Taylor. The former Nittany Lion wrestler and coach has had two busy offseasons since taking over in Stillwater, utilizing NIL to quickly build up his roster to a contender. While Taylor competed in a different financial landscape prior to coaching, he knows how to play the cards that he’s dealt.

“When I was recruited, schools had their 9.9 scholarships and that’s all it was,” Taylor said. “Time evolves and you have to evolve with the times.”

Even though he is not averse to NIL, Taylor continues emphasizing the importance of real leadership that Moore and Pendleton discussed. Rather than offering a blank check and success, Taylor focuses on teaching as much as coaching.

“What’s really important is preparing kids for life after wrestling and life after college,” Taylor said. “It's that balance of gratitude and instant gratification and delayed gratification and constantly trying to give them a good path.”

Regardless of how much each coach uses NIL and the transfer portal, they all acknowledge that it is here to stay and success in the NCAA will be defined by how each program handles the formerly underground discussions about pay-to-play.

“The teams and organizations that understand that and work hard to raise money and build up their support base are going to get a better chance at winning,” Ryan said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘It means a lot to me’ | Penn State's Levi Haines shows gratitude ahead of final NCAA Championships
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
CLEVELAND — Levi Haines will step onto the mat for the final time in his Penn State career, closing a chapter that has flown by in four seasons.

The 174-pounder is Penn State’s lone senior starter, and took more of a team perspective when reflecting on his season and career rather than his individual performance.

“We have a lot of young guys in the lineup, and they’ve been taking care of business, getting better, so it’s been fun to watch those guys get better along with everybody else,” Haines said. “I think we’re right where we need to be headed here into the national championship.”

Penn State has the chance to set more history over the weekend, with seven wrestlers seeded No. 1 in their respective weight classes. Led by Haines as the veteran, the Nittany Lions have that team comradery and leadership needed to hit those marks.

“I think each and every one of us kind of comes in with the goal of being ourselves and staying true to our character throughout these tournaments,” Haines said. “The big moments, it’s easy to stray away from who you are and let that stuff kind of come in a little bit, so that’s our main focus this weekend, just being ourselves and competing hard.”

Not only is Penn State setting high marks, but the state of Pennsylvania continues to exceed any other state in terms of wrestlers’ hometowns. Pennsylvania has 50 wrestlers qualified, almost doubling the next states, New Jersey and Ohio with 28.

Haines, a Pennsylvania native, and some teammates were able to catch some of the PIAA State Wrestling Championships which featured some of the sport’s future stars.

“It’s really cool to be a part of,” Haines said. “I think it’s really neat to see the history and heritage of wrestling in the state of Pennsylvania. It seems like it keeps on getting better and better.”

When Haines made his way to Penn State from Biglerville High School, he had the unique decision to make with the coaching staff on whether or not to redshirt.

Not only did he not redshirt his first year, Haines wrestled four straight seasons, not using his redshirt at all. In a sport like wrestling, that isn’t usual even for some of the sport’s all-time greats.

“My coaches and I decided that at the time, we thought I was ready to go, and I knew they had my best interest in mind,” Haines said. “So just listen to them, and I feel like at Penn State, if you can get in the lineup, you go wrestle.”

Because of wrestling four straight seasons, it led to a quick collegiate career for the Nittany Lion. Haines received some high praise from his coach, Cael Sanderson, who called him “pretty special” and “a guy that we will miss as much as anyone that we’ve ever had come through here” ahead of his final regular season dual at Rec Hall.

Haines showed appreciation to those sentiments, wanting to return the favor to the coaching staff who has helped him win a national title and put him in a strong position to earn his second one. In doing so, he hinted at sticking around in State College to train with the Nittany Lion Wrestling Club following his graduation, something many wrestlers do to train with some of the top wrestlers in the world.

“It means a lot to me,” Haines said. “It means I was doing my job going through school, and hopefully I can repay (Sanderson) a little bit of what he’s given to me and along with our other coaches. I look forward to hopefully being able to give back to him in some way in the future, and I don’t plan on going anywhere anytime soon.”

As his final NCAA Championships begin, the feelings and belief from the Arendtsdville, native remain the same. No matter the stage, no matter the opponent, Haines wants to continue to be himself and let that lead him to success on and off the mat.

“Just getting better each time I stepped on the mat,” Haines said. “It’s what I plan on doing this weekend, not too worried about finishing off my career with the national championship. Hopefully my career is a lot longer than this weekend.”

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: ‘I’m still here’ | How former Penn State RB Nick Singleton handles pre-draft process with broken foot
author: Lexie Linderman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Nick Singleton’s 2025 didn’t go the way he hoped. He fell short of the Penn State rushing record that he was once certain he would break, and he finished his senior season with a career-low 549 rushing yards.
