title: Column | Hooking up isn’t harmless
author: Julia Kushner | The Daily Collegian
description:
College students often lament the infamous “hook-up culture” permeating our campuses. And for good reason.

The most intimate of experiences has also become the most casual. If it sounds oxymoronic, that’s because it is. The mixing of alcohol and lowered inhibitions with something so deeply personal is, unsurprisingly, often a poor mix.

But in the same breath, many of these students extol the supposed virtues that said culture provides. You’ll often hear claims of female freedom, character growth, or even a simple test drive before you purchase the real thing.

And while we’re on the topic of metaphors, we should probably stop being surprised when the benefactors of hook-up culture don’t buy the cow.

In my own experiences with friends, I hear the same lines about female empowerment and hook-up culture in public, and then hear the sadness and disappointment these hook-ups have caused in private.

What’s most telling about this phenomenon isn’t necessarily who participates in it, as it’s pushed everywhere from TV shows to campus events, but the dueling narratives it creates in front of and behind closed doors.

Why, when young people are more starved for genuine connection than ever before, do we continue to play into the most superficial of all connections? Why do we trade a singular moment of possible pleasure for the much longer-lasting pain of regret?

I often hear the (also oxymoronic) phrase “harmless hook-up.” There’s simply no such thing.

I could bore you with ample statistics to back this up, but the most incriminating evidence of all is that those who claim harmlessness continue to speak out of both sides of their mouths. This doublespeak is used more often than not.

For example, Susan Kraus, a therapist, wrote that collegiate women involved in hook-up culture share that they both love the feeling of being picked by a man, and also feel utterly objectified in a column published in the Medium. She also said that men enjoy this culture simply because they can, but then also end up dissatisfied.

An article in The Atlantic titled “ Boys on the Side” published in 2012 calls hook-up culture “an engine of female progress — one being harnessed and driven by women themselves,” while also sharing stories of women being actively degraded by others sharing photos of their sexual activity and lamenting hearing men share their sexual escapades pursuant to this culture’s norms.

Even liberal collegiate women’s publications like Her Campus aren’t immune to this paradox. Authors both praise the liberality of being able to be with anyone at any time and then bemoan the obvious consequences of such actions, like STDs and decreased self-worth.

In an age of supposedly radical ideas that are really just the same old sexual liberation talking points, try something truly radical: save yourself for someone who’s worth it when the time is right.

The positives are endless. And so are the negatives of hooking up.

MORE COLUMNS

title: Column | Why people love raunchy television
author: Emma Strickling | The Daily Collegian
description:
Just last month, Netflix released the second half of Bridgerton’s latest season. Within this book-to-screen adaptation, there’s plenty of drama and, of course, a lavish ball or two. However, probably the most commonly associated aspect of the show is its raunchy scenes.

According to Variety, season four amassed 39.7 million views within its first four days of being released. Bridgerton is one of Netflix's most popular shows, with seasons one and three ranked seventh and ninth, respectively, on their most-watched list of all time.

Similar patterns have been seen with other shows that are quite steamy, such as “Heated Rivalry,” which chronicles the love story of two closeted male hockey players. By the time the final episode of the show was released, the amount of streaming minutes grew to 324 million streaming minutes in comparison to its debut week figure of 30 million minutes.

Raunchy television seems to be incredibly popular, but why?

It’s clear that sex sells, whether you think that’s immoral or not. Shows on the raunchier side get people talking, either through conversations with friends or in social media comment sections.

While perhaps viewers come by these shows by chance, they could also be seeking them out to fill a void — especially Gen Z viewers. Recent data trends show that Gen Z is engaging less in sexual activity and is also dating less. Roughly 24% of adults 18 to 29 had abstained from sex within the past year, according to USA Today, a figure that’s doubled since 2010. While adults under 30 had the highest percentage of a single population at 47%, according to Forbes.

Perhaps these types of shows act as a way to still engage with sex without having to actually engage with it in their personal lives. The dating world poses many risks, which the supposedly risk-averse Gen Z are trying to avoid. Therefore, these shows offer a sense of control.

It’s acting as an alternative, especially since these shows often depict intimacy more naturally and authentically.

While I can’t speak for the shows I haven’t seen, from the shows I am familiar with, like “Bridgerton” and “Heated Rivalry,” there’s more than hot, steamy sex scenes.Once the passion has cooled down, what’s left is a relationship between two people. A relationship of yearning and emotional connection. Which is, unfortunately, something not present in every relationship.

These shows are an escape from a relationship that’s not hitting the bar and into a relationship that the viewer might desire in some aspect. In that long duration, it’s almost as if the viewer is able to be present in the world in front of them and forget about the problems of their own love life.

Once the show is over, it’s back to reality. Which might be why whenever the latest raunchy show comes out, it continues to have a fairly large following until after it ends.

Although it’s a place for an escape, these TV shows ultimately can’t develop the social skills or the same feelings that real-life relationships do. While it might fill a void temporarily, it’s not a successful long-term solution.

If there’s an actual desire to date, but fear is overshadowing it, take a deep breath. Consider what is causing that fear. Is it worth not taking your shot or even simply just trying?

Unlike these TV shows that will be available in some form or another, the opportunities to make something happen romantically aren’t always going to be. Sometimes you just have to take a leap of faith. But even if there’s rejection, at least you’ll know instead of always wondering “what if.”

MORE COLUMNS

title: State College Borough, Penn State collaborate on local sustainability efforts
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
Through borough policies and partnerships with Penn State and other organizations, State College has taken several steps to advance sustainability initiatives over the years.

Peter Buck, the director for education for Penn State Sustainability (PSS), said the borough has already passed several resolutions to aid local sustainability efforts, such as Resolution 944, which declared the borough a climate protection community.

Buck said State College and the Sustainable Communities Collaborative (SCC), an outreach and scholarship program within PSS, have worked together on several projects for over a decade from promoting bicycle and pedestrian safety to solar assessments.

Many of the projects are displayed during the Campus and Community Sustainability Expo, held at the end of each semester. This year’s expo is on April 30 in the State College Municipal Building. Buck said many of the projects have had a sizable impact on State College residents even if they weren’t aware of them.

“The corner of Park Avenue and Atherton Street is notoriously dangerous and part of it has to do with pedestrian and not car traffic and so (the SCC) organized a group of classes, three in a row, that worked on messaging to make sure that pedestrians and bicyclists are safe and you might think, ‘Well, why is that sustainability?’” Buck said. “Well, health and well-being are part of sustainability to transportation-related issues.”

He said collaborations such as these help maintain good relations between the borough and the university.

“It is great to have this way of bringing a few hundred students a year, actually every semester, in touch with the borough in an incredibly positive, socially, educationally and economically productive way,” Buck said.

Buck said the borough also participates in larger sustainability and climate actions in collaboration with organizations like the Centre Region Council of Governments (CRCOG).

Most notably, State College has worked with the CRCOG on projects like the Centre Region Climate Action and Adaptation Plan, meant to make the borough and other involved municipalities more sustainable through various means such as land management and waste reduction.

The borough also planned on implementing infrastructure as part of the Solar Power Purchase Agreement (SPPA). The SPPA aimed to increase the presence of solar power in Centre County as a way to maintain low energy costs and expand renewable energy sources both in and around the borough.

However, Prospect 14, the company that developed the SPPA, raised concerns of economic inviability due to the repealing of economic incentives related to solar power by the federal government, alongside tariffs on solar panels. As a result, the borough voted unanimously to terminate its contract with the company set to provide electricity from the SPPA during a council meeting on March 5

Buck — who contributed to the SPPA as a member of its working group — said he believed the efforts by the federal government in curbing sustainability measures will only inspire local government officials to do more to accomplish sustainability goals.

“If anything, there is more willpower in the State College area because of the challenges brought from Washington,” Buck said. “People will find a way forward on sustainability and climate action.”

Buck said the SPPA is the only sustainability program affected in the region by federal administrative changes or policies.

According to Ilona Ballreich, SCC’s program director, its relationship with State College is mutually beneficial, as the borough gets the resources needed to maintain sustainability and students are given opportunities to get in-the-field experience and gain community connections.

“Let's say community partners are interested in solar but they don't really have the staff in-house, they don't have a solar engineer or a solar expert,” Ballreich said. “I can then work with a group of students in a solar design class, who have the technical knowledge.”

Ballreich said it’s important for the university and the borough to continue to work together on the issue of sustainability in order to best serve both residents and students.

“At the end of the day, I hope that we all want to make the world a better place for everybody, so that's kind of my mantra,” Ballreich said. “That's what SCC likes to do and tries to do … make the world the best it can possibly be for all involved.”

State College Borough Council Member Gopal Balachandran, who also represents the borough on the CRCOG’s climate action and sustainability committee, said the dialogue between the borough and Penn State is helpful for planning sustainability initiatives.

“We end up talking about various efforts to promote awareness to also be able to gain greater climate and resilience,” Balachandran said. “A lot of it is just policy-based work as well as educational work involving things like solar power, so we work with the university on a lot of those things.”

Balachandran said one of the borough’s current projects is attempting to conduct a greenhouse gas inventory to measure emissions produced in State College.

He said he believes the borough is at an advantage due to Penn State’s willingness to assist with sustainability efforts.

“They have so many experts at the university, and so we're able to draw upon that expertise, especially when we have limited staff resources,” Balachandran said. “So many of us are involved in the university in one form or another, and I think we're very lucky to have that.”

MORE BOROUGH COVERAGE

title: Review | Bruno Mars returns as ‘The Romantic’
author: Brennan Kramp I The Daily Collegian
description:
Bruno Mars recently released his fifth studio album, “The Romantic,” which debuted at No. 1 on Billboard's Top 200 after a five-year break since his last project.

The 40-year-old pop artist has a groundbreaking musical career spanning over a decade and a half, with each album gaining considerable notoriety at its release. His latest album takes a mature and mellowed out turn different from his traditional pop and hip-hop heavy discography.

“The Romantic” is a purely feel-good album. Featuring nine tracks with a run time of just over a half hour, every song on this album is made to make your heart skip a beat and reminisce about the cherished memories of lovers and passionate romance.

There are songs that will make you dance, songs that will make you swoon and songs that will make you nostalgic. As the album suggests, each song has romantic connotations and fills every second with intimate bliss — even the break-up ballads.

The album as a whole has an incredibly experimental feel when taking his previous albums into account. There are Latin inspirations that give the project a sincerely soulful and somewhat sedated feel, while still inducing perfect levels of punch behind every verse. Combining these characteristics, Mars leaves us with a modern take on popular 80’s R&B that give hints of prime Marvin Gaye, Prince and Michael Jackson, both musically and lyrically.

Mars, a veteran of crafting an ambitious and win-at-all-costs love song, opens up the album with the pleading “Risk It All.” On this track, Mars unloads his romantic prowess and commitment to doing whatever is asked of him to hold the hand he seeks. “I would swim across the sea just to show you / Sacrifice my life just to hold you / I could go on and on to prove that you belong / Here in my arms.”

On the proceeding tracks, “Cha-Cha-Cha,” “I Just Might,” and “God Was Showing Off,” Mars leads you through a series of love-confessionals that feel like a collection of everything he’s wanted to write since the start of his career.

Lyrically, these songs hold the same weight and charm as previous hits like “Grenade” and “Locked Out Of Heaven,” but presenting them in a more professional manner through the pristine production of his band.

What Mars did with this album is structure it as if it was the soundtrack to an actual relationship. The first half of the album opens with so much sheer joy and passion for love in its simplest form — as if those beginning tracks were just the essence of what true love really feels like.

Those feelings are hard to put into words but Mars finds a way to do it and back them up with a fantastic ensemble. The back half of the album speaks to the end of a relationship, with fights, tension and resentment all under the shade of a desperate call for resolution.

Songs like “Why You Wanna Fight” and “Something Serious” are songs that cry out to the fleeting feeling of love and all of its much welcomed intricacies and perks. They’re songs that plead for immortal adoration while reconciling with the notion that it all might, in fact, be gone despite the effort.

There's a much stronger presence of the band and a sense of communal effort on this album, compared to his previous albums that really kept Mars in the focus through his radio-friendly style of production. With “The Romantic,” Mars creates a project with a lot more camaraderie and soul that permeates through every song.

The whole album is relatively low-key, oozing “late-night-smooching-by-the-fireplace-vibes.” Mars’ use of bongos, trumpets, jazzy drums and a harmonious set of backup singers, gives every song the feel of a dramatic motion picture, moving from one element to the next and then blending them all together with a seamless and groovy end product that feels like you were just whisked away by your loved one.

MORE LIFESTYLE CONTENT

title: Breaking down the online phenomenon for Penn State wrestling known as ‘Cael tax’
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
It’s tax season.

For two Penn State wrestlers, their “taxes” have already been paid this season, but it wasn’t the typical taxes. PJ Duke and Marcus Blaze have both paid their dues for the “Cael tax” leading up to the NCAA Championships.

The term “Cael tax” has become popular on the internet, alluding to the idea that each of Cael Sanderson’s wrestlers have to metaphorically pay a tax — a loss.

This comes because of Sanderson’s historic collegiate wrestling career, where he didn’t lose a single match in his four years as a starter at Iowa State. Now, Sanderson leads one of the most historic dynasties in all of sports, which is entering uncharted territory, but one thing that hasn’t come yet is an undefeated, four-time champion.

Each of the wrestlers under Sanderson at Penn State have lost at least one match, with a few only having that single number in the loss column. But, it’s continued to remain true through this season with each of the wrestlers having to “pay their tax.”

Blaze and Duke were some of the highest regarded high school prospects in recent years, being ranked the No. 1 and No. 2 wrestlers, respectively in the 2025 class, per FloWrestling, and were poised to escape the Cael tax in their first season.

However, Duke fell to Nebraska’s Antrell Taylor during the dual season, before quickly getting his redemption in the Big Ten finals, dominating Taylor to take the 157-pound belt.

Blaze avoided his first threat of the tax, when he escaped with a tiebreaker victory over Ohio State’s Ben Davino. However, Davino handed Blaze his tax in the Big Ten finals at 133 pounds, when he defeated the Nittany Lion in sudden victory. There’s a chance at immediate redemption in the NCAA Championships if both Blaze and Davino make it to the semifinals.

Sanderson has coached two four-time champions in Carter Starocci and Aaron Brooks, with Starocci winning an illustrious fifth title. But neither wrestler went undefeated. Starocci finished with a 104-4 record, while Brooks ended his career with an 89-3 record, both some of the smallest number of loss totals in a blue and white singlet.

All of the starters have paid the Cael tax, with the most “expensive” being Mitchell Mesenbrink’s loss. Mesenbrink fell in the finals of the NCAA Championships against Iowa State’s David Carr in 2024. The only loss of Mesenbrink’s redshirt freshman season is still the only loss of his career to this date.

Levi Haines has been a unique wrestler under Sanderson. While Haines has not redshirted at all, and has wrestled four straight years in the starting lineup, he still couldn’t avoid paying his tax, netting an early loss in his career.

In his second match at Penn State, Haines fell to Northern Colorado’s Vincent Zerban, 8-6, in the Bearcat Open at Binghamton. The Arendtsville, Pennsylvania, native has gone 94-4 thus far and has a chance to get to 99 wins in his career if he wins his second title this weekend.

No matter the talent when entering the Penn State wrestling room. No matter the amount of development each wrestler garners. No matter the historic accolades earned, no wrestler has evaded the infamous tax courtesy of Sanderson.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State women's hockey discusses Frozen Four, team culture
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
No. 3 Penn State women’s hockey’s 2025-26 season continues to make history. After hosting an outdoor game at Beaver Stadium, winning a fourth-consecutive AHA Championship and hosting its first-ever NCAA Regional, the Nittany Lions defeated No. 6 UConn, 3-0, to advance to a program-first Frozen Four appearance.

The blue and white — the hosts of the 2026 Women’s Frozen Four — are set to face No. 2 Wisconsin at Pegula Ice Arena on Friday at 7:30 p.m.

Ahead of the biggest game in Penn State women’s hockey history thus far, coach Jeff Kampersal, forward Maddy Christian and defenseman Kendall Butze took to the podium to discuss the implications of the upcoming weekend.

Frozen Four

Penn State is the lone program to make its debut in this year’s Frozen Four, with Wisconsin, Ohio State and Northeastern all having played in multiple in the past. The Badgers and the Buckeyes have gone all the way to the national championship game together for the past three years, with Wisconsin winning in 2023 and 2025 while Ohio State won in 2024.

This is the second time Pegula Ice Arena has hosted the Frozen Four, having previously hosted it in 2022.

“(Hosting has) been in the back of our heads since freshman year, to be completely honest,” Butze said. “We've known we were going to host the 2026 Frozen Four for quite some time now. Trying to get the program to a position where we can actually make that a realistic goal for ourselves was something that we've been talking about for a few years now."

With a graduating class of six seniors and two graduate students, the Nittany Lions’ postseason success began with the freshman season for the Class of 2026. The senior class propelled Penn State into the limelight with conference success and four consecutive national tournament appearances.

Now — with Penn State’s core being mature — Kampersal’s squad found national tournament success as well, winning its first national tournament game in program history.

“Coming in, freshman year, freshman summer, and we all were just best friends,” Christian said. “Instantly. We all clicked. We all connected. Everybody is a different human at different times, but we also are different hockey players and I think that's what makes us so good, because we appreciate one another so much. We've gone through this together.”

Team culture

When asked about the biggest achievements this season, Christian noted that the “culture” actively aided the on-ice success.

“The women in our locker room are really loud,” Kampersal said. “They get along with each other. And it's, it's the loudest team that I've ever coached, and that's been a long time. They’re helpful, academically, socially, whatever it is on the ice, they're there for one another. That connectivity matters when we hit the ice, too.”

Team chemistry is important. Whether it be on-ice passing or off-ice relationships, that connection is what breeds teamwork. Butze added her experience with the team’s on and off-ice relationship.

“I know that we put a lot of work in,” Butze said. We meet weekly as a team. We really enjoy each other's company. We hang out off the ice so much, which isn't different from previous years, but it's just something that's built the connection between upperclassmen, freshmen and sophomores, which I think is really important."

Community support

It’s no secret that Penn State doesn’t draw the same crowds that the other programs get. The program record for attendance was set at Beaver Stadium with 1,838 spectators. However, with the Frozen Four coming to Hockey Valley, the Pegula Ice Arena is primed to host an electric crowd for the top four teams in the country.

“We have a really dynamite team, and still struggle to fill Pegula,” Kampersal said. “People go to a women's volleyball game — deservedly so; they're amazing athletes — and they fill up 5,000 fans and then maybe (the men’s volleyball team) doesn’t get as much. So I'm not sure why there's a differential, but hopefully on Friday, Hockey Valley will show up and support because they deserve it."

The current home indoor attendance record was set on Oct. 6, 2018, with a crowd of 1,804, when Penn State faced Merrimack. Recently, the Nittany Lions drew a crowd of 1,615 for the regional final game against UConn.

“It's really special to be able to host the AHA Tournament, NCAA Regionals and the Frozen Four,” Butze said. “We love playing in front of the Roar Zone, in front of Hockey Valley. Having that support and just knowing we're gonna go out and we're gonna do everything we can for our fans and for each other has just made it a lot easier.”

MORE HOCKEY COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Penn State women’s lacrosse opens Big Ten play, earns 1st loss
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
All good things must come to an end eventually, and that was Penn State’s undefeated season on Sunday.

After earning their 600th win in school history on Thursday, the Nittany Lions experienced their first loss of the year against Princeton on Sunday.

Between a steady return from Erika Ho, a major milestone and the end of an undefeated season, here’s a look at what happened in Week 5 of the lacrosse season.

600 wins

The Nittany Lions opened Big Ten play on Thursday in one of the biggest ways imaginable — earning their 600th win in school history.

The feat is only held by Maryland and Loyola Maryland, as the blue and white joined rich lacrosse history with the achievement.

Nine of those wins came from the historic first season with coach Kayla Treanor leading Penn State, as the team went 8-0 to start the season, coming into Thursday’s game. That record continued as the Nittany Lions notched their best start to the season since 1989, when they won 12 straight games.

The blue and white gave USC no chance at success or a comeback, as it opened the first quarter 5-2 and stacked on another 7-2 quarter the following period.

Payton Wainman and Kelly MacKinney led the way for the blue and white, each scoring four goals. MacKinney and Delaney Radin each had eight points on the day, as MacKinney added to her goals with four assists, and Radin scored three goals with five assists.

Undefeated no more

Just three days after celebrating their school’s milestone, the Nittany Lions earned their first loss of the season as they fell to Princeton 14-11.

Things weren’t pretty for Penn State in the first two quarters, as it gave up a 5-2 first quarter and a 6-2 second quarter to go into the half down by seven goals.

However, seven players contributed to the goal scoring for the Nittany Lions, including Ho, Wainman, MacKinney and Rocquette Allen’s two goals each, as the blue and white made it a one-goal game before ultimately falling by three.

Junior goalkeeper Sydney Manning had arguably the biggest game amongst the Nittany Lions, as she recorded a 46.2% save percentage. Despite allowing 14 goals, Manning picked up 12 saves and kept things close in the final quarters.

Ho’s planned return

After getting injured against Bucknell in mid-February, Ho has seen a steady return to action over the past weeks, including her biggest jumps in the past two games.

According to Treanor, Ho is on a load management plan and has been working her way back to full recovery.

Against the Trojans, Ho made the starting lineup for the first time since Bucknell. On Sunday against Princeton, she played for three quarters and is close to a full game.

Despite the limited role, the Frankfort, Illinois, native has contributed plenty offensively, scoring eight goals since her injury.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Weekly Recap | Large deficits, staggering offense highlight Penn State softball’s West Coast trip
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
Yet to have a game at Happy Valley this season, Penn State traveled cross-country for its first-ever games in Oregon, with a sole matchup against Oregon State before facing then-No. 21 Oregon for a three-game series.

The Nittany Lions won a close 3-1 contest over the Beavers, but were then swept in their first Big Ten series of the campaign. Following the road trip, the blue and white now possess a 19-9 record entering its first home series against Wisconsin this weekend.

Here’s what went right and wrong for Penn State in its West Coast trip.

Staggering offense

During the Nittany Lions nine-game winning streak in February, their bats were on fire and ranked in the upper half of the conference in many categories. However, the blue and white’s offense couldn’t produce much over these four games.

Penn State fell behind in each contest with its bats starting slow. In the win over Oregon State, the Nittany Lions were shut out through the first four innings and tied the game at one in the fifth. While the squad rallied and plated two game-winning runs in the sixth, the offense needs to score runs earlier against tougher opponents.

The Ducks pounced on Penn State’s pitching early, and its bats couldn’t respond. On Friday’s 8-2 loss, the Nittany Lions were hitless through the first three frames and totaled just two hits. Down 5-0, junior Michela Barbanente broke the cold streak with a solo home run. Sophomore Natalie Lieto was the only other batter to record a hit as she also hit a solo dinger in the next inning.

It was the same story in the blue and white’s 10-1 loss in game two of the series. Oregon's offense started hot and plated runs early while Penn State tallied just three hits. Junior Brooke Klosowicz accounted for two of them. The Johnsburg, Illinois, native homered in the second and singled later in her next plate appearance.

The series finale brought a slightly different result. In the 6-5 loss, the Nittany Lions were shut out through the first four innings, but came back from a three-run deficit and took the lead in the fifth off a grand slam by Klosowicz. Penn State also tied the game in the seventh before Oregon walked it off in the bottom half.

However, the blue and white made it difficult to stay in games against the Ducks’ explosive offense, with its bats not producing runs until the later innings. Now facing hard competition in the Big Ten, the Nittany Lions must get back to plating runs early and often.

Pitching struggles

Elite pitching was one of the main reasons Penn State was once ranked in the top 25 this season. Even after three rough pitching performances against Oregon, the blue and white still hold a low 2.96 ERA, which is fifth-best in the Big Ten.

However, the pitching staff struggled with containing the Ducks' stacked lineup. Junior Bridget Nemeth started on the mound in Friday’s contest and allowed eight hits and five earned runs over just three innings. The bullpen wasn’t much better as junior McKenna Young surrendered five hits and three earned runs in three frames.

Freshman Brevyn Kellepouris has delivered multiple solid outings so far this season, but struggled in Saturday’s loss. The Olathe, Kansas, native threw four wild pitches while surrendering eight hits and eight earned runs over three innings. However, the left-hander allowed just one hit over three innings in the win over Oregon State and threw a hitless 0.2 innings against the Ducks on Sunday.

Sophomore Abigail Britton threw two good games against the Beavers and the Ducks. The LaVale, Maryland, native allowed just three hits and one earned run over three frames against Oregon State. In the series finale in Eugene, Britton surrendered just three hits and two earned runs while she also tallied four strikeouts.

Overall, there were some bright spots in the pitching performance last week, but the pitchers must limit runs against other talented lineups in future games.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's volleyball's Ryan Merk collects seventh EIVA Defensive Player of the Week honor

description:

After taking on a three-game road series, two of which were played last week, the libero tallied some impressive numbers.

The redshirt senior had a total of 18 digs across the two matches, with eight of those coming in four sets against No. 6 Pepperdine on Wednesday. The remaining 10 came in three sets against the No. 5 USC squad on Saturday.

title: Penn State notebook | Cael Sanderson talks NCAA Championships, youth movement in weight classes
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has a big week coming up. The squad has the NCAA Championships in Cleveland at Rocket Arena this weekend and the program has seven No. 1 seeds in the tournament.

Ahead of the postseason wrestling finale, Cael Sanderson spoke to the media on Monday following the Big Ten Championships.

Here’s what he had to say.

NCAA Championships

Penn State enters the NCAA Championships with high expectations. The team enters the tournament with seven favorites and all wrestlers inside the top-15 rankings. Penn State had only two national champions in Mitchell Mesenbrink and Carter Starocci a year ago, but the Nittany Lions are expected to have more than that this time around.

“I don’t really think about it too much. I know what each individual’s goals are and we are trying to help them be the best they can be in the big moments,” Sanderson said. “These guys work hard. They think about this tournament, probably dream about it as a kid and it’s a great opportunity for them.”

Penn State seems to peak at the right time. The Nittany Lions have won the last four national championships and the wrestlers continue to find ways to get it done in the biggest stages.

“It gives us a lot of confidence. We obviously track everything and we know what we did every practice for the last 15 years. We reflect back and learn. A lot of times you learn by accident and by mistake,” Sanderson said. “The way college wrestlers train has kind of changed over the last 15 years. I think for us, peaking, is about enthusiasm.”

Youth at top of weight classes

The top of certain weight classes are led by freshmen and sophomores. At 133 pounds, the top-5 wrestlers are all freshmen. At 157, five of the top-seven wrestlers are freshmen and sophomores. At 184, the top four wrestlers are freshmen and sophomore.

Sanderson spoke on the trend of highly touted wrestlers figuring it out early in their collegiate wrestling careers.

“Kids are ready to go now. They’re wrestling and training in (regional training centers). There’s so many opportunities — coaching, continuing to improve technique. Opportunities are continuing to increase and I think you’ll continue to see freshmen (succeed)," Sanderson said.

Penn State has one of the top ranked wrestlers at 133 in Marcus Blaze, who’s the No. 3 seed, PJ Duke at 157, who’s going into the NCAA Championships as the No. 1 seed, and Rocco Welsh, who’s at the top of 184 pounds. Those weight classes are full of other young talent throughout each class, including Jax Forrest of Oklahoma State, Kaleb Larkin of Arizona State and Max McEnelly of Minnesota.

“We’re excited for our guys and this opportunity they have in front of them, and it's fun to kind of see what they do with those opportunities,” Sanderson said.

Marcus Blaze

Marcus Blaze didn’t get the result he looked for in the Big Ten Championships. The freshman took his first loss of the season to Ohio State’s Ben Davino. Blaze is listed as the No. 3 seed for the NCAA Championships and could bounce back from his only loss of the season.

“I think Marcus will only be that much hungrier and more willing to go take care of things in regulation with all his matches because he’s a competitor. He’s as competitive as anyone I’ve coached,” Sanderson said. “We’ve obviously coached a lot of competitive kids. I think he’s gonna get after it.”

The Perrysburg, Ohio, native went undefeated during the regular season and had been a top-three wrestler in the class for most of the season. He entered the team as the No. 1 recruit in the 2025 class according to FloWrestling, and has made his mark on the program.

“Obviously we’re extremely excited and grateful for Marcus Blaze at 133 as a freshman. Just the character he has and his drive and his competitiveness is a boost for the whole program,” Sanderson said.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s hockey stays put in USCHO, moves up in NPI
author: Chase Fisher | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State made no movement in the USCHO poll this week.

After a decisive 6-2 win over Minnesota on Wednesday and a 5-2 loss to No. 1 Michigan on Saturday, the Nittany Lions stayed put at No. 10 in the USCHO rankings.

pic.twitter.com/mgP0j3DEpZ— USCHO.com (@USCHO) March 16, 2026

However, Penn State made a slight improvement in the NPI rankings, rising to No. 10. The Nittany Lions jumped in the NPI, securing them an at-large bid in the NCAA Tournament, which begins March 26th.

The selection show, which determines the region Penn State will play in and its opponents, is scheduled for Sunday, March 22. The regional tournaments will run from March 26 to 29.

title: Rocco Welsh didn't originally commit to Penn State, but now he's found a home in the blue and white
author: Evan Smith | The Daily Collegian
description:
At the 2024 NCAA Championships, Rocco Welsh stood on the mat, wearing scarlet and gray for Ohio State, for the 174-pound national final. He had completed a tournament run that featured three consecutive sudden-victory wins, shocking the country as the No. 6 seed in his national championship debut.

Across from him stood Carter Starocci in a blue and white Penn State singlet. Cael Sanderson, sitting on the corner of the mat, was already familiar with Welsh, a Waynesburg, Pennsylvania, product.

“We recruited Rocco, but he committed pretty early in the process,” Sanderson said in February. “He’s a hammer-tough PA kid.”

Starocci went on to defeat Welsh, adding credence to Welsh’s decision to spurn Penn State in the recruiting process as he wouldn’t start right away. Starocci’s lock on the Penn State lineup, as a defending national champion, contributed to Welsh’s decision to quickly commit to Ohio State, knowing he was bound to sit for a year.

Despite being a Pennsylvania native and receiving calls from Sanderson, Welsh felt the draw to compete right away. His decision was fruitful, as it led to a 26-4 record as a freshman and a chance to shock the world against Starocci.

“I was young and didn’t really know what I was doing,” Welsh said in February. “I made a quick decision thinking about who was here and if I had to fight for a spot.”

Welsh ended up facing a problem getting back into the lineup his sophomore year. With upperclassman Carson Kharchla being pitted to wrestle Welsh off the spot at 174 pounds, Ohio State coach Tom Ryan gave Welsh the option of competing at 174 pounds, jumping to 184 pounds or utilizing his redshirt.

Not comfortable making the jump, Welsh elected to redshirt, leading to a ripple of rumors he would transfer out of Ohio State midseason. He ended up staying in Columbus throughout the year.

“(Welsh) doesn’t need to stay here,” Ryan told FloWrestling in 2024. “He can go wherever he wants and people would pay a lot of money for him. That’s the day and age we’re in.”

Welsh eventually chose to leave this past offseason, transferring home to Pennsylvania to join the Nittany Lions. Ryan later insinuated the move came after underground conversations and a financial offer by Penn State. The move has been beneficial for all parties, as Ohio State welcomed Ohio native Dylan Fishback to the team to replace Welsh, while Welsh rose to No. 1 in the country for Penn State.

“If I could go back, I definitely would’ve committed here from the beginning,” Welsh said. “I think God took me in that direction and it’s all part of his plan.”

The redshirt sophomore credits his surge to the coaches and teammates in the room, citing Levi Haines and Josh Barr as supporting his pace on the mat and his top-bottom game in training. Welsh also acknowledged assistant coach Casey Cunningham for having an impact on his improvement this season.

“(Cunningham) never tries to really change anything like some coaches, he just sees stuff that I need to add,” Welsh said. “He’s been so helpful in helping me progress into the next level.”

The first time Welsh saw his former team was the Bryce Jordan Center dual on Feb. 13, where he faced his replacement in Fishback. The match was a full-circle moment in front of the largest crowd Welsh has ever competed in front of. Welsh treated it like any other dual, not overthinking his opponent or his singlet, but he came out of the gate slow in the moment.

Fishback controlled the first two periods of the match, leading Welsh and acquiring riding time. Welsh needed two quick takedowns to avoid being upset by his former team. As the tension rose in the arena, Welsh turned it on, completing the comeback with 30 seconds to spare, sparking the loudest ovation from an already ruckus crowd.

“I think the match altogether was a positive, even if the first period wasn’t,” Welsh said. “I overcame adversity and got a victory in a crazy environment against a tough opponent.”

Since his revenge match against the Buckeyes, Welsh has continued to stay dominant, including running through the Big Ten Championships. He defeated Minnesota’s Max McEnelly, last year's national runner-up, to do so, but wasn’t thrilled with the way the match played out. Welsh had to act defensively to secure his conference title, a departure from his typical style.

“I’m not happy with the way I’m kind of holding back,” Welsh told reporters after the Big Ten Championships. “I won a Big Ten title, but If I want to win a national title, I’ll have to do more.”

Now with the Big Ten season on the back burner, Welsh’s eyes are set on returning to the NCAA Championships, and flipping the script on his title-match loss from 2024. The experience and training staff behind Welsh have him stationed as one of the favorites at 184 pounds entering a highly competitive field in Cleveland.

But for Welsh, he’s already seen it before, and is ready to take home his first national title.

“There’s a lot of confidence having went through and made the finals before,” Welsh said. “Hopefully I can change the result in the final this time.”

title: Recapping Penn State wrestling connections in final high school state tournaments of the year
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State is likely on track to win its fifth straight national title and 13th in the last 16 years. But, the future consistently looks bright as each team continues to raise the bar from the next.

Future Nittany Lions competed in their respective state tournaments over the past two weeks in Indiana, Ohio, Pennsylvania, New Jersey and New York.

Here’s how each Penn State connected wrestler did.

110 pounds: Will Soto - Third place

2027 commit Will Soto competed in the Division I New York State Championship at 110 pounds for Newburgh Free Academy. Soto earned the No. 3 seed in the weight class and a first round bye.

He rolled through the first two rounds of competition, winning by tech fall in a little over 3:30 in each of the two matches. He scored a combined 32 points, allowing no points against, showcasing his ability to score nearfall points at a light weight.

Soto ran into the future champion, sophomore Xavier Seabury of North Babylon. The future Nittany Lion dropped the match to Seabury, 4-1, in sudden victory on a last second takedown, but battled back for two straight tech falls to finish third.

121 pounds: Landon Sidun - First place

After losing in the regional finals, Landon Sidun had a chance at redemption in the Pennsylvania State Tournament for Norwin.

Sidun was listed as the No. 6 seed and proved quickly he was underranked. Two straight bonus-point victories put the 2027 commit into the semifinals. Sidun earned a 7-6 victory in tiebreakers over Penn commit Dominick Morrison.

In the finals, it was a dominant 17-0 tech fall over Connellsville’s Tommy Gretz in the finals to secure his second state title.

126 pounds: Grey Burnett - First place

Grey Burnett competed for Perrysburg High School at the Ohio State Wrestling Championships over the weekend. Burnett was the top seed in his weight class and proved it with two falls in a combined time of 5:49.

Burnett rolled into the finals, and took home the title with a 4-1 decision to win his third straight state title. The 2027 commit is the No. 6 wrestler in his class, and the No. 3 wrestler in the country at 126 pounds.

133 pounds: Braiden Weaver - Fifth place

2026 signee Braiden Weaver competed in the Pennsylvania State Championship for Altoona at 133 pounds. Earning the No. 7 seed, Weaver advanced to the semifinals behind a 1-0 decision and 12-1 major decision.

Weaver dropped his semifinal bout to Nebraska signee Willmount Kai, by pinfall at the 2:36 mark. Weaver fell to Abington Heights’ Mason Whitney before securing fifth place with a decision victory.

144 pounds: Clinton Shepherd - First place

Crown Point’s Clinton Shepherd wrestled at the Indiana State Championships and earned his first title. He showcased similar dominance to that of the current Penn State team, going 4-0 with four-bonus wins.

In the finals, Shepherd won by major decision, 16-3, securing his first championship in his junior campaign.

144 pounds: Gavin Mangano - First place

Joining Soto at the New York State Championships was Shoreham Wading River’s Gavin Mangano. A 2027 commit, Mangano looked for his third state title when he took the mat.

Mangano rolled, going 4-0 at 144 pounds, with three tech falls, which included a 54-6 differential, before a decision victory in the finals over Binghamton commit Ashten Haley.

The 7-5 victory over Haley secured Mangano’s third straight state title.

152 pounds: James Whitbred - First place

State College’s James Whitbred also earned the No. 3 seed in his weight class at the PIAA State Championships. He opened his tournament with two straight bonus-point victories. However, his run to the title ended there as he dropped his semifinal bout to Lehigh commit Gabriel Ballard.

He fell in the consolations to George Mason commit, and No. 1 seed Luke Knox. Whitbred secured a 4-1 decision in the fifth-place bout.

165 pounds: Jayden James - First place

Penn State’s top incoming commit, Jayden James, held the top seed at 165 pounds for Delbarton School in New Jersey. A fall and three straight tech falls put the 2026 signee into the finals.

He secured another overpowering victory in the finals, pinning Columbia commit Ryan Gavrish in 1:23.

James’ longest match of the tournament was 2:22, in his second-round tech fall of Bergen Catholic’s Manny Gusfa. It was James’ second state title and will enter Penn State as the No. 3 recruit in the 2026 class.

172 pounds: Teag Sanderson - Third place

Not committed to Penn State, Cael Sanderson’s son, Teag Sanderson, competed at 174 pounds.

Teag was the No. 13 seed at his weight class in his freshman campaign with State College and began his run with two wins, a tech fall and decision to set up a semifinal bout with top-seed McKaden Speece, a Navy signee. Teag Sanderson dropped his match by a 14-6 major decision, but responded with an 8-4 and 11-5 decision to earn third place.

215 pounds: Elijah Brown - First place

2026 signee Elijah Brown showcased why he was the top-ranked wrestler at 215 pounds in the state tournament. Brown opened his tournament with a fall in 34 seconds, following it up with another in 44 seconds.

A 4-1 decision sent Brown to the finals, where he escaped with a 1-0 victory over Garnet Valley’s Paxton Hunt, earning his second state title.

MORE WRESTLING COVERAGE

title: How to commemorate Women’s History Month in State College
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
Since 1987, March has been formally recognized as Women’s History Month . To commemorate the achievements, activism and long-lasting impact of women, there are a number of events happening in the Penn State community.

Here are some events students, faculty and community members can attend to honor women’s history month in State College.

March 1 to 31

Women’s History Month leisure reading/listening virtual display and bookshelf

Throughout the month the Penn State University Libraries’ Libby Reading App will offer a Women’s History Month virtual display of e-books and audiobooks available for students and employee borrowing.

The books will feature themes about extraordinary women, women’s issues and strong female protagonists.

Furthermore, the bookshelf on the first floor of the Paterno Library near the Starbucks’ order pickup area will have books available about women’s stories, struggles and triumphs.

Wednesday, March 18

Laurie Essig - “Tracing the Global Anti-Gender Movement: Feminism, Fascism, and the Future

From 3:30 to 5 p.m., Laurie Essig will give a talk as part of the Women’s, Gender and Sexuality Studies Speaker Series. During her talk, Essig will explore the international rise of anti-gender and their impact on feminism, transgender rights, reproductive justice and gender studies.

Essig is a professor and chair of Gender, Sexuality and Feminist Studies at Middlebury College, she will also talk about ideas from her podcast, “Feminism, Fascism, & the Future.”

Wednesday, March 25

Women’s History Month Trivia Night

From 5 to 7 p.m. in the HUB Flex Theatre, there will be a trivia night dedicated to women’s history and celebrating the impact and achievements of historical women.

Food will be provided and prizes will be handed out. Everyone is welcome to attend.

Thursday, March 26

Women’s History Month Festival

Taking place from 5 to 8 p.m. in 325 and 326 of the Engineering Design and Innovation Building, there will be a Women’s History Month Festival. During this event the organizing groups — The Society of Hispanic Professional Engineers, Society of Asian Scientists and Engineers and WIREDIN — will also give presentations.

There will also be a panel with professors from a number of Penn State colleges, including Information Sciences and Technologies, Earth and Mineral Sciences, Health and Human Development and Engineering.

This event is free and open to all.

Friday, March 27

Women’s History Month R-VOICE Sticker Drop

Stop by the HUB tables where R-VOICE will be debuting five new sticker designs in honor of Women’s History Month. The designs will include “Geckos Against Gaslighting,” “Capybaras for Consent” and “Cats Against Catcalling.”

Quantities are limited and only available while supplies last.

Distinguished Rebels

At 11 a.m. WPSU will be honoring the women who have changed the face of classical music.

Listeners will be able to learn more about women who have contributed to classical music as composers, performers, conductors and educators.

Friday, March 27 and Saturday, March 28

Afropop’s Tribute to Cesaria Evora

On March 27 and 28 at 6 p.m., WPSU will be hosting their final episode in a four part special with Afropop Worldwide. They will be celebrating the life of Grammy Award winning singer Cesaria Evora from Cape Verde who passed away in 2011 at the age 70.

Fantcha and Mayra Andrade will pay their tribute with stories and songs inspired by one of the most influential and accomplished artists of the modern African era.

Tuesday, March 31

Her STEM Story: A Women in Science Gallery

From 3:30 to 5 p.m. the Verne M. Willaman Gateway to the Sciences will be celebrating Women’s History Month at the Women in STEM Gallery. This will be a community event featuring handmade posters designed by undergraduate students honoring women scientists and engineers from around the world.

The Osaze Osagie Memorial Lecture: Chamique Holdsclaw

From 6:30 to 8:30 p.m. in the Hintz Family Alumni Center, Olympic medalist and WNBA Hall of Famer and mental health advocate Chamique Holdsclaw will be discussing her story in a series on Global Black Communities and Mental Health.

During the lecture Holdsclaw will talk about her journey with mental health and her subsequent mental health advocacy efforts.

Holdsclaw was once known as the “female Michael Jordan” as she led the Tennessee Lady Volunteers basketball team to three consecutive NCAA championships under head coach Pat Summit.

MORE NEWS COVERAGE

title: Comedy shows, concerts, yoga | This week in State College
author: Aly Cromar | For the Collegian
description:
This week the State College community has a number of events to keep students occupied. Here are a few ways to kick off the second half of the semester.

Monday, March 16

Yoga & Meditation

Penn State Health Promotion and Wellness is offering two weekly yoga sessions at the HUB-Robeson Center (HUB) and Pasquerilla Spiritual Center.

The first class is from 12:30 to 1:30 p.m. in HUB Room 232 and the second runs from 5:30 to 6:30 p.m. in the Pasquerilla Memorial Lounge.

Due to limited supplies, attendees are encouraged to bring their own equipment. No experience or registration is required.

Mind Gym

From 6 to 7 p.m., Penn State gym-goers are invited by the Counseling and Psychological Services to a Mind Gym workshop in the HUB Flex Theater.

This monthly mindfulness presentation is for athletes of any level, with this specific session focusing on body image. Students can register through this link.

Tuesday, March 17

St. Patrick's Day Fair

The Penn State Center for Spiritual and Ethical Development is holding a St. Patrick’s Day celebration from 12:30 to 3 p.m. in 124 Pasquerilla Spiritual Center Pod 2.

Everyone is invited to play St. Patrick's Day games and discover the history of St. Patrick and Ireland, while connecting with communities that celebrate this holiday.

Students can register at this link.

Study Smarter Session

Penn State Learning is hosting another Study Smarter Zoom workshop at 6 p.m. The session aims to teach attendees how to apply six studying techniques to each of their classes.

Students can register via the Penn State Learning website.

Wednesday, March 18

Colin Jost Live

In collaboration with the Penn State Student Programming Association, SNL comedian Colin Jost will perform a stand-up comedy show in the Bryce Jordan Center at 7:30 p.m.

The doors will open at 6:30 p.m. and tickets are available on Ticketmaster for $20.

Mental Health is not a Drag Show

From 6 to 9 p.m., the Center for Sexual and Gender Diversity, Health Promotion and Wellness, Eating Recovery Center and Eric Dorsa (Fonda K.) are hosting a mental health awareness drag show in the Freeman Auditorium.

The show will feature Fonda K., Freeda Kulo, Marti Cummings and DD Fuego from RuPaul’s Drag Race. After the performance, attendees are invited to a mental health panel discussion and meet-and-greet with the drag artists.

All students and faculty can register through Discover at this link.

Thursday, March 19

Nittany Valley Symphony ‘America at 250’

From 7:30 to 9:30 p.m., the Center for the Performing Arts will present “America at 250,” a concert to celebrate 250 years of independence in the United States.

The Nittany Valley Symphony will perform a range of orchestral music in the Eisenhower Auditorium. Highlights of the program include William Grant Still's Poem, Leonard Bernstein's On the Waterfront Suite and additional pieces by John Williams, Cole Porter and Irving Berlin.

Tickets can be purchased through Ticketmaster for $9 with a student discount and adult tickets are $29.

Build Your Career Skills

As part of their weekly workshop series, Penn State Career Services invites students to drop in for a job and internship search from 11 a.m. to 12 p.m. at the Bank of America Career Services Building.

No registration is required to attend.

Friday, March 20

Softball vs. Wisconsin

After a slew of away games, the Penn State softball team will have its first home game and Big Ten opener against the Wisconsin Badgers at 5 p.m. in the Penn State Softball Arena and Beard Field.

There will be two additional home games against Wisconsin on March 21 and 22.Tickets for this game, along with all other regular-season home games, are available to the public at no cost.

Brandon Lake’s King of Hearts Tour

Grammy Award-winning contemporary Christian artist Brandon Lake will perform at the BJC stage at 7 p.m. for a big-ticket event to close out the week.

Special guests Franni Cash and Pat Barret are set to make an appearance. Doors will open at 5:30 p.m. and tickets are available on Ticketmaster for a set cost of $30.

Saturday, March 21

Men's Lacrosse vs. Maryland

At 3 p.m. at Panzer Stadium, Men’s Lacrosse will face off against the Maryland Terrapins in their white-out Big Ten season opener.

Anyone looking for a free Saturday afternoon sporting event can head over in white-out gear to support the Nittany Lions in this rivalry match.

MORE CAMPUS COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball falls to No. 21 Oregon, leaving first Big Ten series winless after late collapse
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State stepped onto the dirt in Eugene with a chance to put up any sort of fight against Oregon, who had led the blue and white 18-3 over two emphatic wins for the Ducks.

Ultimately, that wouldn’t be the case, as the Nittany Lions fell once again to Oregon, in their closest clash with the Ducks in the three-game series, closing out with a 6-5 loss in the bottom of the last frame.

Penn State coach Clarisa Crowell sent out sophomore right-handed arm Abigail Britton to start the clash, marking her first appearance against the Ducks this season, and only second of the West Coast series after a two-inning, one-run start against Oregon State.

Neither side started hot offensively, as Penn State got runners on in both the first and second frames via errors, but couldn’t capitalize. Britton allowed a single and a walk to start the home half of the second, which eventually led to the first run of the game off another base hit.

Yet, when the side flipped over to the third, the Nittany Lions started with two lead-off outs, but threatened with another base via an error and a hit by pitch, yet the Ducks were able to send down star designated player Brooke Klosowicz on strikes to end the inning.

The action remained focused through the circle and defensive play, as neither side’s offense was able to break through. Oregon held the advantage in that regard, shutting down Penn State’s bats and cruising to a leadoff runner on third courtesy of sloppy infield fielding from Britton, which marked Penn State’s third allowed.

A walk and steal only complicated things, but when Britton tossed a punchout and then a grounder to short, it looked as if the Nittany Lions could escape the jam, but shortstop Kalli Karwowski went for the runner at home, who was called safe.

The Ducks then smashed a double to score their third run of the game, but Britton struck out the next two batters to end the threat. Penn State got its first hits of the game in the fifth frame courtesy of a pair of leadoff singles by first baseman Gabby Gradishar and right fielder Cara Bohner, which forced an Oregon pitching change.

After a wild pitch advanced the runners, Penn State attempted a suicide squeeze bunt to steal its first run of the game, but the Ducks snuffed out the effort. Oregon then recorded a punchout, but second baseman Michela Barbanente drew a nine-pitch at bat with an eventual walk to load the bases at two outs, putting the game within one swing’s reach.

Klosowicz stepped up with the decisive swing to make, and waited through a 13-pitch at bat to find her pitch, taking Oregon deep to center field for a grand slam. Penn State led 4-3, the first time it had led in the series with the Ducks.

With this unique position, Crowell opted for change inside the circle, as Britton exited for fellow righty, freshman and team-ERA leader Mackenzie Duncan. The Winter Garden, Florida, native got out of the frame unharmed, with a great diving play from Karwowski at shortstop.

The Nittany Lions went down scoreless in the top half, striking out in a pair, and it became apparent they would have to win this game from the circle. That effort started shaky for Penn State, as Duncan allowed a leadoff single, which eventually moved into scoring position after a pinch runner earned a steal.

Duncan continued to struggle, as a wild pitch allowed that runner on second to tie the game, and a single scored another, flipping the score back in Oregon’s favor. Brevyn Kellepouris began to warm up in the bullpen to potentially enter, but soon after Duncan ended the jam.

Now the focus shifted to the batter’s box for the Nittany Lions, as the leadoff pinch hitter Jordan George worked a walk to start the effort strong. George was replaced on the bases, and the tying run advanced to third with one out courtesy of a wild pitch and groundout.

A hit by pitch made the situation do-or-die, with the double play to end the game now also a possibility. Klosowicz knocked a pitch to second, but Oregon opted to throw home. The toss was late, Penn State scored to tie it, and then the Ducks intentionally walked to load the bases.

With one out and the opportunity to decide things, catcher Macy Chamberlin stepped up to the plate and lined out into a double play to send the Oregon bats back up. Penn State sent in a new pitcher, McKenna Young, with the chance to send up the offense once again, but a leadoff error at shortstop and a walk quickly ended her day, as Kellepouris entered.

A bunt sent runners into scoring positions, and a deep fly ball to right field followed, ultimately allowing the Ducks to win via the tagging runner.

With the result, the blue and white fall to 19-9 on the season.

Up next

Penn State continues conference play with the blue and white’s home opener at Nittany Lion Softball Park and Beard Field against Wisconsin. The three-game series kicks at 5 p.m. on Friday.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball falls to Iowa, ends first Big Ten series
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State returned to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park against Iowa for a chance at a series win in the first Big Ten weekend of the campaign.

However, after a blowout loss on Saturday, it was déjà vu in the final matchup as the Hawkeyes came away with a 12-4 win.

Isaiah Shayter got the nod for the blue and white, and while he allowed multiple base runners, he escaped with no damage on a force out at second.

Penn State got the bats going in the bottom half of the inning, as Jayden Davis and Michael Anderson reached base on a leadoff walk and a double to left field.

A Spencer Barnett sacrifice fly and an RBI single from Jack Porter put the blue and white ahead 2-0.

Shayter maintained a strong inning, allowing just one hit while striking out two Hawkeyes, before the Nittany Lions went three up, three down in the bottom of the second.

Penn State extended its lead in the third inning as Anderson continued his hot weekend. An RBI single brought home Davis, who scored from second after his leadoff double. While the blue and white threatened for more runs, Maddux Frese shut down any other opportunity.

The top of the fourth is when Iowa got to Shayter. An RBI double from Kooper Schulte started the rally. Three batters later, Gable Mitchell sent a single through the right side that scored two runs. Both runs were marked as unearned to Shayter after a throwing error in the prior at-bat from Penn State shortstop Preston Yaucher.

Mason Horwat replaced Shayter a batter later and allowed two straight hits, including a two-RBI double from Caleb Wulf.

Horwat hit the next two batters, allowing a free runner to score, making it 6-3 Iowa, with all six runs coming with zero or one outs. Kyle Emmons took the mound in place of Horwat, marking the third pitcher for the blue and white in the fourth inning alone. Emmons allowed a runner to score on a groundout, but escaped with no further damage.

Now trailing 7-3, Penn State looked to respond in the bottom of the fourth, but three straight batters were sent back to the dugout after a leadoff walk from Jesse Jaconski.

The fifth inning was pretty silent, except that a Bryce Molinaro fielder’s choice allowed Anderson to score, pulling the deficit to just three.

Iowa erupted again in the seventh inning with four runs on four hits. Six straight batters reached base, allowing those four runs to score on two singles and a double.

Outfielder Joey Nerat added to the Hawkeyes’ lead in the top of the eighth. Nerat sent a 1-2 pitch over the left-field wall, expanding Iowa’s lead to 12-4.

Penn State couldn’t find any more success at the plate, falling to the Hawkeyes 12-4 in the Big Ten opening series.

The Nittany Lions were outhit 13-6, while the pitching staff allowed nine free batters to reach base on walks or hit by pitches.

Up next

The blue and white will travel to Morgantown, West Virginia, for a matchup with the Mountaineers at 6:30 p.m. Tuesday.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State women’s lacrosse suffers first loss of season against No. 19 Princeton
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Despite the spring weather outside, Penn State was back inside Holuba Hall for the Pink Game and the second-ranked matchup of the season.

Despite a six-goal run in the second half, the Nittany Lions’ comeback wasn’t enough as they fell to No. 19 Princeton 14-11 for their first loss of the year.

“It’s a bummer, this game,” coach Kayla Treanor said. “The way it opened up was really tough.”

After the blue and white won the faceoff, Anna Salerno and Rocquette Allen each had an attempt to score, but came up empty. The Tigers had a lot of luck out of the gate, earning a pair of goals from Haven Dora and Maggie Molnar.

Following two straight goals allowed by Sydney Manning, she found a sliver of success with her first save of the game, but was scored on just 15 seconds later by Meg Morrisroe.

The Nittany Lions finally found their mark offensively as freshman Rocquette Allen earned her 24th goal of the season to make it a two-goal game. After a foul on the Tigers, Salerno scored her first goal of the game.

While Penn State had a brief scoring run, things went back into Princeton’s favor as it earned a pair of free-position goals to take a 5-2 lead.

Coming out of the quarter, Princeton extended its lead once again with a goal from Jami MacDonald and earned its biggest lead of the day as it took a 7-2 lead early in the period.

The Tigers' momentum continued as they capitalized on their fifth free position attempt of the day, earning their third goal from a foul. Despite seven saves from Manning, Princeton’s lead continued to grow with another two goals.

“(Sydney) saved us a bunch of times; she was excellent,” Treanor said.

After seven straight goals from the Tigers, the Nittany Lions ended the run as Allen scored her second goal of the day to make it a 10-3 deficit. That success continued as Kelly MacKinney scored again for the blue and white after nearly three minutes of no action.

Penn State’s run was over soon after it started, as the Tigers scored again to make it a seven-goal game going into the half.

The second half started a little differently for the blue and white, as Erika Ho earned her first goal of the game after playing a limited role over the past month following an injury. However, the Tigers struck back less than a minute later with Morrisroe’s second of the day.

Neither side changed the scoreboard for over six minutes as a behind-the-back shot from Allen missed the net, and a combined four turnovers kept changing momentum.

“Roc is a threat on offense, but I think defensively, Susannah O’Conell played great today,” Treanor said. “She made a ton of great plays to get the ball back. Our young players are playing well, and we got to keep that going.”

However, assist leader Delaney Radin earned her first goal of the day, followed by goal leader Payton Wainman as the Nittany Lions made it a five-goal game. MacKinney capped off the quarter with her second of the day, cutting it to 12-8 with 15 minutes left.

After neither side found success in their first drives to open the final quarter, Ho capitalized on a free position shot, followed by a free position from Maddie Greco, to make it a two-goal game.

Penn State’s run continued as Wainman scored her second goal of the game, giving the blue and white five straight goals.

Following the Nittany Lions’ hot streak, the Tigers earned their first goal in over 20 minutes to make it a two-goal contest. Three minutes later, Princeton scored again to make it a three-goal game as Penn State’s comeback fell short.

“I’m really proud of our kids, the way they battled and fought back like that,” Treanor said. “This is a hard thing to do, and I think a lot of teams would crumble in that. We just stayed at it and made it a one-goal game.”

Up next

The Nittany Lions stay home as they face No. 7 Michigan for Senior Day at noon on Sunday.

MORE LACROSSE COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s volleyball falls to USC, leaves West Coast winless
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
West Coast woes continued for Penn State; three matches, three ranked foes and three whopping losses among the Golden Coast for the Nittany Lions, who stumbled to 11-7 on the year.

Penn State stepped into the Galen Center in Los Angeles against a USC team with something to prove, suffering back-to-back losses the last time out on its home floor to rival UCLA, including a nail-biting five-set series opener. Ultimately, the Trojans toppled the blue and white in three sets, marking one one one set for Penn State over its three-match series.

Penn State coach Mark Pavlik sent out a first unit of Jaxon Herr, Bennett Wilson, Ryan Merk, Gaige Gabriel, Sean Harvey, Owen Rose and Matthew Luoma. While that group has experienced immense success, they fell behind early to a fiery USC squad.

The Trojans stormed out to an early 10-5 lead before Pavlik was forced to call a timeout, and that only barely stopped the USC attack. The two squads went on a nine-consecutive exchange of kills, in which USC surfaced on top 17-8. Soon thereafter, Rose logged the only ace of the set, but that would be the only bright spot for Penn State, as it went down 25-13.

The second set resembled the first, with USC storming out to a 7-3 lead, punctuated by a pair of aces. The blue and white soon pulled within one, 9-8, due to an effervescent attack led by Harvey. That offensive effort was rather fleeting, however, as a struggle persisted to counter USC’s dominant attack. With an 18-11 deficit clear in their face, the Nittany Lions once again called a timeout.

Once again, the chance to talk things over did little to decide action, with USC earning the second set off of a pair of Harvey attack errors to close out the frame.

Harvey redeemed himself with a kill to open up the third frame, and unlike the first two sets, the Nittany Lions started on pace offensively and defensively with USC. That lasted for the majority of the set, with levelheaded action right at each other over the net, the Trojans boosted into the 20s and put the win within reach with three consecutive kills.

Jaidyn Bethel committed an error on his attack and then saw his next one blocked, which put the match one point away from closure. USC soon after logged the decisive kill to end the action.

Up Next

Penn State returns to the Commonwealth with a clash against Ohio State in the University of Pittsburgh’s Fitzgerald Field House at 6 p.m. on Wednesday, March 25.

MORE VOLLEYBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State baseball loses first Big Ten game of year to Iowa
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Returning home to Medlar Field at Lubrano Park, Penn State immediately turned around all negatives in its first Big Ten clash of the year against Iowa, with a decisive 11-6 win, its second massive at home.

Yet, in the second clash against the Hawkeyes, the blue and white took its first home loss of the season on the chin, 13-4, as the high-powered Iowa offense shut down the Nittany Lions.

For the second game of the series, coach Mike Gambino sent one of Penn State’s most in-form starters to the bump in Ben Hudson, yet the righty couldn’t make good on his attempt to continue recent positive results. Ultimately, the sophomore’s day only lasted 4.2 innings, with seven earned runs. After starting the year with only four earned runs in three starts, Hudson allowed 18 in his last two appearances.

With an early 3-0 deficit due to Hudson’s issues on the mound, the Nittany Lions finally got on the board in the bottom of the fourth courtesy of a Bryce Molinaro single to drive in a run, which marked his fourth consecutive game with an RBI.

Eventually, when it flipped over, however, the offensive barrage continued from Iowa, with four more runs tacked on via a pair of home runs. The Hawkeyes, which entered with the fewest long balls in the Big Ten, ended Hudson’s day with those swings.

Ethan Bauerschmidt, who came in to replace Hudson, couldn’t remedy the situation on the rubber. With a triad of walks and a hit-by-pitch, allowing hits from the Hawkeyes to extend their lead. The Nittany Lions scored their own run with walks and savvy baserunning, but it wasn’t much to cut into the lead, heading into the seventh.

Mason Butash, who replaced Bauerschmidt to finish out the sixth, got out to a strong start, but again continued the pitching struggles for Penn State, allowing a pair of singles, which resulted in a run, and a walk to end his day.

Iowa started to show its own pitching problems, with five walks in the bottom of the eighth to plate two Penn State runs, but the Hawkeye bats immediately got three runs back in the top half to cancel out the control issues.

The Nittany Lions’ offense couldn't get anything going against strong Iowa pitching, only logging two hits on the day to 19 by the opposition. The blue and white allowed the same amount of walks and only struck out two more times than Iowa, but it just couldn’t generate any offensive momentum and only took what Iowa gave it, which wasn’t much.

Up Next

Penn State closes its first Big Ten series of the year with a chance to claim victory at 1 p.m. on Sunday against the Hawkeyes.

MORE BASEBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State softball falls to Oregon in run-rule fashion
author: Ashley Beres | The Daily Collegian
description:
After Friday’s 8-2 loss against No. 21 Oregon, Penn State looked to bounce back and take the second game of the series on Saturday night. The Nittany Lions struggled with shutting down the Ducks’ lineup in the series opener and that trend continued early in this contest.

Penn State fell in its second straight game in Oregon’s commanding 10-1 victory as the Nittany Lions couldn’t stop the Ducks' powerful lineup.

While right-hander Maddie Milhorn retired the blue and white quietly in the top half of the first, Oregon plated three runs later in the opening frame for the second straight game. Despite right-hander Brevyn Kellepouris striking out lead-off Ayanna Shaw, the ball went to the backstop and she reached first safely.

Unlike Friday night, the Ducks’ runs came from small ball. Kaylynn Jones poked a single through the right side and Amari Harper drew a one-out walk a few batters later. The freshman struggled with her command throughout the start and hit Rylee McCoy with the bases loaded.

Stefini Ma’ake drove in the second run off a sacrifice fly to right fielder Cara Bohner and Emma Cox followed with an RBI single to left and Oregon was quickly up 3-0. Down in an early hole for the second straight game, the Nittany Lions needed to strike back quickly.

Designated player Brooke Klosowicz delivered and blasted her ninth home run of the season over the batter’s eye in center and inched Penn State closer, 3-1. However, the Ducks scored three more runs in the second as Kellepouris struggled with location.

Addison Amaral rolled a routine ground ball to shortstop Kalli Karwowski, but she committed an error and Kellepouris worked with a lead-off runner again. Shaw then knocked a single through the left side and then both runners advanced a base when Kellepouris threw a wild pitch.

Facing the heart of the Ducks’ lineup, Elon Butler smacked a double down the left field line and extended Oregon’s lead, 5-1. The bleeding didn’t stop and Harper lifted a single to center and Kellepouris faced runners on the corner and just one out.

The Olathe, Kansas, native struck out McCoy, but Butler scored off another wild pitch thrown by Kellepouris. Bar a two-out Natalie Lieto single to right center, Penn State went down quietly in the third frame and the Ducks opened the game up later in the inning.

Karwowski committed her second error at shortstop and Oregon’s lead-off hitter reached base for the third straight inning. Amaral pulled a double down the left field line and plated another run across.

After Kellepouris recorded a strikeout, the Ducks put together a two-out rally. Jones walked and then both Ducks were in scoring position off Kellepouris’ fourth wild pitch. Oregon’s lineup is filled with many power hitters and the long ball put this game out of reach.

Butler blasted a three-run home run over the right field wall and blew the game completely open, 10-1. Down nine runs, Klosowicz was the only Nittany Lion to reach base in the fourth and was left stranded in scoring position.

The Ducks secured the series win in five innings as Penn State committed three errors and allowed eight hits. With the loss, the blue and white fell to 19-8 on the season.

Up Next

Penn State wraps up its road trip in the series finale against No. 21 Oregon at 3 p.m. Sunday. The game will be streamed on Big Ten Plus.

MORE SOFTBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men’s lacrosse loses to North Carolina in ranked clash
author: AJ Piazza | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State men’s lacrosse entered today’s game on a two-game win streak to end its Ivy League schedule. The Nittany Lions faced their first ACC opponent in the regular season for the first time since 2014.

The result ended in a victory for North Carolina, 10-8 over Penn State.

Penn State started with the faceoff win, but couldn’t get much going. Anthony Raio got the scoring started for the Tar Heels, and it started a dominant trend for the squad. The Nittany Lions had opportunities, but couldn’t find the back of the net, unlike North Carolina.

Andrew Beard put the Nittany Lions on the board, but Mason Szewczyk answered with two of his own and forced Jeff Tambroni to call a timeout. The blue and white came out of the timeout, fired up as it had a few offensive opportunities and Liam Matthews cut the lead to one.

Jack Iannantuono continued to be a difference-maker, tying the game for Penn State. The game was tied at 3-3 and the second quarter was full of sloppy lacrosse from both teams.

Anthony Raio got the first goal from an assist from Gary Merrill, who extended the lead to 5-3 in favor of the Tar Heels.

It wasn’t until after that that the goal scoring got started. John Jude Considine brought the Nittany Lions within one, but North Carolina kept answering. Brevin Wilson extended the lead back to two going into the half.

The big difference for the Nittany Lions in this matchup was the faceoffs. The Nittany Lions were losing the faceoff battle 8-4 halfway through the game. Penn State was the first team to put the ball in the back of the net in the second half. Iannantuono was the top attacker for Penn State in this game.

The freshman cut the lead to one, and it was followed up by a faceoff win by Reid Gills, and a goal by Chase Robertson to tie the game at 6-6. The Nittany Lions had built momentum in the third quarter.
